The agent escalated over how heated the exchange had gotten and the reputational threat the customer was making.
options.explanation: Wrong. Every input needed to resolve this is present: the refund is verified and within the agent's authority, so the agent can grant it now. The conversation is difficult and the threat raises the stakes, but difficulty and tone are not the same as being unable to resolve the case, and the demand for a senior callback does not change what the agent is empowered to do.

questions.explanation: C is correct: the outcome is already determined (the refund is owed), but the agent cannot carry it out—the only disbursement method policy allows is gone, no tool offers an alternative, and the agent has no authority to approve an exception—so a human is genuinely required to act. A is wrong because the policy's catch-all default routes unlisted products like subscriptions to the standard 30-day rule, which the agent may apply; the lack of a subscription-specific line is not a gap. B is wrong because the unconfirmed detail (whether the replacement shipped) is retrievable through the agent's order tools, so the fix is a lookup the agent skipped, not a handoff. D is wrong because the refund is verified and within the agent's limit, so it can resolve the case now; a hostile tone and a threat of public reviews raise the stakes but do not make the case unresolvable.

questions.topic: Batch API vs. iterative tool loops
questions.text: Your nightly CI stage has Claude write integration tests for each changed module. Telemetry shows that before emitting a test file Claude calls `list_fixtures` and `get_schema`, and the number of these lookups varies per module — averaging 3.4 round-trips — because later lookups depend on what earlier ones return. The stage isn't latency-sensitive, but Finance flags the per-PR cost, so an engineer wants to move it onto the Message Batches API for the 50% discount. How should the stage be restructured to capture that discount?

options.label: A
options.text: Resolve `list_fixtures` and `get_schema` for every changed module up front, inject those results into each prompt, and submit the now self-contained generation calls as one cold batch.
options.explanation: This is the cleanest single-window migration — one batch, full discount, no live tool calls — which is why it is tempting. But the lookups branch: the second and third calls depend on what the first returned, so a snapshot frozen at submit time can't reproduce the per-module follow-up sequence, and tests get written against a context the model would never actually have assembled.

options.label: B
options.text: Route the frequent `list_fixtures` and `get_schema` lookups (3.4 per module) through the Batches API to capture the discount on the high-volume calls, and run each test-generation step synchronously.
options.explanation: It targets the highest-volume calls, where a per-request discount intuitively pays off most. But those lookups are the cheap part of the spend and they're sequentially dependent, so they can't be issued as one batch — meanwhile the one expensive call, the full-context test-generation completion, is left at full price, which is the spend Finance actually flagged.

options.label: C
options.text: Submit each module's full multi-turn exchange as a single batch job so the 50% discount covers every round of that module in one submission.
options.explanation: It reads as the most direct lift-and-shift of the existing exchange. But a batch request runs to completion and stops at the first `tool_use` block with no caller hook to run the tool and resume, so rounds two onward never execute and no test file is produced.

options.label: D
options.text: Keep the fixture and schema gathering as a live synchronous loop, then collect the final test-generation call for each module and submit them together as one batch.
options.explanation: The branching lookups stay live and synchronous so they reflect what each module actually needs, and the expensive completions — which carry the spend and have no remaining tool dependency — are grouped into a single batch window, capturing the discount on exactly the calls worth discounting.

questions.explanation: D is correct: the lookups must stay live because they branch on intermediate results, so they can't be frozen or batched, while the costly test-generation completions have no further tool dependency and can be grouped into one batch window — putting the discount on the calls that carry the spend. A is wrong because injecting an up-front snapshot freezes lookups that actually depend on each other's output, so the model generates against a context it would never have assembled per module. B is wrong because it discounts the cheap, high-volume lookups (which are sequentially dependent and can't batch anyway) and leaves the single expensive completion at full price. C is wrong because a batch request halts at the first `tool_use` block with no hook to run the tool and continue, so the later rounds never fire and no test file is produced.

questions.text: Your monorepo organizes code by feature, so each feature folder holds its own `migrations/` directory with `*.sql` files beside that feature's UI, services, and tests. Across ~40 feature folders every migration must follow identical conventions (transactional wrappers, reversible `-- down` blocks, snake_case names), though the surrounding code differs wildly. In last week's on-call review, several engineers found Claude had written migrations in each feature's local style and broken the shared standard, and feature folders are added and retired most weeks. You want those conventions in Claude's context whenever it edits any `migrations/*.sql` file, without re-touching the setup every time the folder layout shifts. Which approach fits best?

options.label: A
options.text: Commit a single file to `.claude/rules/` with `paths: "**/migrations/*.sql"` in its frontmatter, holding the migration conventions in its body
options.explanation: A `.claude/rules/` file lives in version control, so it ships to everyone who clones the repo, and the `paths` glob loads its body specifically when a matching `migrations/*.sql` file is in play rather than on every turn. One committed file covers all current and future feature folders without edits as the layout changes.

options.label: B
options.text: Put the same `paths`-scoped rule file in your `~/.claude/rules/` instead, since you write most of the migrations and maintain several repos with the same conventions
options.explanation: A `paths`-scoped rule in `~/.claude/rules/` does match the glob and self-updates as folders change, and centralizing it in your home directory is convenient if you personally touch several repos with one standard. The catch is that `~/.claude` is personal and uncommitted, so it never reaches teammates' sessions — and the broken migrations in the review came from work across the team, not only yours.

options.label: C
options.text: Add a PreToolUse hook on Edit/Write that checks for a `migrations/` target path and injects the convention text before the edit runs
options.explanation: After Claude broke the standard, a deterministic hook that fires on every Edit/Write is a natural instinct, and it can key off the path and inject the text. But you then own and version a script plus its payload to reproduce what a `paths`-scoped rule expresses declaratively in one committed file, and the hook runs on every edit rather than loading only when a migration file is in scope — more to build and maintain for the same delivery.

options.label: D
options.text: Commit one rule file to `.claude/rules/` with no `paths` frontmatter, so its conventions are available across the whole repo
options.explanation: A single committed rule with no `paths` field is one file that reaches the team, which looks like the leanest option. But an unscoped rule loads into every session unconditionally, so the migration conventions sit in context for all UI, service, and test work too — extra baggage in a large monorepo where the `paths` field exists precisely to load them only when a migration file is in scope.

questions.explanation: A and B are near-identical `paths`-scoped rule files; the deciding fact is distribution. A lives in `.claude/rules/` under version control, so it reaches every engineer who broke the standard, and its `paths: "**/migrations/*.sql"` glob loads the conventions only when a matching file is edited and self-updates as folders come and go. B is the same mechanism in `~/.claude/rules/`, which is personal and uncommitted, so it would cover only the author's machine while the review showed the drift was team-wide. C (PreToolUse hook) is the deterministic instinct after a broken standard, but it makes you maintain a script and payload to do declaratively what a rule already does, and it fires on every Edit/Write instead of loading only when a migration file is in scope. D (a committed rule with no `paths` field) does reach the team but loads unconditionally into every session, carrying the migration conventions through all non-migration work — the `paths` field exists to avoid exactly that.

questions.text: Halfway through a security sweep, you watch the main session balloon: you asked Claude to trace every place the codebase reads an untrusted `request.headers` value across 90 services, and it has spent several minutes streaming back match after match with surrounding code blocks, eating most of the window before the trace is even complete. The matches are not uniform — different sinks call for different mitigations. Once the full picture is in, a security teammate joins the session to talk through, sink by sink, which mitigation to standardize on; only after that agreement does Claude patch each site. For the patches to come out consistent across all 90 sites, that live design discussion and the patching it drives have to run with the full set of sites in view at once.\n\nWhat is the most effective way to finish the sweep while keeping the patches consistent across every site?

options.label: A
options.text: Hand the header-tracing pass to the Explore subagent, but have it append each match to a running scratch file rather than summarize, then load that file back into the main session for the design talk and the patching.
options.explanation: Loading the full match log back into the main session re-fills the window with the same verbose detail Explore was supposed to keep out of it, so the design talk starts from a context already as crowded as the one you were escaping. The scratch file solves persistence, not the context pressure the scenario is about.

options.label: B
options.text: Hand the header-tracing pass to the Explore subagent so its bulky match output stays in a separate context, then bring its compact summary into the main session and run both the design talk and the patching from that single retained view of all the sites.
options.explanation: Explore absorbs the verbose match stream in its own context and returns a summary small enough that the main window stays open. With every site held in that one retained context, the teammate's sink-by-sink decisions and the patches that follow are made against the whole set at once, which is what holds them consistent across all 90 services.

options.label: C
options.text: Offload the trace to Explore and hold the design talk in the main session, then once the mitigation shape is agreed, fan the patching out to parallel subagents split by service group, each handed the agreed shape and its slice of the sites.
options.explanation: Parallel patching scales, and each subagent can apply the agreed shape competently within its own group. But every subagent works in an isolated context that sees only its slice, and consistency across the 90 sites is a property that can only be enforced from a context that sees all of them together, so groups drift from one another even when each is internally faithful to the shape.

options.label: D
options.text: Offload the trace to Explore and have it return not just the matches but its recommended standard mitigation for each sink type, so the teammate walks into a session that already proposes the shape to apply.
options.explanation: Letting Explore pre-write the mitigations turns the teammate's session into a review of decisions already made, when the scenario hands the sink-by-sink judgment to the two people in that talk. A summary that carries the sites and their differences is what the design conversation needs; the chosen mitigations are the talk's output, not its input.

questions.explanation: B is the single best answer: Explore isolates the verbose trace in its own context and returns a summary compact enough that the main window stays open, and because every site is held in that one retained context, the teammate's sink-by-sink decisions and the patches they drive are made against the full set at once, which is what keeps them consistent. A also offloads to Explore but loads the full match log back in, re-filling the very window the offload cleared. C parallelizes the patching sensibly, but each subagent sees only its slice in an isolated context, and cross-site consistency can only be enforced from a context that sees all 90 sites together, so the groups drift. D offloads correctly but has Explore pre-decide the mitigations, taking over the sink-by-sink judgment the scenario reserves for the two people in the design talk.

questions.text: Your CI pipeline runs a Claude Code review on every pull request, passing only the PR's unified diff as the input. On a PR that refactors the payment-retry module, Claude flags the helpers `backoff_jitter` and `retry_budget` as unused dead code to delete, but the team lead notes both are imported and called by the scheduler module, which is unchanged and so absent from the diff. The repo holds ~4,000 files and the team merges dozens of small PRs a day. What change would most effectively reduce these false dead-code flags?

options.label: A
options.text: Pass the entire repository alongside the diff on each review so Claude always has every caller available and cannot miss a usage of the changed symbols
options.explanation: A genuinely supplies the missing call sites, which is why it is tempting, but it does so at the wrong scale: feeding a 4,000-file repo into every one of dozens of daily reviews drives token cost and latency up sharply and dilutes the model's attention away from the diff under review. C reaches the same call sites with a fraction of the input.

options.label: B
options.text: Add an instruction telling Claude to treat any symbol as potentially used elsewhere and only recommend deletion when it can confirm the symbol has no remaining callers
options.explanation: This asks for a confirmation the model cannot make from a diff alone. With the scheduler's calls outside its input, Claude has no callers to check against, so the instruction either suppresses every deletion suggestion or is quietly ignored on the cases that matter.

options.label: C
options.text: Include the files that import the changed module in the input, not just the diff itself
options.explanation: The false flags happen because the scheduler's call sites live outside the diff Claude receives. Since the helpers are imported directly, the importing files are exactly the call sites, so adding them gives Claude the references it needs to separate genuinely dead code from code used elsewhere, at a fraction of the cost of shipping the whole repo.

options.label: D
options.text: Add a post-processing step that drops any deletion suggestion for a symbol whose name also appears as an exported identifier in the changed module
options.explanation: Being exported says nothing about whether a symbol is actually called, so this filter keeps false flags for exported-but-genuinely-dead helpers while suppressing valid flags for unexported dead code. It patches the output with a signal uncorrelated to real usage and never closes the missing-context gap.

questions.explanation: The key is C. The false flags arise because the review sends only the diff, so Claude never sees the scheduler that imports and calls the helpers; supplying the importing files puts those exact call sites in context, letting it tell truly dead code from code used elsewhere without the cost of the whole repo. A also supplies the call sites but at the wrong scale—shipping a 4,000-file repo into dozens of daily reviews is cost-, latency-, and attention-prohibitive, and C is the scoped version of the same idea. B requests a no-remaining-callers confirmation the model cannot make when the callers are absent from its input. D filters output on an exported-name signal that is uncorrelated with actual usage, so it both retains false flags and suppresses valid ones while leaving the missing-context cause untouched.

questions.text: Production traces for your support agent show a recurring defect: in roughly 1 in 9 sessions the agent emits a final customer-facing reply in the same response that requests a `process_refund` call, so the refund tool never runs and the customer is told their money is on the way when nothing happened. The loop runner decides, after each Messages API response, whether to execute the requested tools and call Claude again or to deliver the reply to the customer. What should the runner inspect on each response to make that decision?

options.label: A
options.text: The `stop_reason` field, treating `"tool_use"` as Claude signalling it is ready to hand the conversation back, so the runner stops there, and treating `"end_turn"` as a request to continue working.
options.explanation: Wrong. This reads the right field with the mapping inverted. `"tool_use"` is precisely when a tool call is pending and the loop must continue; `"end_turn"` is when the response is complete. Stopping on `"tool_use"` reproduces the traced defect, delivering the reply while `process_refund` is still unexecuted.

options.label: B
options.text: The content blocks, continuing the loop only when the response is a `tool_use` block with no accompanying assistant text, and otherwise treating the presence of a customer-facing message as the turn being finished.
options.explanation: Wrong. This looks reasonable if you read text as the model's signal that it is done, but it is exactly the failure in the traces: Claude routinely narrates alongside a tool request, so the "any text means finished" gate delivers the reply in the very mixed responses where the tool still needs to run.

options.label: C
options.text: The ordering of the content blocks, delivering the reply when the response ends on the assistant's text message and continuing the loop only when it ends on a `tool_use` block.
options.explanation: Wrong. Block ordering looks like a position-based signal because tool calls often trail the narration, but it is not a guaranteed control contract. In the traced responses text and the tool-use block coexist, so where the tool call lands relative to the prose does not reliably tell the runner that an execution is still pending.

options.label: D
options.text: The `stop_reason` field, continuing the loop and executing the pending tool when it is `"tool_use"`, and delivering the reply to the customer when it is `"end_turn"`.
options.explanation: Correct. `stop_reason` is the response's loop-control signal: `"tool_use"` means a tool call is pending and the runner must execute it and call Claude again, while `"end_turn"` means the response is complete and may be delivered. Branching on it resolves the defect even when a customer-facing message accompanies the tool request.

questions.explanation: D is the best answer: the runner branches on `stop_reason`, continuing and executing on `\"tool_use\"` and delivering on `\"end_turn\"`, which directly fixes the traced defect where a reply ships while a `process_refund` call is still pending. A inspects the same field but inverts the mapping, so it stops exactly when work remains and reproduces the bug. B gates on content blocks but requires the `tool_use` block to arrive with no assistant text, which misfires because Claude narrates alongside its tool requests, so the gate delivers in the very mixed responses the trace flags. C reads block ordering as a position-based signal, but ordering is not a control contract and text coexists with the tool-use block in the traced responses, so position does not reliably indicate a pending execution. Only D survives the test that the signal must hold a robust per-turn continue/stop decision even when text accompanies the tool call.

questions.text: Production logs show that in 23% of straightforward refund requests, the agent invokes `escalate_to_human` instead of calling `process_refund` to complete the refund itself. The two tools currently carry one-line descriptions ("Escalate to a human agent" / "Process a customer refund") and accept similar inputs. What should you change first to cut these unnecessary escalations?

options.label: A
options.text: Rewrite both tools' descriptions to spell out which request each one handles and the conditions that distinguish a routine refund from a genuine escalation
options.explanation: Correct. The agent picks tools by comparing their descriptions, and right now both lines are thin and overlapping, so it cannot tell a routine refund apart from an escalation. Stating each tool's applicability and its boundary against the other gives the model the comparative signal it is actually missing, which is why this is the first move before adding examples or rules on top of ambiguous definitions.

options.label: B
options.text: Add 5-8 few-shot examples to the system prompt showing straightforward refund requests being routed to `process_refund`
options.explanation: Incorrect. Demonstrating correct routing can nudge behavior, but it patches the symptom with extra prompt tokens while the underlying selection signal, the descriptions, stays ambiguous. The examples also generalize poorly to refund phrasings they do not cover, whereas fixing the descriptions corrects the signal the model relies on for every case.

options.label: C
options.text: Add a system-prompt rule telling the agent to prefer `process_refund` and reserve `escalate_to_human` for cases it cannot handle
options.explanation: Incorrect. A preference rule is a reasonable steer, but it sits on top of two tools the model still cannot tell apart, so it leans on probabilistic compliance to compensate for a definition gap. Until the descriptions state where each tool applies, the rule gives the agent a preference without the criteria to act on it.

options.label: D
options.text: Add a precondition that auto-routes requests below a fixed refund amount straight to `process_refund` and sends the rest to `escalate_to_human`
options.explanation: Incorrect. A deterministic threshold feels reliable, but the misroutes are not a missing business rule; a low amount does not make a request straightforward, and a high one does not make it an escalation. The agent is failing to distinguish the two tools, so an amount cutoff routes on the wrong dimension and would misclassify cases the descriptions would handle correctly.

questions.explanation: The answer is A. The agent selects tools by comparing their descriptions, and both are thin and overlapping, so it cannot separate a routine refund from a genuine escalation; rewriting each description to state its applicability and boundary fixes the exact signal that is failing, and does so before layering anything on top. B (few-shot examples) patches the symptom with prompt tokens while the descriptions stay ambiguous and generalizes poorly to unshown phrasings. C (a system-prompt preference rule) relies on probabilistic compliance and gives a preference without the criteria the agent lacks. D (an amount-threshold auto-router) routes on the wrong dimension, since refund size does not determine whether a case is straightforward, so it would misclassify cases the descriptions handle correctly.

questions.text: Your team ships a `/migrate-config` skill that rewrites legacy config files to the new schema, and it runs cleanly every time someone types the command by hand. Telemetry shows the problem is upstream: when developers describe the same work in plain English ("convert this old settings file", "update our legacy config"), Claude edits the files itself and the skill stays dormant. The skill's frontmatter `description` is `"Rewrites v1-schema configuration files into the v2 schema using the migration AST"`, and its body holds a thorough step-by-step procedure with worked examples of the input and output formats. You want the skill to engage on its own when these tasks surface. What is the most effective fix?

options.label: A
options.text: Prepend a section to the skill body listing the everyday phrasings developers use ("convert old settings", "update legacy config", "migrate this file") so the procedure starts by recognizing those requests
options.explanation: Tempting because it adds the exact developer vocabulary the match is missing, and to the skill's own files. But the body is read only after a skill is loaded; the decision to load it never consults the body, so these phrasings never reach the matcher and the skill stays dormant on plain-English requests.

options.label: B
options.text: Rewrite the `description` to lead with the everyday phrasings developers use ("convert or migrate an old/legacy settings or config file to the new schema") rather than the internal mechanism
options.explanation: Correct. The decision to engage a skill is made by comparing the incoming request against the `description` text, so a description framed around the internal mechanism ("v1-schema", "migration AST") has little to match against "convert this old settings file". Surfacing the words developers actually use closes that gap, and the body's procedure is untouched.

options.label: C
options.text: Add a line to the project `CLAUDE.md` instructing Claude to use `/migrate-config` whenever a request involves converting or updating legacy config files
options.explanation: Tempting because CLAUDE.md is loaded every session, so the instruction is reliably present and names the right trigger. But it is indirection layered on top of the matcher rather than a fix to it: it still depends on Claude connecting a free-form request to the named skill, costs context on every unrelated task, and leaves the description that drives matching unchanged.

options.label: D
options.text: Add before/after few-shot examples of real legacy files and their migrated output to the skill so its conversions are more reliable on the messy inputs developers have
options.explanation: Tempting because few-shot examples are the standard way to harden a procedure against varied real-world inputs. But this improves how the skill runs once invoked; the reported failure is that it is not invoked at all, so sharpening execution leaves the dormancy untouched.

questions.explanation: B is correct: a skill is auto-engaged by matching the incoming request against its `description`, so a description written around the internal mechanism (\"v1-schema configuration files\", \"migration AST\") barely overlaps with how developers phrase the task (\"convert this old settings file\"); leading the description with those everyday phrasings makes the match fire, with the body left as-is. A is the near-miss: it adds the same developer phrasings, but to the body, which is consulted only after a skill is loaded and so never influences whether it loads. C routes the trigger through a CLAUDE.md instruction, which is loaded every session but is indirection on top of the matcher rather than a fix to it, still depends on Claude linking the request to the named skill, and leaves the matching description unchanged. D adds few-shot before/after examples to harden the conversion itself, which improves execution once invoked but does nothing for the invocation that never happens.

questions.text: Auditing last sprint's commits, your team finds that the database migration files Claude Code authors are far more consistent (naming, up/down structure, transaction wrapping) when the prompt includes your two canonical "gold" migration scripts. They help only when you're writing a brand-new migration. The same db/migrations/ folder also hosts seed-data edits, fixes to broken migrations, and schema-diff reviews — and on the surface these all look identical: same folder, same file types, same kind of phrasing in your requests ("update the orders migration", "look at the migration diff"). Nothing in the open files or what you type marks which kind of work it is; you simply know, as you sit down, whether you're authoring fresh or not. You want the gold scripts in front of Claude when you're authoring and not the rest of the time. Which configuration best fits?

options.label: A
options.text: Put the two gold migration scripts and the authoring rules in the project CLAUDE.md so the conventions are part of Claude's baseline context for the repo.
options.explanation: Project CLAUDE.md loads at the start of every session in the repo, so the gold scripts ride along during seed-data edits, migration fixes, and schema-diff reviews too — the look-alike tasks the scenario says they don't help. A smart engineer reaches for CLAUDE.md because that's where project conventions normally live, but here it can't distinguish authoring from the sibling work that shares the folder.

options.label: B
options.text: Package the gold scripts and authoring rules as a skill under .claude/skills/ with a carefully written description like "use when writing a new database migration," so Claude pulls it in when the task matches.
options.explanation: Skills are the canonical on-demand mechanism for reusable context, which makes this the most tempting choice. But Claude decides to load a skill by matching its description against what it can see — the folder, the files, your wording — and the scenario states those are identical across authoring, fixing, seeding, and diffing. No wording of the description can split cases the task surface doesn't split, so the skill over-fires into the sibling work or misses when you want it.

options.label: C
options.text: Add a slash command under .claude/commands/ that bundles the gold scripts and authoring rules, which you type (e.g. /new-migration) when you sit down to write one.
options.explanation: A slash command enters the context on exactly the turns you type it, so the gold scripts arrive when you author and stay out of seed edits, fixes, and diff reviews in the same folder. The one signal that actually separates authoring from its look-alikes is the thing you know as you start — and a typed command is keyed to that, not to anything Claude has to infer from an indistinguishable task surface.

options.label: D
options.text: Define a migration-author subagent preloaded with the gold scripts, and delegate the drafting to it when you need a new migration.
options.explanation: Delegating to a focused subagent does scope the gold scripts to authoring and is explicitly invoked, so it avoids the over-triggering of A and B. But a subagent works in its own isolated context and hands a finished draft back across a boundary, whereas you're authoring interactively in your main editing session; the round-trip and context split are a mismatch for in-session work that a typed command handles directly.

questions.explanation: The scenario needs the gold scripts present only while you're authoring a migration, in a folder where authoring is indistinguishable on the surface from its sibling tasks (seed edits, fixes, diff reviews) — same files, same phrasing — so the only thing that marks authoring is what you know as you start. C — a slash command under .claude/commands/ that you type — fits because the load is keyed to that action: it lands on authoring turns and nothing else. B (a skill) is the closest competitor and the real trap: skills are the canonical on-demand context mechanism, but Claude loads one by matching its description against what it can observe, and the scenario stipulates the tasks present the same cues. No description, however careful, can separate cases the task surface doesn't separate, so the skill either over-fires into the sibling work — the exact crowding you're avoiding — or fails to fire when you want it. That is the tiebreak: the trigger you control by typing beats a trigger the model must infer over look-alikes. A (project CLAUDE.md) puts the scripts in every session's baseline, crowding the sibling tasks that share the folder. D (a preloaded subagent) is explicitly invoked and correctly scoped, but it runs in an isolated context and returns a draft across a handoff, mismatching the in-session, interactive authoring described here. Only C ties the context to the moment you act, without over-triggering or a delegation boundary.

questions.text: A lead engineer's committed .claude/CLAUDE.md doesn't spell out the team's conventions directly; it pulls them in with the line `@~/.claude/team-conventions.md`, which on her machine holds several rules — among them "name new test files with the .spec.ts suffix." Her sessions follow all of it. Two teammates pull the same commit (clean git status, identical Claude Code version) but their sessions keep producing *.test.ts and miss the other conventions too — because they have no team-conventions.md in their home directory, so the import resolves to nothing. What's the most effective fix so all three teammates' sessions reliably apply the team's conventions?

options.label: A
options.text: The team-conventions.md file is being excluded by .gitignore, which is why the pull never delivers it; remove the matching entry from .gitignore and commit the file so teammates receive it on their next pull.
options.explanation: Tempting because a file that one developer has and others lack, with a clean git status everywhere, is the classic signature of a gitignored file. But the target sits at ~/.claude/team-conventions.md, outside the repository tree entirely, so .gitignore was never the gate keeping it out of the pull — un-ignoring does nothing for a path git was never tracking. The file has to be relocated into the repo to travel with a clone.

options.label: B
options.text: Add a setup step to the repo's onboarding docs telling each developer to place their own copy of team-conventions.md into ~/.claude/ so the existing import resolves on every machine, leaving the home-directory layout untouched.
options.explanation: Reasonable because the import already points at the home directory, and keeping conventions there lets each dev mix in personal preferences. But it makes correct behavior depend on a manual step that drifts the moment the lead edits the conventions, and a fresh clone or a new hire who skips the step silently loses the rules again — the propagation problem returns instead of being closed.

options.label: C
options.text: Add a pre-commit hook that flags or renames any staged *.test.ts file to *.spec.ts, so the suffix convention holds for every developer.
options.explanation: Appealing because it enforces the one symptom you can see and works without touching how CLAUDE.md is shared. But it only patches the .spec.ts suffix; the teammates' sessions are still missing every other convention the import was supposed to carry, and a hook is separate machinery that doesn't restore the lost CLAUDE.md context that drove the behavior in the first place.

options.label: D
options.text: Paste the conventions into the committed .claude/CLAUDE.md, or move the target file into the repo and reference it with a repo-relative @-path; then commit.
options.explanation: Correct: the import line is shared by the commit, but its target lives under the lead's home directory and is never version-controlled, so only her sessions load the text. Putting the conventions inside the committed file, or moving the target into the repo so the @-import resolves through a path every clone contains, makes one shared source that reaches all three teammates and survives fresh clones and new hires.

questions.explanation: D is correct: the @-import line travels with the commit, but it points at ~/.claude/team-conventions.md, which lives outside the repo and is never committed, so teammates resolve an empty import and lose every convention it carried. Pasting the conventions into the committed .claude/CLAUDE.md, or relocating the target inside the repo and pointing the @-import at it with a repo-relative path, creates one version-controlled source that every clone receives. A misreads the cause as a .gitignore exclusion, but a path under ~/.claude is outside the repository tree and was never tracked, so un-ignoring it changes nothing. B keeps the home-directory layout and asks each developer to copy the file in by hand, which drifts on every edit and breaks for fresh clones and new hires. C only suppresses the visible .spec.ts symptom with a hook while leaving the rest of the team's conventions absent from teammates' sessions and not restoring the missing CLAUDE.md context.

questions.text: Telemetry from your support agent shows single-issue tickets like "where is order #5582" closing in 2-3 tool calls at a 93% resolution rate. A bundled ticket such as "change my email on file, re-issue the invoice for my March order, and waive the restocking fee" averages 14 tool calls and resolves only 48% of the time, with traces showing the agent re-fetches the customer record several times and often loses track of which asks it has already finished. The three asks touch different records but all key off the same customer. Which change is most likely to lift resolution on the bundled tickets?

options.label: A
options.text: Have the agent enumerate the asks up front into a tracked checklist, fetch the customer profile once, and work the list item by item, marking each done before composing one reply.
options.explanation: Correct. The traces name two failures: redundant profile fetches and the agent losing track of which asks are finished. An explicit up-front checklist over a single fetched profile fixes both directly, the enumeration gives the agent a durable record of completion so it stops re-doing or dropping asks, and the one fetch removes the re-reads, without adding any machinery the small, shared-customer asks do not need.

options.label: B
options.text: Hand the model the full ticket plus the customer profile in one prompt and let it reason through all the asks together in a single holistic pass.
options.explanation: Wrong. Preloading the profile does kill the re-fetches, and a single pass is appealingly simple, but an undifferentiated pass over three heterogeneous asks is exactly what produces the tool-call thrash and dropped requests; the asks need an explicit structure to track completion, not a larger prompt.

options.label: C
options.text: Hold the fetched profile in a shared context object the asks read from, and have the agent step through them in turn so each one builds on the results of the last.
options.explanation: Wrong. A shared context object cleanly removes the redundant fetches, and chaining steps so each sees prior results is the right move when the work is dependent; but these asks are independent, so forcing them to build on one another keeps the turn count inflated and adds an ordering coupling the problem never required.

options.label: D
options.text: Spin up an isolated sub-handler per ask, each running its own loop against the shared customer, then merge the three handlers' outputs into one reply.
options.explanation: Wrong. Isolating sub-tasks earns its keep when each carries a large independent context you want kept out of the main loop; here the contexts are tiny and identical, so isolation buys nothing, and because the handlers cannot see each other the completion-tracking the traces flag just reappears in the merge step instead of being solved.

questions.explanation: The traces point to two concrete failures: the agent re-fetches the customer record and loses track of which independent asks it has finished. A targets both: enumerate the asks into a tracked checklist, fetch the profile once, and mark each item done before one reply, which gives the agent a durable record of progress and removes the re-reads without extra machinery. B preloads the profile but drops the structure, and an undifferentiated single pass over heterogeneous asks is what causes the thrash and dropped asks in the first place. C shares the profile but chains the asks so each builds on the last, the right shape only when the work is dependent, here it inflates turns and adds needless ordering coupling. D isolates each ask in its own sub-handler, which pays off when sub-tasks carry large independent contexts, but these contexts are tiny and shared, so isolation adds coordination cost and, because the handlers cannot see one another, pushes the very completion-tracking problem into the merge rather than fixing it.

questions.text: On a long-lived feature PR, the CI pipeline re-runs Claude Code review on every push. Across 9 pushes the bot posted 47 review comments, and the author reports that about 40% re-raise concerns from earlier pushes on lines that were refactored two commits ago. The team wants to stop the repeated comments without dropping any class of check from the review. Which change best achieves this?

options.label: A
options.text: Restrict each run to the diff introduced since the previous push, so the bot reviews only the newly changed hunks and never revisits lines from earlier commits.
options.explanation: Scoping every run to the latest push's diff does cut repeats, and incremental review is a common CI cost-saver, so it looks like a clean fix. But it shrinks coverage: a concern that spans earlier and later commits, or one whose fix in a new hunk depends on untouched code, falls outside the window entirely. That violates the team's constraint of keeping every class of check, trading duplicates for blind spots.

options.label: B
options.text: Feed the prior run's comments and the latest commits to the model and have it judge, finding by finding, whether the new code resolved each concern, posting only the ones the changes left unaddressed.
options.explanation: Correct. The model reconciles each earlier finding against the actual current code, so a concern the refactor fixed is dropped while one the refactor merely touched-but-didn't-fix is re-posted. It still reads the whole changeset, so no class of check is lost, and the duplication is removed at generation time by deciding addressed-versus-still-live per finding rather than by whether the comment was seen before.

options.label: C
options.text: Deduplicate against the prior run by keying each finding on file path, line number, and rule id, and rely on GitHub's outdated mechanism to suppress matches on lines that have since changed.
options.explanation: Wrong. Position-and-rule keying is the standard dedup primitive and feels precise, but the anchor breaks on exactly the refactored lines the author flagged: when code moves, a still-unaddressed concern no longer matches its old key and re-posts as new, while a different rule firing on the old line slips through. The mechanism keys on where the code sits, not on whether the concern was actually resolved.

options.label: D
options.text: Give the model the prior run's comments along with the latest commits and instruct it to skip any finding it has already raised on this PR, posting only findings it has not surfaced before.
options.explanation: Wrong. Like the correct option this passes prior context to the model, so it sounds equally grounded, and skip-what-you-already-said is the intuitive dedup instruction. But suppressing by whether a finding was previously raised conflates having-mentioned-it with having-fixed-it: a concern the refactor touched but did not resolve gets dropped because it was reported earlier, quietly losing a live check the team wanted kept.

questions.explanation: B is correct because reconciling each prior finding against the latest commits decides addressed-versus-still-live per finding: fixed concerns are dropped and ones the refactor touched but did not resolve are re-posted, all while the model still reads the whole changeset so no class of check is lost. A restricts review to the newest diff, which removes repeats but loses coverage on cross-commit and dependency cases, breaking the keep-every-check constraint. C dedupes on file, line, and rule id, but that key is exactly what refactoring destabilizes, so still-live concerns on moved lines re-post as new. D also feeds the model prior context, but it suppresses by whether a finding was already raised rather than by whether the code fixed it, so a touched-but-unresolved concern is dropped because it was mentioned before. The discriminator is that only B judges resolution against the current code per finding.

questions.text: Your team's `/audit-upgrades` slash command has Claude weigh several dependency-bump strategies (pin, range, lockfile-only) before a release. Telemetry from the last sprint shows that in 9 of 14 sessions where the command ran, Claude's later answers kept resurfacing upgrade plans the command had already rejected and stale version trade-offs, derailing the patch work that followed. Which change most effectively keeps that weighing from coloring the rest of the session?

options.label: A
options.text: Run `/compact` right after the command so the long deliberation is condensed before patch work begins.
options.explanation: `/compact` summarizes the conversation and reloads that summary as the new context, which does cut token usage and is the natural reach when deliberation is cluttering a session. But the summary preserves the deliberation's conclusions, including which plans were rejected and the trade-offs behind them, so the exact content steering later answers stays in context.

options.label: B
options.text: Run `/clear` right after the command so the deliberation is gone before patch work begins.
options.explanation: `/clear` removes the deliberation outright, which feels like the cleanest reset. The cost is that it wipes the entire conversation, taking the release goals, the chosen strategy, and any context the upcoming patch work depends on with it, and it leans on the user remembering to run it every time.

options.label: C
options.text: Have the command launch a general-purpose subagent via the Task tool to do the weighing and return only its recommended strategy.
options.explanation: The subagent does the weighing in its own context window, so the rejected plans and stale trade-offs play out there and never enter the main thread; only the recommended strategy comes back. Baking the delegation into the command makes the isolation structural rather than a step the user has to remember, so later turns keep the recommendation without the deliberation to bias them.

options.label: D
options.text: Add `context: fork` to the command's frontmatter so the weighing runs in a forked context that discards itself afterward.
options.explanation: Scoping the isolation to the command via frontmatter is the right instinct, and `context: fork` reads like a sibling of real keys such as `model` and `allowed-tools`. But it is not an actual slash-command frontmatter field, so it is silently ignored: the command still runs inline and the rejected plans land in the main thread exactly as before.

questions.explanation: The weighing of upgrade strategies needs to stay out of the main conversation so its rejected plans cannot bias later turns, while the chosen strategy still has to survive. C does both: launching a general-purpose subagent through the Task tool runs the deliberation in a separate context window, so only the recommended strategy returns and the rejected plans never enter the main thread; baking it into the command makes the isolation structural rather than a manual step. A (`/compact`) condenses the session but its summary keeps the rejected-plan conclusions that are doing the biasing. B (`/clear`) removes the deliberation but also destroys the release goals and chosen strategy the patch work still needs, and depends on the user running it every time. D names `context: fork`, which sounds like a real frontmatter key but is not a supported slash-command field, so it is ignored and the deliberation runs inline as before. C is the only change that actually isolates the weighing in a separate context and still returns just the recommendation the session needs.

questions.text: A dashboard for your billing-support agent shows that gathering the facts for one dispute now takes four API round-trips before any action is taken. Tracing a session, you see Claude emit three separate tool_use turns in a row — `fetch_account_status`, then `get_payment_history`, then `check_dispute_eligibility` — each its own request/response, even though the three are independent and all are needed before the agent decides anything. The later `file_dispute` action is correctly a separate downstream turn and is not the concern. The three lookup tools work fine. What most effectively cuts the round-trips spent gathering facts?

options.label: A
options.text: Add a planning instruction telling Claude to enumerate every fact it needs for the dispute up front, then issue the lookups, so the gathering trips collapse into one pass.
options.explanation: Enumerating the needed facts in its reasoning makes Claude's plan explicit, but it does not change how that plan is executed: Claude still emits one tool_use block per turn unless asked to do otherwise, so the three lookups stay sequential. Planning sharpens what gets called, not how many turns the calls span.

options.label: B
options.text: Replace the three lookup tools with one `gather_dispute_facts` tool that queries all three sources internally and returns the combined facts in a single round-trip.
options.explanation: Bundling the three sources behind one tool does reach the facts in one round-trip, but it couples three independent, individually reusable lookups into a fixed composite that other flows can no longer call piecemeal. The same single trip is available without giving up that composability, so the new surface trades away flexibility for no extra latency gain.

options.label: C
options.text: Have the executor recognize the three consecutive independent lookups and run the later two speculatively while the first is still in flight, returning each result as it finishes.
options.explanation: Speculative execution overlaps the wall-clock time of the calls, but Claude still emitted three separate tool_use turns, so the conversation still makes three request/response trips to the model. It shortens how long the lookups take, not the count of round-trips, which is what the dashboard is measuring.

options.label: D
options.text: Prompt Claude to issue the three independent lookups as parallel tool_use blocks in one turn, and have the executor return all three results together before the next call.
options.explanation: Claude can place several independent tool_use blocks in a single response; directing it to do so for the three lookups, and returning their results together, makes the model take one turn instead of three. That changes the emission pattern at its source, collapsing the gathering into a single round-trip using only the existing tools.

questions.explanation: The dashboard counts round-trips, and a round-trip is created each time Claude emits a tool_use turn — so the only fix that reduces the count is one that makes Claude emit the three lookups in a single turn. D does exactly that: Claude natively supports multiple independent tool_use blocks in one response, and prompting it to batch the three lookups, with the executor returning all results together, turns three gathering turns into one using the existing tools. A makes Claude's plan explicit but leaves emission untouched — without asking for multiple blocks per turn, the enumerated lookups still go out one turn at a time. B's `gather_dispute_facts` reaches one round-trip too, but only by fusing three independently reusable lookups into a fixed composite; the same single trip is achievable without sacrificing that composability, so it gives up flexibility for nothing. C overlaps the lookups' wall-clock time speculatively, but the model still emitted three tool_use turns, so three round-trips remain — it cuts latency, not trip count. The discriminator is whether the option changes how Claude emits tool_use turns; only D does without a tradeoff.

questions.text: Your four-person team triages production errors in Claude Code through a Sentry MCP server, and each engineer hits the API with their own personal Sentry token. The server lives in the project-scoped `.mcp.json` so everyone runs the identical setup, but this morning the pre-commit secret scanner blocked a push: a teammate had pasted their literal token into that committed file. You want to keep the one shared `.mcp.json` in the repo and have each engineer's session pick up their own token. How should you wire up the credential?

options.label: A
options.text: Reference the token as `${SENTRY_TOKEN}` in the committed `.mcp.json`, and have each engineer export `SENTRY_TOKEN` from their shell profile before launching Claude Code.
options.explanation: Correct. `.mcp.json` expands `${SENTRY_TOKEN}` from the environment of the shell that launches Claude Code, so each engineer's own exported value is substituted at startup and the tracked file holds only the placeholder. Every teammate runs the same committed config and the scanner sees no credential.

options.label: B
options.text: Keep `${SENTRY_TOKEN}` in the committed `.mcp.json`, and have each engineer drop their token into a gitignored `.env` beside it so the value loads when the server starts.
options.explanation: Incorrect. `.mcp.json` expansion draws from the launching shell's environment and does not auto-source a project `.env`, so without a wrapper like `dotenv -- claude` the variable stays unset and the Sentry server starts with an empty token. The gitignored file looks like the right secrets idiom but its value never reaches the expansion.

options.label: C
options.text: Write `${SENTRY_TOKEN:-<shared team token>}` in the committed `.mcp.json` so a freshly cloned checkout authenticates immediately and each engineer overrides it once they export their own token.
options.explanation: Incorrect. The fallback literal is itself a real credential committed to the tracked file, which is exactly what the scanner blocked. It also means any engineer who hasn't exported their variable runs on the shared token instead of failing at launch, so error attribution and per-user rate limits quietly break.

options.label: D
options.text: Store the value as a `op://team-vault/sentry/token` secret reference in the committed `.mcp.json` so the literal never lands in the repo and 1Password resolves it per engineer.
options.explanation: Incorrect. `.mcp.json` only performs `${VAR}` shell-environment expansion; it does not dereference `op://` secret-manager URIs, so the server receives the literal string `op://team-vault/sentry/token` as its token. Resolving it would require launching through an `op run` wrapper, which the committed config alone doesn't provide.

questions.explanation: A is correct: `.mcp.json` substitutes `${SENTRY_TOKEN}` from the environment of the shell that launches Claude Code, so each engineer's exported token is injected at startup while the committed file carries only the placeholder. B fails because expansion reads the launching shell's environment and does not auto-load a project `.env`, so the variable is unset and the server starts with no token unless launched through a dotenv-style wrapper. C re-commits a real credential as the `:-` fallback (the very thing the scanner blocked) and lets an engineer who never set their variable run on the shared token, breaking per-user attribution. D puts an `op://` reference in the file, but `.mcp.json` only does shell-variable expansion and passes the literal `op://...` string through unresolved without an `op run` wrapper. Only A delivers each engineer's own token at launch with nothing secret in the tracked file.

questions.text: Production dashboards show the /reports/export endpoint's p95 latency climbed from 400ms to 9s over the past week, with three timeouts in last night's on-call log. The endpoint streams a CSV by looping rows in app code. Three structurally different fixes are on the table: push the aggregation down into one SQL query (fast, but report logic moves into the database), generate the file asynchronously and return a download link (changes the API contract and client UX), or paginate and stream incrementally (keeps the contract and caps memory, but not total time). Open questions remain: whether callers can tolerate an async link, how large the biggest export is, and which step inside the request dominates the 9s. You open Claude Code. How should you approach this?

options.label: A
options.text: Add per-step timing around the query, the row loop, and the CSV write, redeploy to capture a real breakdown, then implement whichever stage dominates the 9s.
options.explanation: Tempting because profiling before optimizing is the textbook move and it would settle one of the open questions empirically. But it resolves only the runtime breakdown; whether callers tolerate an async link and how large exports get are product and contract questions no profiler surfaces, and those are what decide which of the three fixes is even permissible. It commits to a code path while two of the three unknowns still gate the architecture.

options.label: B
options.text: Use plan mode to trace the export path and characterize the cost breakdown, lay the three fixes against the open questions, and come back with a recommended direction before editing code.
options.explanation: Best: the constraints span code (which stage costs the 9s, traceable by reading the export path) and product (async tolerance, max export size) and the three fixes diverge architecturally rather than stylistically, so any single edit made now is a bet on unresolved questions. Plan mode investigates the runtime and frames the fixes against every open question, producing one grounded recommendation before a line is committed.

options.label: C
options.text: Implement incremental pagination and streaming, keeping the existing API contract and bounding memory while avoiding any change callers can observe.
options.explanation: Tempting as the lowest-risk change: it preserves the contract and rules out memory blowup as the largest export grows. But the scenario states this caps memory, not total time, and the regression is latency (400ms to 9s with timeouts), so it leaves the actual symptom intact while feeling like progress.

options.label: D
options.text: Implement async generation with a job queue and a signed download link so the endpoint returns immediately and the 9s work happens out of band.
options.explanation: Tempting because it most decisively kills the latency and timeouts regardless of which internal stage is slow. But it changes the API contract and client UX, and whether callers can consume an async link is one of the unanswered questions; shipping it commits every caller to a new flow that may not be acceptable.

questions.explanation: B is best: the deciding constraints are split between code (which step inside the request dominates the 9s, discoverable by tracing the export path) and product (whether callers tolerate an async link, how large the biggest export is), and the three candidate fixes diverge architecturally rather than stylistically. Any edit made now bets on unresolved questions, so plan mode is used to investigate the cost breakdown and weigh the fixes against the open questions before recommending a direction. A profiles to find the dominant stage, which settles only the runtime question and leaves the contract and size questions—the ones that decide which fix is permissible—unanswered while still committing a code change. C ships incremental streaming, which the scenario says caps memory but not total time, so it does not touch the latency regression that caused the timeouts. D ships async generation, which decisively removes the latency but changes the API contract no one has confirmed callers can accept.

questions.text: On-call is debugging the support agent. In the trace logs, customer messages that contain the word "warranty" (e.g., "is my blender still under warranty?") end the turn with escalate_to_specialist as the agent's very first tool call about 97% of the time, with check_order_status never issued. The same question phrased without that word (e.g., "is my blender still covered?") issues check_order_status first about 94% of the time and resolves without escalating. The split shows up even on brand-new single-turn conversations, and the four MCP tool schemas are byte-for-byte the same across both phrasings. What most likely explains the pattern?

options.label: A
options.text: A pre-model input classifier tags inbound messages for "warranty" and routes those straight to escalate_to_specialist, so the agent never gets a chance to call check_order_status for them.
options.explanation: Tempting because a deterministic input router would explain the clean, word-keyed cliff. But a pre-model route bypasses the agent: the trace would show no model decision at all for warranty turns. Here the logs show the agent itself issuing escalate_to_specialist as its own first tool call, so the choice is being made inside the model's turn, not upstream of it.

options.label: B
options.text: The system prompt carries a few-shot block whose worked examples for warranty questions all demonstrate immediate escalation, training the agent to copy that pattern.
options.explanation: Plausible because few-shot examples strongly shape behavior and could push warranty messages to escalation. But few-shot patterns generalize by meaning, not by literal string, so a semantically identical phrasing like "still covered?" would inherit the same escalation pull. The fact that synonyms route normally argues against examples being the driver.

options.label: C
options.text: The system prompt contains a literal conditional keyed on the token "warranty" that directs the agent to call escalate_to_specialist, so the outcome follows the exact word rather than the request's meaning.
options.explanation: Correct. A verbatim string condition the model is following short-circuits ordinary tool selection only when that exact token is present, which is precisely why "warranty" escalates while "covered" runs the normal check_order_status path, and why the model itself issues the call on a fresh single-turn message.

options.label: D
options.text: The escalate_to_specialist description lists "warranty-related questions" as its primary use case, so the agent selects it over check_order_status whenever warranty comes up.
options.explanation: Plausible because tool descriptions are the main signal the model uses to pick tools, and naming warranty would pull those messages toward escalation. But description-driven selection is semantic and competes with the other tools rather than overriding them, so it would also catch "covered"/"under guarantee" and would not produce a switch keyed to one literal word.

questions.explanation: C is correct: a verbatim string condition in the system prompt is the one mechanism that fires only on the exact token \"warranty,\" which matches the model issuing escalate_to_specialist itself on fresh single-turn messages while the synonym \"covered\" runs the normal check_order_status path. A puts the trigger upstream of the model, but the trace shows the agent making its own first tool call, so the decision is inside the turn, not in a pre-model router. B (few-shot examples) and D (a warranty-naming tool description) both act semantically and would carry over to synonyms, so neither can explain why outcomes track one literal word rather than the question's meaning.

questions.text: You're using Claude Code to generate a function that converts user-entered date filters into a SQL WHERE clause for an analytics pipeline. The phrasing users type is messy and overlapping: "this quarter," "QTD," "the current period," "last 30 days," "month to date," "trailing 4 weeks," and dozens of near-synonyms, each colliding with your company's offset fiscal calendar, a Monday-start week, and a reporting timezone that crosses DST. Across three iterations you've kept elaborating the boundary rules in prose, and each rewrite makes Claude resolve some cases the way you meant while quietly shifting others—"trailing 4 weeks" snaps to calendar weeks one pass and rolling days the next; the fiscal offset gets applied to some phrases but not others. No single written rule seems to cover every interacting case the same way twice. What's the most effective approach for the next iteration?

options.label: A
options.text: Keep formalizing the prose: tighten the rule set into a numbered specification that spells out each phrase, the fiscal offset, week start, and timezone handling explicitly.
options.explanation: A smart engineer reaches for this because writing the rules down once, completely, feels like it should settle the matter. But it is more of the same channel that has already drifted three times: with this many interacting phrases, calendars, and boundary conventions, a prose spec dense enough to cover them all becomes ambiguous prose itself, and Claude keeps resolving the unstated interactions differently. The scenario has already shown that elaborating the rules in words doesn't converge.

options.label: B
options.text: Have Claude first build a parser that tokenizes each phrase into a structured form (period type, length, unit) and then generate SQL from that structure.
options.explanation: Parsing the input into a normalized structure is a reasonable instinct—it gives every phrase a single intermediate representation. But the parser still has to decide what each structured form means against your fiscal offset, week start, and timezone, and those are exactly the interactions you have never pinned down. It adds a tokenization layer while leaving the contested semantics for Claude to guess, so the same drift reappears one level down.

options.label: C
options.text: Point Claude at a well-known date library and have the generated SQL derive its quarter, week, and period boundaries from that library's conventions.
options.explanation: Anchoring to an established library's date semantics is tempting because it replaces hand-written rules with a tested authority. The problem is that the library encodes generic calendar conventions, not your offset fiscal calendar, Monday week start, or reporting timezone, so its boundaries won't match your intended ones for the very phrases that drift. It trades your ambiguity for the library's fixed choices rather than your actual ones.

options.label: D
options.text: Give Claude a handful of concrete input-to-output pairs: each contested phrase mapped to the exact WHERE clause you expect, including the tricky boundary cases.
options.explanation: Concrete input-output pairs show Claude the exact SQL you want for the phrases that keep shifting—'trailing 4 weeks,' the fiscal-offset cases, the DST boundary—rather than describing them. Where prose under-determines how the conventions interact, a worked example fixes each contested case to one observable answer, giving Claude a target to match instead of a rule to interpret.

questions.explanation: D is correct: the failure is that prose rules keep being read differently because too many phrases interact with the fiscal offset, week start, and timezone for any single written rule to converge. Concrete input-output pairs for the contested cases pin each one to an exact expected WHERE clause, replacing interpretation with matching. A repeats the channel that already drifted three times—a prose specification dense enough to cover all the interactions is itself ambiguous, so it keeps shifting. B normalizes phrases into a structure but still leaves the parser to decide the same unspecified semantics, so the drift moves down a layer rather than disappearing. C anchors to a library whose generic calendar conventions don't match your offset fiscal calendar, Monday week, or reporting timezone, so its boundaries are wrong for exactly the drifting phrases. D is one sentence and not the longest option.

questions.text: A monorepo's root CLAUDE.md has grown to ~700 lines mixing lint rules, commit conventions, GraphQL schema patterns, and on-call runbooks. Every edit triggers merge conflicts, and no single team owns the file. The platform team wants each topic to live in its own small file that its owning team edits independently, while Claude still has all of that guidance in context for any work in the repo, not just work that touches a matching directory. After splitting the content into `docs/commits.md`, `docs/graphql.md`, and `docs/runbooks.md`, how should they wire those files in?

options.label: A
options.text: Add `@docs/commits.md`, `@docs/graphql.md`, and `@docs/runbooks.md` lines to the root CLAUDE.md so each file is expanded into context at session start.
options.explanation: Correct. The `@path` import lines are resolved when the session starts and the referenced files are expanded into context in full, so all three topics are present for any work in the repo while each file stays small and separately owned. This is the mechanism that satisfies both the always-loaded and team-owned-files requirements here.

options.label: B
options.text: Move each topic into `.claude/rules/` as its own file and add a `paths:` frontmatter block scoping each rule to the directories that topic governs.
options.explanation: Tempting because `.claude/rules/` is the documented way to break instructions into topic files, and scoping looks tidy. But a rule with a `paths:` field only loads when Claude works with files matching that pattern, so commit conventions and runbooks would be absent during work that doesn't touch their directories, which violates the requirement that all guidance be present for any work in the repo.

options.label: C
options.text: Place each topic file as a CLAUDE.md inside the subdirectory it governs, letting the directory hierarchy distribute ownership across the teams.
options.explanation: Tempting because nested CLAUDE.md files are a real hierarchy feature and naturally hand ownership to each subtree. But files in subdirectories load on demand only when Claude reads files there, not at launch, so their guidance is missing whenever Claude is working elsewhere in the monorepo.

options.label: D
options.text: Keep the topic files in a shared directory and load them with `--add-dir` plus the `CLAUDE_CODE_ADDITIONAL_DIRECTORIES_CLAUDE_MD` environment variable so the memory files come in at launch.
options.explanation: Tempting because this genuinely does pull external memory files into context at startup. But it depends on the flag and env var being set on every invocation, and the topic files then live outside the project rather than versioned and owned alongside the code, which is the opposite of what the teams want here.

questions.explanation: The split files must be present for any work in the repo and stay owned by their teams inside version control. A wires them with `@path` import lines, which are expanded into context at session start, satisfying both needs. B uses `.claude/rules/` with `paths:` frontmatter, but path-scoped rules load only when Claude touches matching files, so cross-cutting guidance disappears during unrelated work. C uses nested subdirectory CLAUDE.md files, which load on demand when Claude reads files in those directories rather than at launch. D loads files via `--add-dir` and the additional-directories env var, which works but requires the flag every launch and moves the files outside the repo, breaking team ownership in source control. Only A keeps every topic loaded for all work while leaving each file small and independently owned.

questions.text: A postmortem covers a `/seed-data` skill that populates staging tables from a fixture name passed via `$ARGUMENTS`. Three findings: of 40 invocations last sprint, 9 ran with no fixture name and wrote a file literally called `seed-.sql`; in two cases the generated SQL pulled column names from an unrelated refactor discussed earlier in the same session; and once the skill ran a `TRUNCATE` against a shared table because it inherited the session's full tool access. Which change set most reliably prevents all three from recurring?

options.label: A
options.text: In SKILL.md, add a rule that the skill must have a non-empty fixture name before it generates any SQL, set context: fork so the skill runs in its own context window, and list only the file-write tools under allowed-tools.
options.explanation: The fork and the scoped allowed-tools are exactly right for the schema-bleed and the destructive TRUNCATE, which makes this look complete. But the fixture-name guard lives as an instruction the model is asked to honor, so an empty argument can still slip through on the runs where the model does not stop first. The one finding the postmortem opens with is left to model compliance rather than a hard check.

options.label: B
options.text: Have the skill body check $ARGUMENTS at the top and exit with an error when it is empty, set context: fork so session turns are not carried into the skill, and restrict allowed-tools to the file-write tools the skill needs.
options.explanation: The early check on $ARGUMENTS halts the run before any SQL is written, so the empty-name path is closed by control flow rather than by asking the model to behave. The fork gives the skill a fresh context window so the earlier refactor cannot be pulled in, and the narrowed allowed-tools means the TRUNCATE call has no tool to reach. Each finding is closed by something that holds independent of what the model decides to do.

options.label: C
options.text: Switch the parameter from $ARGUMENTS to a $1 positional so the fixture name is required, pin the schema with an @schema.sql reference in SKILL.md, and add a description field noting that the skill writes to staging tables.
options.explanation: $1 does not force the caller to supply anything; an omitted positional resolves to an empty string, so the seed-.sql case can recur. The @schema.sql reference adds the correct columns to context but does not remove the earlier refactor's columns that are already in the session, and the description field is help text that does not touch tool access. All three findings remain reachable.

options.label: D
options.text: Split /seed-data into a /seed-build skill that emits SQL and a /seed-load skill that runs it, each with allowed-tools narrowed to its own job, and add a wrapper command that validates the fixture name before dispatching either one.
options.explanation: The wrapper does validate the fixture name up front and the per-skill allowed-tools keeps the loader from holding write access it does not need, so two findings look handled. But neither new skill sets context: fork, so /seed-build still runs inside the session and can pull the earlier refactor's column names into its SQL. The split adds two skills and a dispatcher without addressing the context-isolation finding.

questions.explanation: B closes each finding with something that holds regardless of the model's choices: an early $ARGUMENTS check exits before any SQL is generated, context: fork gives a fresh context window so the earlier refactor cannot leak in, and a scoped allowed-tools removes the tool the TRUNCATE used. A is the closest competitor and gets the fork and the scoped tools right, but its fixture-name guard is a rule the model is asked to follow, so the empty-argument path it shares with B stays open to compliance failures. C relies on $1, which resolves to an empty string when omitted, an @-reference that adds rather than replaces context, and a description field that does not restrict tools, so none of the three are actually blocked. D validates the name in a wrapper and narrows each skill's tools but never isolates context, so the schema-bleed finding persists while the decomposition adds surface for no gain.

questions.text: A trace from the research system shows the coordinator dispatching the web-search and document-analysis subagents in parallel; each subagent's window fills with raw pages and long PDFs while the coordinator's window holds only the short summaries they return. A new engineer asks why the coordinator doesn't just load all of that retrieved material into its own window and analyze it directly. Which explanation best accounts for why the coordinator's window stays small in this design?

options.label: A
options.text: Running each subagent in its own window is what lets them execute concurrently, so several large retrievals are processed at once instead of sequentially through the coordinator.
options.explanation: Concurrency is a real benefit of separate windows, but it explains why the work finishes faster, not why the coordinator's window stays small. Parallel subagents could still funnel all their raw output back into a single bloated coordinator window.

options.label: B
options.text: Each subagent carries its own focused system prompt and tool set in isolation, so the coordinator's instructions don't bleed into the search or analysis steps.
options.explanation: Per-agent specialization is genuine, but it comes from how the orchestration layer configures each subagent, not from the size of any window. It says nothing about why the coordinator holds only summaries.

options.label: C
options.text: The bulky raw tokens are spent inside each subagent and only distilled findings come back, so the coordinator can reason across far more sources than would ever fit in one window.
options.explanation: Correct. Because the heavy material is consumed where it lands and only condensed results return, the coordinator's finite budget is spent on synthesis across many subagents rather than on raw pages, which is exactly the asymmetry the trace shows.

options.label: D
options.text: A malformed or adversarial document corrupts only the window of the subagent that fetched it, keeping the coordinator's state clean if one retrieval goes bad.
options.explanation: Fault containment is a real advantage of isolation, but the trace shows no corrupted or adversarial input; the question is about token volume, and blast-radius isolation doesn't explain why the coordinator's window stays small in the normal case.

questions.explanation: C is correct: the raw retrieval (page dumps, long PDFs) is consumed inside each subagent and only distilled findings flow back, so the coordinator's limited budget covers synthesis across many more sources than could fit in a single window — the exact asymmetry the trace shows. A names a true benefit (parallel execution) but that explains speed, not why the coordinator stays small; parallel subagents could still dump raw output into one bloated window. B describes per-agent specialization, which is set by the orchestration config rather than by window size and doesn't bear on token volume. D describes real fault containment, but the trace shows no corrupted input — the issue is the volume of raw tokens, not blast radius.
