questions.text: Your support agent processes refunds through `process_refund`, a managed payments connector your team integrates but does not own the source of. Company policy caps any single ticket at $200, but production traces show that in roughly 8% of tickets the agent submits a refund above that figure, and finance confirms the funds settle as soon as the connector runs. You want over-cap refunds stopped without depending on the model getting the math right. Which approach most reliably keeps them from being paid out?

options.label: A
options.text: Add a guard clause at the top of `process_refund` that compares the requested amount to $200 and rejects the call when it is higher, returning an error the agent can relay.
options.explanation: A validation check inside the function is the natural deterministic fix, but `process_refund` is a managed connector your team does not own the source of, so you cannot insert code into its body. The enforcement has to live in a layer you control around the call, not within the connector itself.

options.label: B
options.text: Register a PostToolUse hook on `process_refund` that reads the settled amount and submits a reversing charge through the connector whenever it exceeded the $200 cap.
options.explanation: A PostToolUse hook is real code-level enforcement, but it runs after the connector has settled the refund, so the over-cap payout has already happened. Clawing the money back depends on a reversing charge that can fail or require finance to intervene, so it does not prevent the payout the way the requirement demands.

options.label: C
options.text: Expose a `check_refund_policy` tool that returns the allowed maximum for a ticket, and instruct the agent to call it and stay within that figure before invoking `process_refund`.
options.explanation: Surfacing the limit through a tool gives the agent better information, but honoring it still rides on the same model reasoning that already misfires ~8% of the time. A turn where the agent skips the check or rounds up still reaches the connector with an over-cap amount, so the cap is advisory rather than enforced.

options.label: D
options.text: Register a PreToolUse hook on `process_refund` that reads the requested amount and returns a deny decision when it exceeds $200, so the call is rejected before the connector runs.
options.explanation: A PreToolUse hook executes in your own code on the way to the connector and can deny the dispatch, so an over-cap refund is rejected before any funds move. Because it sits outside the connector you cannot edit and fires before settlement, it closes both gaps the other options leave open.

questions.explanation: D is correct: a PreToolUse hook runs in your own code before the call reaches the connector and can return a deny decision, blocking any refund over $200 before funds settle—without relying on the model's arithmetic. A proposes a guard clause inside `process_refund`, the natural deterministic fix, but the connector is third-party and you do not own its source, so you cannot add code there; the check must live in a layer around the call. B uses a PostToolUse hook, which is genuine enforcement code but fires only after the refund has settled, so the over-cap payout already happened and a reversing charge may fail or need finance to step in. C exposes a `check_refund_policy` tool, which informs the agent but still leaves compliance to the same model behavior that fails ~8% of the time, so a skipped check still reaches the connector over-cap. Only D enforces the cap deterministically in a layer you control and before settlement.

questions.text: Reviewing session transcripts, you notice Claude keeps pulling your CI/CD pipeline runbook and your test-fixture generation steps into context during small unrelated edits such as rewording a log message or splitting a function, even though nothing about the pipeline or fixtures is changing. Both runbooks currently live in CLAUDE.md alongside your naming conventions and secret-handling rules, and the pipeline work often starts from root-level scripts while fixture work happens across many test files. You want the runbooks to appear only when Claude is genuinely doing that work, while the conventions and security rules keep applying every session. Which restructuring is most effective?

options.label: A
options.text: Extract each runbook into its own Skill whose description names the task it covers, so Claude pulls it in when the running work matches that description.
options.explanation: Correct. A Skill is matched to the task Claude is actually performing via its description, so the CI/CD runbook loads during pipeline work and the fixture runbook during fixture work, no matter which files are touched, and neither appears during an unrelated log-message or refactor edit. The conventions and secret-handling rules left in CLAUDE.md continue to load every session. This is the only option whose load trigger is the task itself.

options.label: B
options.text: Convert each runbook into a slash command in .claude/commands/ that the developer runs at the start of the relevant task to load that guidance.
options.explanation: Incorrect. A slash command is a fine way to package an on-demand runbook, but it loads when a person runs the command, not when Claude detects that pipeline or fixture work is underway. The scenario asks for the guidance to surface based on the task in progress, so it depends on the developer remembering to trigger it rather than on the work itself.

options.label: C
options.text: Move the pipeline runbook into a CLAUDE.md under your CI directory and the fixture runbook into one under your tests directory, so each loads when Claude works in that subtree.
options.explanation: Incorrect. A nested CLAUDE.md keys on file location as a stand-in for the task, which fits some work but not this one: pipeline runbooks are needed when reasoning from root-level scripts outside the CI directory, and any small edit inside the tests directory drags in the whole fixture runbook even when no fixtures are being built. A description-matched Skill keys on the task itself, which is what the requirement describes.

options.label: D
options.text: Move each runbook into the system prompt of a dedicated subagent and delegate pipeline or fixture work to it, keeping that material out of the main context.
options.explanation: Incorrect. Putting a runbook in a subagent's context is a real way to isolate specialized guidance, but it restructures how the work runs by handing it to a separate delegated agent. The need here is for the runbook to reach the agent doing the inline edit at the moment the task calls for it, which a description-matched Skill delivers without changing the execution model.

questions.explanation: All four are real Claude Code mechanisms; they differ in what triggers the load. A wins because a Skill is matched to the task in progress through its description, so each runbook surfaces during the relevant work regardless of which files are open, while the conventions and secret-handling rules stay in the always-loaded CLAUDE.md. B triggers on the developer running a command rather than on Claude detecting the task, so the guidance depends on the person remembering. C triggers on file location as a proxy for the task, which misses pipeline work driven from root-level scripts and over-loads the fixture runbook on any incidental edit inside the tests subtree. D triggers on delegating to a separate subagent, restructuring execution instead of surfacing the runbook to the agent making the inline edit. Only A keys the load on the task itself, which is the stated requirement.

questions.text: A teammate on your Claude Code project wants `/refactor` to enforce stricter type-checking for their own work without changing it for anyone else. The project ships `.claude/skills/refactor/SKILL.md`, and they added `~/.claude/skills/refactor/SKILL.md` with the tighter rules. Running `/refactor` still applies the team's lenient type-checking; the stricter checks they added never run. What should you tell them to do?

options.label: A
options.text: Move the stricter type-checking into the shared `.claude/skills/refactor/SKILL.md`, since that is the file `/refactor` actually executes for the project
options.explanation: This is the file the command runs, so the stricter rules would take effect immediately, but editing the shared project skill applies the tighter type-checking to every teammate's `/refactor` runs, breaking the requirement to leave others unaffected.

options.label: B
options.text: Register the personal customization under a separate command name such as `/refactor-strict`
options.explanation: Correct. A distinct command name has no collision with the project's `refactor` skill, so the personal SKILL.md is invoked on its own terms. This gives the teammate their stricter type-checking while the shared `/refactor` continues to behave the same for everyone else.

options.label: C
options.text: Copy the project skill's full contents into `~/.claude/skills/refactor/SKILL.md` and layer the stricter type-checking on top, so the personal copy is complete rather than partial
options.explanation: This assumes the personal skill failed because it was incomplete, but `/refactor` resolves to the project skill of that name; a same-named personal skill is not the one invoked no matter how complete its contents are, so the stricter rules still never run.

options.label: D
options.text: Put the stricter type-checking guidance in their personal `~/.claude/CLAUDE.md` so it applies across their own sessions
options.explanation: CLAUDE.md is the standard user-scoped way to add personal guidance, but invoking `/refactor` runs the project skill's body; ambient CLAUDE.md instructions do not replace the steps of an explicitly invoked skill, so the command's behavior is unchanged.

questions.explanation: The scenario imposes two constraints: the teammate's stricter type-checking must actually take effect, and the shared `/refactor` must stay the same for everyone else. B is the only option that satisfies both: a separate command name (e.g. `/refactor-strict`) avoids the name collision entirely, so the personal skill is invoked while the team's `/refactor` is untouched. A makes the stricter rules run but applies them to all teammates, violating the no-impact constraint. C keeps the same `refactor` name, so the personal skill is still not the one `/refactor` invokes regardless of how complete it is. D uses a real personalization channel (CLAUDE.md) but ambient guidance does not override the body of an explicitly invoked skill, so the command's behavior does not change.

questions.topic: Multi-Agent Orchestration: subagent scope vs. coordinator decisions
questions.text: In a research run, the web search subagent's top-ranked, on-topic hit for a market-sizing query is a polished microsite whose figures could swing the report's conclusions. On closer inspection it is published by an industry lobbying group and reads as sponsored advocacy dressed up as neutral analysis. The figures themselves are specific and internally consistent; what is in doubt is the source's independence. How should the subagent return this result?

options.label: A
options.text: Score the page's provenance as low-trust, attach that reduced confidence weight to the result, and report the downgrade so the coordinator can see the page was discounted for advocacy bias.
options.explanation: A is tempting because search subagents already return ranked, scored results, so quantifying a trust signal feels like adding information for the coordinator. But folding the subagent's own independence judgment into the result's weight makes a cross-source trust call locally and bakes it into the data the coordinator inherits; the coordinator receives a pre-discounted figure rather than the raw provenance facts it needs to weigh that page against every other source in the run.

options.label: B
options.text: Run a follow-up query to find an independent source that corroborates the same figures, then cite that source for the numbers and report that the original hit was advocacy content.
options.explanation: B is tempting because the figures are usable, so anchoring them to a clean citation looks strictly safer than relying on a lobbying microsite. But a second source agreeing on a number speaks to whether the figure is plausible, not to whether the original source is independent, and re-citing replaces a provenance question with a corroboration one. The coordinator loses the trail it would use to judge how much the advocacy origin should matter to the conclusion.

options.label: C
options.text: Return the page among its other results with the figures intact, tagged with its source and a note that it appears to be sponsored advocacy rather than independent analysis, and leave the keep-or-discard call to the coordinator.
options.explanation: Correct: the subagent's job is to deliver retrieved material with its provenance attached, not to settle source trust. Returning the page whole, with figures preserved and the advocacy concern flagged, gives the coordinator the complete artifact and the context to weigh this source's independence against the rest of the run. C is the only option that hands the cross-source trust decision up without first altering, substituting, or withholding the underlying data.

options.label: D
options.text: Return the rest of the results now but hold this page back, asking the coordinator to rule on whether it is admissible before including it, so no advocacy figures enter the report until they are cleared.
options.explanation: D is tempting because gating questionable data behind an explicit ruling reads as a fail-safe against tainted figures reaching the report. But withholding the page deprives the coordinator of the very thing it needs to rule on (the figures and the provenance), and nothing downstream consumes the result before the coordinator decides anyway, so the gate adds a round-trip without adding protection. The coordinator can adjudicate just as well with the flagged page in hand.

questions.explanation: The concept is separation of concerns in a multi-agent pipeline: when a subagent encounters a decision beyond its scope (here, whether a source is independent enough to trust), it should deliver its retrieved material whole, with provenance attached, and let the coordinator make the cross-cutting call. Every option here surfaces the concern to the coordinator, so the discriminator is not who decides but what the coordinator receives. C returns the page with figures intact and the advocacy concern flagged, preserving full fidelity so the coordinator can weigh this source against the rest of the run. A bakes the subagent's own trust judgment into the result as a confidence weight, handing the coordinator pre-discounted data instead of raw provenance. B substitutes a corroborating source, which answers whether the figures are plausible rather than whether the original is independent, and loses the provenance trail. D withholds the page pending a ruling, depriving the coordinator of the data it needs to rule and adding a round-trip that protects nothing, since nothing acts on the result before the decision. Correct: C.

questions.text: A weekly trace review shows the agent fires `process_refund` on roughly 1 in 6 borderline messages like "this charge looks wrong and I'm pretty upset about it" — cases policy says should go to `escalate_to_human`. Clear-cut refund requests and clear-cut escalation requests both route correctly, including angry customers who clearly do want their money back. The team has pulled the exact borderline transcripts that misrouted. Which change most reliably cuts these misroutes?

options.label: A
options.text: Expand both tool descriptions with explicit when-to-use boundaries, input formats, and example phrasings that separate a refund request from a dispute that needs a human.
options.explanation: Tempting because sharpening tool descriptions is the standard first move when an agent confuses two similar tools, and it scales without per-case work. But these descriptions are not what is failing: the clear-cut cases already route correctly, so the model understands what each tool is for. The gap is on inputs that genuinely read either way, where the deciding factor is this org's policy on charge disputes — a judgment the model has to see applied, not a capability the description can state in the abstract.

options.label: B
options.text: Add a tie-break rule to the system prompt: when a message disputes a charge and expresses dissatisfaction, prefer `escalate_to_human` over `process_refund`.
options.explanation: Tempting because it encodes the policy directly and is cheap to add. But the rule fires on surface signals — disputed charge plus dissatisfaction — that also describe the upset-but-valid refund cases currently routing correctly. A blanket prefer-escalate rule pulls those clear-cut refunds toward the human queue, trading one misroute for another instead of teaching the model where the actual line sits.

options.label: C
options.text: Require the agent to run a policy-eligibility check before `process_refund` can execute, so a borderline message is held back from an automatic refund until that check clears.
options.explanation: Tempting because a hard precondition feels more dependable than coaxing the model, and it puts a real gate in front of the costly action. But the eligibility check still has to judge whether a borderline, plausible-either-way message qualifies — it relocates the same ambiguous refund-versus-escalate decision into a new step rather than resolving it. The case that was hard to route is exactly the case the gate now has to adjudicate.

options.label: D
options.text: Add the captured borderline transcripts as in-context examples, each annotated with the policy signal that decides the call between the two tools.
options.explanation: Correct: the misroutes cluster on these specific ambiguous messages, and the captured transcripts are the cases the model is actually getting wrong. Pairing each with the signal that settles it shows the model how to apply this org's policy to the exact inputs it stumbles on, which is what fixes a discrimination failure on plausible-either-way messages — at the cost of a handful of examples.

questions.explanation: D is the best answer because the failures are concentrated on specific borderline messages the team has already captured, and turning those exact cases into annotated in-context examples teaches the model how this org's policy resolves the refund-versus-escalate call on the inputs it keeps missing. A sharpens the tool descriptions, but the clear-cut cases already route correctly, so the model understands the tools — the gap is policy judgment on ambiguous inputs, which a description states only in the abstract. B adds a prefer-escalate tie-break rule, but its surface trigger (disputed charge plus dissatisfaction) also matches the upset-but-valid refunds that route correctly today, so it would start misrouting those. C gates `process_refund` behind an eligibility check, which only relocates the same ambiguous judgment into a new step rather than resolving it. D alone uses the captured failing cases to teach the discrimination directly.

questions.text: A coding agent on your team drafts a plain-language review summary for each pull request from the diff and the CI output it pulled. Over the last 50 PRs the summaries are never factually wrong, yet engineers reopened 18 of them because something material was left unsaid — and it was a different gap nearly every time: one summary never mentioned a changed public API, another skipped the drop in test coverage, a third said nothing about a newly pinned third-party dependency. You want to bring the reopen rate down without putting a person back in the loop on every PR. Which approach is most effective?

options.label: A
options.text: Have the agent first write out, from this diff, what a complete review of this particular change would need to address, then check its draft against that list and add whatever it skipped.
options.explanation: Correct. The agent derives the coverage criteria from the specific diff in front of it, then grades its own draft against them. Because the omitted item differs from PR to PR, criteria regenerated per diff catch whichever material point this change introduces — a perf regression, a migration, a config flip — where any pre-set list eventually meets a category it does not contain. No human is needed on each PR.

options.label: B
options.text: Give the agent a review rubric in its prompt naming the dimensions a strong PR summary should cover — API surface, test-coverage impact, new dependencies — and have it confirm each one before posting.
options.explanation: Wrong. A standing rubric makes the agent self-check, which is the right shape, but it locks coverage to the dimensions you wrote down — drawn from the gaps already seen. The next reopen is the category the rubric never anticipated, so it confirms three boxes while the novel omission passes through unchecked.

options.label: C
options.text: Feed the agent the reopen comments engineers left on past PRs and have it self-check each new draft for the kinds of things reviewers said were missing before posting.
options.explanation: Wrong. Learning from real reviewer feedback is a sensible signal, but the reopens were a different gap nearly every time, so the historical comments describe a backward-looking sample, not the long tail. The agent guards against last month's omissions while this PR's unprecedented gap slips by.

options.label: D
options.text: Have the agent score its own draft for completeness on a 1–5 scale and rewrite any summary that falls below a set threshold before it posts.
options.explanation: Wrong. A self-assigned completeness score keeps a human out of the loop, but a single number tells the agent that something is thin without telling it what is absent. A blind rewrite resamples the same draft and tends to surface a different incomplete version rather than locating and repairing the specific missing point.

questions.explanation: A is correct: deriving the coverage criteria from each individual diff and grading the draft against them is the only approach that adapts to a different missing point every time, with no human per PR. B has the agent self-check too, but against a fixed rubric built from gaps already seen, so a new category escapes it. C self-checks against past reviewer comments, which sample backward and miss the long tail of novel omissions. D self-scores completeness as a scalar that flags thinness without localizing what is missing, so its rewrite just resamples instead of repairing the specific gap.

questions.text: Your support resolution agent uses rolling-window compaction: once a conversation passes 50 turns, the oldest turns are compressed into a running recap while the last several turns stay verbatim. A billing-dispute review surfaces a recurring failure: in long sessions the agent quotes a refund figure that contradicts the one it negotiated with the customer earlier (e.g., it confirms "$240" around turn 8, then near turn 60 tells the customer "$190" and calls process_refund for that wrong amount). The dispute case in the backend tracks status and order ID but not the agreed amount—that number was settled live in the chat and, by the time the error occurs, has been folded into a recap line reading "agreed on a partial refund." What is the most effective fix?

options.label: A
options.text: Add a precondition to process_refund that re-derives the agreed amount from the conversation and rejects the call when the requested figure disagrees with it.
options.explanation: A deterministic guard on anything that moves money is a sound instinct, and blocking a mismatched payout is exactly what you want at the last gate. The catch is what it checks against: the only place the agreed amount lives is the conversation, which is the lossy source already corrupted by compaction, so the guard re-reads the same drifted figure and waves the call through. It also fires only on the tool call—the customer was told "$190" earlier in the turn, and that misstatement stands.

options.label: B
options.text: When the amount is agreed, write it onto the backend dispute case alongside status and order ID, and prepend that case record to the context on every subsequent turn.
options.explanation: Correct. The agreed figure is load-bearing for both what the agent says and the process_refund argument, yet it lives only in the chat, so it has to be promoted to the backend case the moment it is settled—before compaction can touch it—and re-injected from there each turn. That makes the dispute case the source of truth: one authoritative value drives the customer-facing reply and the tool call alike, no matter how the older turns are summarized.

options.label: C
options.text: Mark the turn where the amount is agreed as pinned so it is carried forward verbatim and exempted from compaction even after the window rolls past it.
options.explanation: Pinning the committing turn keeps the exact words around deterministically, which is why it is tempting. But it only adds the correct figure back to the transcript; it does not remove the later turns where the agent restated "$190," so both numbers sit in context as ordinary prose and attention can still land on the wrong one—the exact failure reported. It also leaves the value as a line of conversation rather than an authoritative field, so a renegotiation produces two pinned turns with competing figures and no signal for which governs.

options.label: D
options.text: Replace the free-text recap with a typed summary template that has dedicated slots for monetary amounts, dates, and identifiers, so each recap carries those values in fixed fields.
options.explanation: A structured summary schema is a genuine best practice and makes the recap far more legible than free prose. But the value still has to be transcribed into the slot by the same probabilistic compaction step, and the slot is re-derived each time the window rolls, so the figure can still be altered or dropped on a later pass. It improves the format of the lossy path instead of routing the critical fact around it.

questions.explanation: B is correct: the agreed figure governs both the agent's reply and the process_refund argument and lives only in the chat, so it must be promoted to the backend dispute case the moment it is settled—before compaction touches it—and re-injected each turn, making that case the single source of truth behind both the statement and the call. A puts a deterministic guard on the payout but has nothing trustworthy to check against: it re-derives the amount from the same lossy conversation, and it cannot unsay the wrong figure already quoted to the customer. C only re-adds the correct turn to the transcript without removing the later "$190" restatements, so the competing number still sits in context where attention can drift to it. D upgrades the recap to typed slots, yet the value is still transcribed and re-derived by the lossy compaction step on every pass rather than kept outside it.

questions.text: Production traces show the web-search subagent hitting a cited URL that returns a 404, and re-checks confirm the page is gone for good rather than briefly unavailable. The subagent has gathered other findings on the same task and is about to hand its results back. How should it handle this dead source so the rest of the pipeline behaves correctly?

options.label: A
options.text: Run a fresh search for another source that covers the same claim, swap it in for the dead URL, and hand back a complete set of findings.
options.explanation: The subagent is the search specialist, so recovering the claim itself looks like the efficient move. But silently swapping the backing source changes what evidence underpins the report; whether that specific citation can be substituted is a provenance decision the coordinator owns, not the subagent.

options.label: B
options.text: Emit the 404 and the URL to the error-tracking/observability stream, then return the remaining findings so the task can keep moving.
options.explanation: Logging the failure makes it visible and avoids stalling the run, which feels like sound failure handling. The catch is that this visibility is post-hoc and aimed at humans; the coordinator driving the current task never sees the gap and treats the trimmed findings as complete.

options.label: C
options.text: Mark the source as failed with its 404 status and URL in the structured result it returns, so the coordinator can choose the next step.
options.explanation: Correct: putting the failure in the structured result as a discrete, typed signal gives the coordinator something it can branch on in-loop—drop the source, dispatch a replacement search, or flag the gap—keeping decision ownership where it belongs.

options.label: D
options.text: Return the findings as usual and add a sentence in the prose summary noting that one cited page could not be retrieved.
options.explanation: This does route the gap back to the coordinator and degrades gracefully, which is why it is tempting. But a note buried in free-text findings carries no machine-readable failure signal, so the coordinator can easily read the summary as a finished result and never act on the missing source.

questions.explanation: C is correct: returning the failure as a discrete, structured signal (status plus URL) in the result gives the coordinator something it can branch on while the task is still live, preserving decision ownership at the level that can weigh dropping, replacing, or flagging the source. A has the subagent unilaterally swapping in a different source, changing the report's evidence base on a provenance call that belongs to the coordinator. B makes the failure visible only to humans after the fact while the coordinator sees trimmed findings as complete. D does surface the gap to the coordinator but only as prose, with no actionable signal, so it is likely read as a finished result; C beats it by being machine-readable and decision-ready.

questions.text: Post-deployment review of a scheduling assistant finds that 14% of cancellations remove the wrong meeting. When a user says "cancel my sync with the design team," the agent calls `find_events`, which returns several recurring meetings whose titles all contain "design," and the agent cancels one of them by calling `delete_event`. What is the most effective way to fix this?

options.label: A
options.text: Have the agent re-query `find_events` with added filters such as attendee list and a narrow time window so it resolves the matches down to the single meeting the request points to.
options.explanation: Tighter filters can narrow the set, but the refined query can still return several meetings or none, and the agent then picks among them just as before. It improves the search without ever obtaining the one fact the error rate depends on: which meeting the user meant.

options.label: B
options.text: Before calling `delete_event`, have the agent state the meeting it selected and ask the user to confirm that this is the one to cancel.
options.explanation: Confirming before a destructive call is sound practice, but the agent confirms a single meeting it already chose and never surfaces the other matches. A user who declines has corrected nothing, since the agent still does not know which meeting was meant, and a user who agrees may approve the wrong one without realizing alternatives existed.

options.label: C
options.text: Have the agent ask a clarifying question, such as which design team or which day the meeting falls on, and then run `find_events` again with the user's answer folded into the query before deciding what to cancel.
options.explanation: Asking for more detail and re-running the search sounds thorough, but it sets aside the concrete matches the first call already returned and starts over from a refined query, which can again come back with several meetings or none. The user is made to re-specify blind instead of choosing from the events that actually qualify.

options.label: D
options.text: When `find_events` returns more than one match, have the agent list those matched meetings and have the user pick which one to cancel before calling `delete_event`.
options.explanation: The matches `find_events` already returned are the exact events that could be deleted, so presenting them and letting the user choose maps the decision directly onto the real candidates. This resolves the ambiguity that drives the 14% rate with one selection, rather than guessing or re-searching.

questions.explanation: When a tool returns several valid matches, the reliable fix is to let the user choose among the matches actually returned, since those are the exact events at risk of deletion. D does this: it surfaces the set from `find_events` and maps the user's pick onto a real candidate. A re-queries with more filters but still resolves the result itself and can land back on multiple or zero matches. B confirms only the single meeting the agent already picked and never reveals the alternatives, so a decline leaves the agent no wiser and an approval can still hit the wrong meeting. C asks a clarifying question and re-runs the search, discarding the concrete matches in hand for a fresh query that can again be ambiguous.

questions.text: Run traces show the document-analysis subagent calling the web-search subagent directly to grab its raw findings, then stitching both into a half-formed report that skips the synthesis step entirely. Citations come out duplicated and contradictory. Which change best fixes the architecture?

options.label: A
options.text: Have both gathering agents return their findings to the coordinator, which dispatches them to the synthesis agent before any report is assembled.
options.explanation: Correct. This is the only change that fixes both defects at once: the worker-to-worker call is replaced by results flowing back to the coordinator, and the synthesis stage is reinserted ahead of report assembly, so the synthesis agent reconciles the contradictory and duplicated citations before anything is written up.

options.label: B
options.text: Have the document-analysis agent pass the combined findings straight to the synthesis agent, so the synthesis stage runs before the report is built.
options.explanation: Wrong. This does restore the missing synthesis step, which is tempting because synthesis is the stage actually skipped. But it leaves the agents talking peer-to-peer with no coordinator in the loop, so the workflow is still wired by the workers themselves rather than dispatched centrally, and the same shortcut can reappear elsewhere.

options.label: C
options.text: Add a citation-reconciliation pass that dedupes and resolves conflicting references just before the report is produced.
options.explanation: Wrong. Duplicated and contradictory citations are the visible symptom, so cleaning them up looks like a direct fix. But it leaves the document-analysis agent assembling the report without ever invoking synthesis, so the stage meant to merge and reconcile findings is still absent and only its output is being patched after the fact.

options.label: D
options.text: Route the gathering agents' outputs back through the coordinator, which then forwards them to the report-generation agent.
options.explanation: Wrong. This rightly puts the coordinator back in charge of dispatching results, which is half the problem. But it hands the raw findings to report generation and never reaches the synthesis agent, so the contradictory and duplicated material is still written into the report unreconciled.

questions.explanation: A is correct because it is the only option that repairs both failures in the trace: it sends each gathering agent's results back to the coordinator (ending the worker-to-worker call) and routes them through the synthesis agent before any report is assembled (ending the skipped-synthesis defect). B reinstates synthesis but keeps the agents wiring the flow peer-to-peer with no coordinator dispatching work. C cleans up the duplicated and contradictory citations but only treats that symptom, leaving synthesis skipped and the wrong agent assembling the report. D puts the coordinator back in control but forwards results to report generation, so synthesis is still bypassed and the findings reach the report unreconciled. Only A closes both the broken control flow and the missing synthesis stage.

questions.topic: Error Propagation: graceful degradation with transparency
questions.text: A telemetry dashboard for the multi-agent research system shows that on a run about "regional EV battery supply chains," the web search subagent returned 14 of 20 expected citations; the six it missed were paywalled trade journals (403s) that all covered two narrow subtopics, recycling economics and second-life battery markets, while the rest of the topic is fully backed by the 14 citations and the PDFs the document-analysis subagent parsed. The report generator subagent now assembles the final cited report from this uneven evidence base. Which approach should the report generator subagent take?

options.label: A
options.text: Open the report with a limitations preamble listing the six inaccessible journals and noting that sourcing on those topics is incomplete, then write the body normally.
options.explanation: A single upfront caveat states that coverage is incomplete in general, but it cannot tell a reader working through the recycling-economics section that that section is the thin one while the adjacent sections are fully backed. The gap is localized to specific subtopics, so the disclosure has to be localized too; a global preamble leaves each individual conclusion unqualified.

options.label: B
options.text: Write the report from the 14 citations and the parsed PDFs, attaching a source-confidence note to each section that marks the recycling and second-life sections as resting on partial sourcing.
options.explanation: The missing journals cluster on two subtopics, so the evidence is uneven section by section rather than across the whole report. Attaching the confidence note to each section maps the disclosure onto exactly the claims it qualifies, letting the coordinator and readers tell the well-supported conclusions from the thin ones instead of discounting the entire report.

options.label: C
options.text: Soften the wording throughout the report to reflect lower overall confidence, qualifying claims so the prose never overstates what the partial evidence supports.
options.explanation: Hedging the whole report is honest about uncertainty but spreads it uniformly, so the strongly sourced sections read as tentative as the thin ones. Vague qualifiers across the prose cannot be mapped back to which specific conclusions lack the six journals, which is less actionable than a discrete per-section marker for the affected subtopics.

options.label: D
options.text: Return control to the coordinator to re-attempt the six paywalled sources before writing, so the report can be assembled from the full evidence base.
options.explanation: Surfacing a gap to the coordinator is usually right, but the 403s are deterministic paywall responses that a re-fetch will hit again, and the coordinator already sees the 14-of-20 shortfall on the same telemetry dashboard. Blocking the deliverable on a retry that cannot succeed stalls the report without adding any evidence.

questions.explanation: B is correct: because the six missing journals cluster on the recycling and second-life subtopics, the evidence base is uneven section by section, so the confidence disclosure has to be attached to the specific sections it qualifies; that lets downstream consumers tell solid conclusions from thinly sourced ones. A discloses the gap honestly but with a single global preamble that cannot localize which conclusions are thin. C is also honest but hedges the entire report uniformly, dragging down the well-sourced sections and giving readers no way to map the uncertainty to the affected subtopics. D surfaces to the coordinator, which is normally sound, but the 403 paywalls are deterministic so a re-fetch is futile, and the coordinator already sees the shortfall on the dashboard, so it only stalls the deliverable.

questions.text: A trace review of your parallel research system shows that on the brief "vendor landscape for edge-inference chips," the market-intel agent and the patents agent each independently dug into the same three subtopics—thermal design, memory bandwidth, and incumbent share—and returned heavily overlapping analysis. Across the last 40 runs, mean tokens per brief rose from 38k to 71k while the count of unique claims reaching synthesis barely moved. What is the most effective way to address this?

options.label: A
options.text: Give both agents a shared scratchpad that records which subtopics are already being worked, and have each agent consult it before picking what to investigate next.
options.explanation: A shared blackboard is a standard coordination pattern, but the two agents are dispatched at the same time and make their subtopic choices before either has written anything to it. At the moment that matters the scratchpad is empty or stale, so both still land on the same three subtopics; the record only reflects the overlap after the fact.

options.label: B
options.text: Sharpen each agent's role prompt so the market-intel agent is told to reason through a demand-and-pricing lens and the patents agent through an IP-filing lens, pushing them toward different angles.
options.explanation: Distinct lenses are worth having, but a lens shapes how an agent treats a subtopic, not which subtopics it selects. Thermal design, memory bandwidth, and incumbent share each have both a pricing angle and a filings angle, so two well-differentiated agents can still converge on the same three slices and produce parallel analyses of each.

options.label: C
options.text: Have the lead agent hand each subagent an explicit list of subtopics to cover in its dispatch instructions, with no subtopic appearing on more than one agent's list.
options.explanation: Correct. The overlap is set the instant both agents choose subtopics on their own; assigning disjoint lists at dispatch removes the chance to collide, so each subagent owns a distinct slice and the parallel runs add breadth rather than repeat it. The token climb tracks duplicated work, and disjoint assignment is what stops the duplication being created.

options.label: D
options.text: Cap each agent at two subtopics per brief so the volume of analysis any single agent can generate—and the tokens it burns—stays bounded.
options.explanation: A per-agent cap does bound how much one agent emits, but nothing stops two capped agents from selecting the same two subtopics, so the overlap survives at smaller size while you have now also shrunk the breadth each agent contributes. It limits the symptom's magnitude without touching the shared-selection cause.

questions.explanation: C is correct: the duplication is created at the moment each agent independently selects subtopics, so the lead agent dividing the space into disjoint per-agent assignments at dispatch is what prevents two agents from ever working the same slice. A's shared scratchpad is read before either agent has written to it, so it records overlap rather than preventing it. B's distinct lenses change how each subtopic is analyzed but not which subtopics are chosen, so both can still pick the same three. D's per-agent cap shrinks each agent's output but lets two agents pick the same slices, leaving the overlap intact while also reducing coverage.

questions.text: The document-analysis subagent processes a batch of three sources for a regulatory-compliance report. A 40-page filing parsed cleanly and yielded 6 relevant clauses; a vendor whitepaper parsed cleanly but contained no sections matching the query; a scanned PDF returned "PDF render failed: corrupt xref table." The coordinator will use whatever the subagent returns to decide its next move on each source. How should the subagent structure the results it returns?

options.label: A
options.text: Return a per-source relevance score alongside the clauses (filing 0.9, whitepaper 0.0, scanned PDF 0.0) so the coordinator can rank sources and drop the ones below a usefulness threshold.
options.explanation: A relevance score is a clean, uniform schema and lets the coordinator threshold sources without reading prose, which is why it is tempting. But a 0.0 means the same thing for the whitepaper, which was fully read and held nothing relevant, and for the scanned PDF, whose contents are still unknown because it never rendered. Those two demand opposite next moves, and a single score cannot tell the coordinator which is which.

options.label: B
options.text: Return the 6 clauses plus a follow-up list naming the sources that produced no usable clauses and should get another pass: the whitepaper and the scanned PDF.
options.explanation: Returning a concrete worklist of what still needs attention is actionable and reads as good orchestration hygiene. The flaw is that the whitepaper does not belong on it: it was fully analyzed and re-running it will keep finding nothing, so putting it in the same queue as the corrupt PDF spends another pass to confirm an answer the subagent already has.

options.label: C
options.text: Forward each source's raw parser output and diagnostic strings verbatim (the extracted clauses, the "no sections matching" message, the "corrupt xref table" error) and let the coordinator interpret them.
options.explanation: Passing the unaltered parser output preserves maximum fidelity and avoids the subagent throwing away anything the coordinator might want, so it looks like the safe choice. But it pushes the failure-versus-empty judgment downstream as free-text parsing, when the subagent already knows which source failed to render and which completed empty; declining to classify forces the coordinator to re-derive a distinction the subagent is best placed to state.

options.label: D
options.text: Label each source by what happened to it, keeping render-failed, parsed-with-no-match, and parsed-with-clauses as distinct outcome types in the returned result.
options.explanation: Separating render-failed from parsed-with-no-match is the distinction that maps onto recovery: the corrupt PDF's content is still unknown and is a candidate for a retry or alternate fetch, while the whitepaper completed and its empty result is a settled finding, not a pending task. Tagging by outcome type carries exactly that split to the coordinator without making it re-derive it.

questions.explanation: D is the best answer among plausible competitors because it carries the one distinction the coordinator's recovery actions hinge on: an access failure (the scanned PDF, whose content is still unknown and is therefore retryable) versus a completed analysis that found nothing (the whitepaper, a settled result). A collapses both of those into an identical 0.0 score, so the coordinator cannot tell the retryable source from the settled-empty one. B is actionable but wrongly enqueues the whitepaper for another pass that will only reconfirm its empty result, treating a finished source as pending work. C preserves every detail but defers the failure-versus-empty classification to the coordinator as text parsing, even though the subagent already knows which source rendered and which did not. Only D propagates the outcome-type distinction itself, which is what lets the coordinator retry the corrupt PDF and accept the whitepaper's empty result.

questions.text: A regression review of the multi-agent research system shows that 38% of requests phrased like "compile the findings into the final write-up" are delegated to the synthesis agent rather than the report-generation agent. The synthesis agent's `compile_summary` tool is described as "compiles findings into a structured summary"; the report-generation agent's `compile_report` tool is described as "compiles findings into a finished report." The synthesis step is meant to run earlier in the pipeline and feed the report agent, but the coordinator treats both tools as candidates for the same request. Which change most reliably stops the misrouting?

options.label: A
options.text: Rename `compile_summary` to `consolidate_research_notes` and re-describe it as "merges raw subagent outputs into an internal working draft handed off to downstream agents."
options.explanation: Correct. The two tools collide because both their names and descriptions read as "compile ... findings," so a request for the final write-up matches either. Re-rooting the synthesis tool's identity, name and description, around its true internal-handoff role removes the shared signal the coordinator was keying on, so "final write-up" requests no longer resolve to it.

options.label: B
options.text: Expand both tool descriptions to spell out their input formats, typical example queries, and an explicit "use this instead of the other tool when…" boundary for each.
options.explanation: This is the canonical description-quality fix and adds real disambiguating context. But it leaves both tool names sharing the `compile_*` verb and both descriptions still opening on "compiles findings"; the coordinator continues to see two compile-findings tools and weigh added boundary prose against an identical-verb name, so the strongest collision signal survives.

options.label: C
options.text: Reorder the pipeline so the synthesis agent always runs before the report agent, and mark `compile_summary` as a non-terminal step that cannot produce a final deliverable.
options.explanation: Enforcing the intended ordering matches how the pipeline is supposed to flow and feels like it constrains the synthesis tool. But the misrouting happens at delegation, where the coordinator picks which tool answers a single request; sequencing the agents does not change which tool a "final write-up" request semantically matches, so the request still lands on synthesis.

options.label: D
options.text: Append example use cases to `compile_report`'s description, such as "use for the client-facing final report," so its description more strongly matches final-write-up phrasing.
options.explanation: Strengthening the winning tool's description is a reasonable, low-effort pull toward the right target. Yet it is one-sided: `compile_summary` still advertises "compiles findings," so it remains an equally plausible match for "compile the findings into the final write-up," and the overlap is narrowed rather than removed.

questions.explanation: A wins because the collision lives in the shared name verb and the shared \"compiles findings\" description; re-rooting the synthesis tool's name and description around its internal-handoff role is the only change that removes the signal both tools share. B genuinely improves both descriptions but keeps both `compile_*` names and the \"compiles findings\" opening, so two compile-findings candidates still compete. C enforces the intended run order, but the misrouting is a per-request selection problem, not a sequencing one, so the request still semantically matches synthesis. D strengthens only the report tool's description, leaving `compile_summary` an equally plausible match for the final-write-up phrasing.

questions.text: A data-query subagent runs read-only SQL against the analytics warehouse to gather figures for reports. Run metrics show its failures split two ways: about 22% are transient (deadlock-victim aborts and connection-pool timeouts that clear on a re-run within seconds), and the rest are permanent (queries that reference a dropped column, hit a missing-table error, or exceed the row-scan quota). Today every failure of either kind is raised to the coordinator, which halts the report run and decides whether to re-issue the query, drop the metric, or abort. The platform team finds the coordinator is now spending most of its turns adjudicating routine re-run decisions. Which architectural change best addresses this?

options.label: A
options.text: Have the coordinator collect the full set of metric queries up front, dispatch them in one pass, and triage the resulting failures together at the end of the run rather than pausing on each one as it occurs.
options.explanation: Batching the triage into a single end-of-run pass does cut the back-and-forth, but it still routes every transient deadlock through the coordinator to adjudicate, and it breaks down when one metric's query depends on a figure an earlier query was supposed to produce. It consolidates the routine decisions rather than removing them from the coordinator.

options.label: B
options.text: Let the subagent re-run the deadlock and pool-timeout failures on its open connection, and pass the permanent failures up to the coordinator with the failing query, the error class, and the figures already gathered.
options.explanation: Correct. The transient class is exactly what clears on a quick re-run, and the subagent already holds the warm connection and in-flight context to retry just the failed statement in seconds, so those routine decisions never reach the coordinator. Only the genuinely unrecoverable failures go up, carrying enough detail for the coordinator to decide knowingly.

options.label: C
options.text: Wrap the subagent invocation in a coordinator-side retry policy keyed to the returned SQL error code: bounded retries for deadlock and timeout codes, immediate escalation for the rest.
options.explanation: Keying retries to the error code captures the real transient-vs-permanent split deterministically, which is appealing. But retrying at the coordinator boundary re-invokes the whole subagent from a cold start—new connection, re-established context—forfeiting the cheap warm re-run the transient errors are designed to clear with, and adds the round-trip cost back for every transient case.

options.label: D
options.text: Extend the subagent's return schema so each failure is tagged retryable or permanent with a suggested action, and have the coordinator apply that tag to decide retry-or-drop per failure.
options.explanation: A structured, self-describing failure return is sound practice and makes each coordinator decision faster. The trouble is the stem's complaint is the volume of routine decisions, not their per-decision cost: every transient failure still lands on the coordinator to act on, so the turn-by-turn adjudication load it is meant to relieve largely remains.

questions.explanation: B is correct: the transient deadlock and pool-timeout failures are precisely the ones that clear on a quick re-run, and the subagent holds the warm connection and live context to retry the failed statement in seconds—so those routine re-runs never consume a coordinator turn—while permanent failures travel up with the query, error class, and partial figures the coordinator needs to decide. A batches the triage but still adjudicates every transient at the coordinator and breaks on cross-metric dependencies. C encodes the right error-class split but retries at the coordinator boundary, cold-starting the whole subagent and losing the cheap warm re-run plus paying the round-trip again per transient. D adds a clean structured failure return yet still sends every transient to the coordinator to act on, lowering per-decision cost but not the volume of decisions that is the actual problem.

questions.text: The report generation agent has a `write_file(path, content)` tool so it can save finished reports. A trace audit of last week's runs shows it occasionally writing intermediate drafts and reasoning notes into whatever path it improvised mid-task -- including the shared cache the coordinator reads and the web search agent's input folder -- overwriting files later stages depend on. The reports themselves are the only output the system keeps; everything else is throwaway working text. What is the most effective fix?

options.label: A
options.text: Have the report agent return finished report text to the coordinator and let the coordinator perform the actual disk write, removing `write_file` from the agent entirely.
options.explanation: A smart engineer picks this because centralizing writes in the trusted coordinator is a clean way to keep an agent from touching anything it shouldn't, and it follows the surface-the-output-to-the-controller pattern. It loses to C because routing every report through the coordinator serializes a high-frequency operation behind it and adds a round trip for the one thing the agent does constantly, when the goal is simply to bound where the agent can write -- not to take writing away from it.

options.label: B
options.text: Keep `write_file` and prepend a system-prompt section telling the agent that drafts and notes are not artifacts and that it should only call `write_file` for a final report destined for the report directory.
options.explanation: A smart engineer picks this because the audit shows the agent confusing scratch text with real output, and clarifying that distinction in the prompt looks like fixing the actual confusion at low cost. It loses to C because the tool still accepts any path, so compliance is left to the model -- one stray call during a long run re-corrupts the shared cache, and a guarantee about a shared resource cannot rest on probabilistic instruction-following.

options.label: C
options.text: Replace `write_file` with a `save_report` tool that takes only a filename and the report content and writes into a destination the tool sets internally.
options.explanation: Correct. The agent has exactly one thing worth persisting -- the finished report -- and the corruption comes from the tool exposing a path at all. A `save_report` tool that owns its destination and never accepts a path makes writing to the cache or another agent's folder unexpressible, so the misdirected drafts have nowhere to land and the only legitimate write still works.

options.label: D
options.text: Keep `write_file` but add a precondition that rejects any path resolving outside the report agent's own working tree, so it can still write freely within its sandbox.
options.explanation: A smart engineer picks this because confining the agent to its own subtree is a recognizable least-privilege move and lets the agent keep writing drafts wherever it likes inside that boundary. It loses to C because the agent's working tree is broader than the report directory and the cache it corrupts is one it legitimately reads from; a path parameter that still resolves across that read/write surface leaves the same overwrite vector open, whereas C removes the path entirely.

questions.explanation: The concept is scoping a tool to the one capability the agent actually needs. The agent's only durable output is the finished report; the corruption stems from `write_file` accepting an arbitrary path, which lets misdirected scratch writes land in the shared cache and another agent's folder. C is best: a `save_report` tool that owns its destination and takes no path makes out-of-scope writes inexpressible while still covering the single legitimate need. A removes writing from the agent and serializes a constant operation behind the coordinator. B leaves the path open and depends on the model never misfiring on a shared resource. D scopes by sandbox rather than by capability, but the agent's working tree is wider than the report directory and overlaps the cache it reads, so the same overwrite path persists; C wins by removing the path parameter outright.

questions.text: Your on-call incident-triage agent ingests one aggregated 90K-token event timeline (the opening pager alerts, then the chronological remediation actions operators ran, then the final all-clear and resolution notes) and drafts the postmortem. Across the last month of incidents, the drafts reliably capture the initial alerts and the closing resolution, but in about 1 in 4 reviews an engineer flags that a mitigation step taken partway through the timeline -- present verbatim in the input -- never appears in the postmortem. How should you reorganize the aggregated timeline to stop these mid-timeline steps from being dropped?

options.label: A
options.text: Wrap each remediation action in a labeled section delimiter (e.g. <step id="7" phase="mitigation">...</step>) so every mitigation action is individually addressable inside the timeline.
options.explanation: Incorrect. Delimiting and labeling content is a sound organization technique and makes steps easier to reference, which is why it is tempting. But tagging changes the formatting, not the ordinal position of the mitigation actions; the labeled steps still sit in the under-attended middle of the 90K-token span, so the same positional drop recurs even though each step now has an id.

options.label: B
options.text: Split the timeline into phase-sized segments (alerts, remediation, resolution), draft a section for each segment independently, then concatenate the sections into the final postmortem.
options.explanation: Incorrect. Chunking shrinks each context so no single pass has a long middle, which is a reasonable instinct. But drafting the remediation segment in isolation severs the cross-phase causal thread a postmortem depends on, and that segment is itself the longest one -- its own interior steps remain in a middle position, so the dropped step can still be lost while you have added a stitching pass.

options.label: C
options.text: Run a first pass that compresses the full 90K-token timeline into a roughly 8K-token summary, then have the agent draft the postmortem from the compressed version.
options.explanation: Incorrect. Reducing the input to a well-attended size sounds like it removes the conditions for the drop, which is the appeal. But the compression pass reads the same long timeline and inherits the same positional bias, so it tends to omit the mid-timeline mitigation step before the drafting pass ever sees it -- the loss simply moves upstream into the summarizer.

options.label: D
options.text: Lead the timeline with a digest of the mitigation steps operators ran and repeat that digest at the close, keeping the full chronological body between them.
options.explanation: Correct. The drops follow position, not content: material at the start and end of a long context is recalled reliably while the middle is under-weighted. Placing the mitigation steps in both the opening and closing regions puts the at-risk content into the two positions the model attends to most, so the steps are surfaced regardless of where they fall in the chronological body.

questions.explanation: The omissions track position rather than content: content at the beginning and end of a long context is recalled reliably while middle content is under-attended, which is why the mid-timeline steps disappear despite being present in the input. D moves the at-risk mitigation steps into both the opening and closing regions, the two best-attended positions, so they surface no matter where they sit chronologically. A labels the steps but leaves them in the same middle position, so the drop recurs. B chunks by phase but isolates the remediation segment from the rest of the incident's causal narrative, and that long segment keeps its own interior middle where the step can still be lost. C compresses first, but the summarizer reads the same long timeline and inherits the identical positional bias, dropping the step before drafting begins. Correct answer: D.

questions.text: A CI step uses Claude Code to generate integration tests for each new endpoint and, in the same run, to judge whether those tests adequately cover the endpoint before merge; the judging output reuses the generation transcript. Last sprint, 9 endpoints shipped with tests rated "comprehensive," but four failed in staging on input combinations that fall straight out of each endpoint's contract — declared parameter ranges, optional-field interactions, and documented error responses. Those same combinations appear in Claude's generation notes, flagged as "edge cases worth covering" and then dropped with a one-line justification. Which change most directly removes the cause of the missed gaps?

options.label: A
options.text: Run the coverage judgment as a fresh Claude invocation whose inputs are the endpoint contract and the generated test file, with the generation transcript excluded.
options.explanation: Correct. The required combinations are derivable from the contract itself (declared ranges, optional-field interactions, error responses), so a judge that re-reads the contract from scratch reconstructs what must be covered and notices the tests do not exercise it. Because it never sees the generation notes, it cannot inherit the one-line justifications the generator used to drop those cases, so it grades against the spec rather than re-confirming the generator's conclusions.

options.label: B
options.text: Run the coverage judgment as a fresh Claude invocation, supplying it the contract, the test file, and the generation notes so it can confirm each flagged edge case was actually handled.
options.explanation: Tempting because handing the reviewer the generation notes looks like the more thorough cross-check: it can walk the flagged list item by item. But the notes are exactly where each dropped case carries its rationalization, so the new instance reads those justifications and is led to accept the same omissions. Separating the instance does not help when the contaminating reasoning travels with the input.

options.label: C
options.text: Keep the judgment in the same run but add an explicit coverage rubric that enumerates the required input combinations from the contract and forces a covered/uncovered mark for each before the comprehensive rating is allowed.
options.explanation: A rubric that pins coverage to concrete, enumerated criteria is the standard fix for inconsistent ratings, so this looks like the disciplined choice. Here, though, the grader is still the same context that already enumerated these very combinations in its notes and rated the tests comprehensive anyway; the justifications for skipping them are present, so the rubric is marked against reasoning that has already excused the gaps. The problem is contaminated self-review of known cases, not vague criteria.

options.label: D
options.text: Generate the test suite twice from independent invocations and merge the two outputs, so combinations one pass omits the other pass is likely to include.
options.explanation: Ensemble diversity is a real way to catch blind spots that any single generation misses. But the notes show these cases were not missed for lack of ideas — they were identified and deliberately deprioritized — and the merge still has no independent gate, so both passes can apply the same reasoning and skip them. This diversifies generation while leaving the unreliable self-judgment in place.

questions.explanation: A is correct: the cases that failed are derivable from the endpoint contract, so a fresh judge given only the contract and the tests reconstructs the required coverage and flags the omissions, without inheriting the generator's justifications for dropping them. B fails because giving the fresh judge the generation notes re-imports those justifications, so it confirms the same omissions despite running separately. C fails because the rubric is still scored by the same context that already enumerated and excused these combinations; the disease here is contaminated self-review of known cases, not ambiguous criteria. D fails because the notes prove the cases were known and deliberately skipped, so diversifying generation does not address the missing independent gate.

questions.text: Your CI pipeline has Claude triage each pull-request comment into "blocker", "warning", or "nit", and the kinds of findings keep growing as new linters and reviewers feed in. Auditing last week's runs, you see an unhandled promise rejection tagged "blocker" on one PR and "nit" on another, and a few other findings split the same way, so teams no longer trust which comments gate a merge. What is the most effective way to make the label assignments consistent across PRs?

options.label: A
options.text: Add a few-shot block to the prompt with one labeled finding for each of the three tiers, drawn from comments the team agrees were tagged correctly.
options.explanation: Few-shot exemplars are the canonical fix for variable output, so this is a natural first reach. But three exemplars only anchor findings that resemble them; a finding unlike any of the three, or one sitting near the blocker/warning line, has no exemplar to match, so it keeps drifting. With the finding space growing, the long tail the examples don't cover is exactly where the inconsistency lives.

options.label: B
options.text: Spell out in the prompt what separates blocker from warning from nit, pinning each boundary with a borderline finding that shows which side it lands on.
options.explanation: The drift comes from the model redeciding the tier boundaries on each PR. Describing what distinguishes the tiers and fixing the borderline cases gives a rule that resolves not just the findings you have seen but new ones the team has not, so an unhandled promise rejection lands on the same tier wherever it appears. This is the only option that supplies the reference points the other approaches assume already exist.

options.label: C
options.text: Tell Claude that whenever a finding could reasonably fit more than one tier, it should pick the most severe tier that plausibly applies to it.
options.explanation: A precedence rule reads like a direct hit on the symptom—it explicitly handles the case where a finding looks like it could be either tier. But it only adjudicates between tiers the model has already decided are candidates; it never says what makes a promise rejection blocker-eligible in the first place. If the model judges it a nit on one PR, the tie-break never fires, so the cross-PR drift continues underneath it.

options.label: D
options.text: Pass a registry of how previous comments across past PRs were labeled into the prompt, so each new finding is tagged to match how similar findings were handled before.
options.explanation: Matching new findings to precedent seems to attack the problem head-on, since the complaint is that equivalent findings got different labels. But the precedent comes from the same audited runs that already labeled the promise rejection both 'blocker' and 'nit'—the history is internally contradictory, so it offers no single answer to match, and a genuinely new finding has no precedent at all. It encodes the inconsistency rather than resolving it.

questions.explanation: B is correct: the labels drift because the model re-draws the tier boundaries on each PR, and stating what distinguishes blocker, warning, and nit—with borderline findings fixing where each boundary falls—gives a rule that generalizes to equivalent and to never-seen findings, so the same finding lands the same way across PRs. A (per-tier few-shot examples) anchors only findings that resemble the three exemplars, leaving the growing long tail and the boundary cases undefined. C (most-severe-tier tie-break) only chooses among tiers the model has already deemed candidates and never establishes what makes a finding eligible for a tier, so a finding judged a nit on one PR never triggers it. D (precedent registry) draws on the very audited runs that contain the contradictory labels, so it has no single precedent to match and nothing for a new finding—propagating the inconsistency instead of fixing it.

questions.text: Your CI job runs the Claude Code CLI in `--print` mode to generate unit tests for changed files, and the suggestions are solid. But the pipeline's pass/fail gate consumes JUnit XML, and right now Claude returns a prose report ("Test A covers the happy path and passes; Test B for the empty-input case fails..."). A hand-written extractor that parses that narrative mis-reads about 1 in 8 runs, so the gate sometimes goes green on a build that should be red. The gate needs each generated test as a discrete record carrying its identifier, status, and failure message. What is the most effective approach?

options.label: A
options.text: Switch the CLI to `--output-format json` and have the gate read the test records out of the `result` field instead of from raw stdout.
options.explanation: `--output-format json` is the natural first reach for structured CLI output, but it only wraps the run in a JSON envelope (session id, cost, the model's reply under `result`). The reply itself is still the same prose report, so the gate is back to parsing narrative out of `result` and inherits the exact mis-read rate the extractor already has.

options.label: B
options.text: Add a second `--print` call that feeds the prose report back to Claude and asks it to return the per-test records as JSON for the gate to read.
options.explanation: Using a model pass to convert the report is more robust to phrasing than regex and feels like the modern fix, but it adds a second probabilistic step that still reads free-form prose. It is a more capable version of the flaky extractor, not a removal of it, and can drop or mislabel a record on any given run.

options.label: C
options.text: Pass `--json-schema` describing the per-test record to the generation run, then map the returned objects to JUnit XML.
options.explanation: Constraining the run with `--json-schema` makes Claude emit objects in the record shape the gate expects rather than a paragraph, so converting to JUnit XML becomes a direct field-to-field mapping. The 1-in-8 failure came from parsing narrative; removing the narrative step is what removes that failure, leaving the test verdicts Claude reports to flow straight through.

options.label: D
options.text: Keep the prose output but add worked examples to CLAUDE.md showing each test rendered as an id/status/message record, so the model produces that shape from project context.
options.explanation: CLAUDE.md examples nudge the model toward the desired layout and are cheap to add, but they shape output only probabilistically. At a failure cost that flips the gate you need the record present and well-formed on every run, which context examples do not enforce when the model paraphrases or omits a field.

questions.explanation: C is best: `--json-schema` constrains the generation run to emit each test as a structured object in the record shape the gate needs, so the JUnit conversion is a mechanical field mapping and the narrative-parsing step that caused the 1-in-8 mis-reads is gone. A (`--output-format json`) only adds an envelope; the prose still sits inside `result`, so the gate keeps parsing narrative and inherits the same failure rate. B converts with a second model call, which is robust to phrasing but is still a probabilistic pass over prose, i.e. a fancier extractor that can still drop a record. D relies on CLAUDE.md examples, which influence output only probabilistically and cannot guarantee every record is present and parseable on each run.

questions.text: Your CI bot uses Claude to triage performance concerns on pull requests, instructed to "flag changes that introduce significant slowdowns." Across last sprint's 90 merged PRs, the same kind of change was labeled a blocker on one PR and waved through on another, and reviewers have stopped trusting the severity field. What change addresses the root cause of this inconsistent triage?

options.label: A
options.text: Have Claude rank every change in a PR by its likely latency impact and flag the top 20% as blockers, giving the severity field a precise, repeatable rule instead of a one-word judgment call.
options.explanation: Swapping a vague word for a crisp, deterministic rule is exactly what looks like the fix here. But ranking changes against the other changes in the same PR makes the cutoff move with each PR's contents: the same change lands in the top 20% of a quiet PR and below it in a busy one, so the identical-change-flips problem the scenario reports survives intact.

options.label: B
options.text: Build an eval set from last sprint's 90 triaged PRs, then tune the triage prompt until Claude's blocker/non-blocker calls match how those PRs were actually decided.
options.explanation: Grounding the prompt in real labeled data is the textbook way to make a classifier reliable, which is why this is tempting. The trap is that the 90 PRs are the inconsistent decisions described in the scenario, so tuning to reproduce them teaches Claude the contradictory bar rather than a stable one.

options.label: C
options.text: Run the perf benchmark suite in CI and pass Claude the measured before/after latency delta for each PR, instructing it to flag changes that meaningfully degrade timing now that it has real numbers to reason over.
options.explanation: Replacing guessed impact with measured deltas reads as the most rigorous option, and it does remove one source of error. But it still leaves Claude to decide what counts as meaningfully degrading on each run, so even with accurate numbers the cutoff drifts PR to PR and the same delta can be called a blocker once and waved through the next time.

options.label: D
options.text: Set a fixed added-latency budget on named hot paths and have Claude judge each diff against that budget, so a change blocks or passes on its own measured cost, not on the PR it rides in.
options.explanation: A fixed budget tied to specific paths gives the verdict an external reference that does not move with each PR's other contents or with past calls. The same change is measured against the same bar every time, so it blocks or passes the same way regardless of which PR carries it.

questions.explanation: The label flips because the bar a change is judged against keeps moving from PR to PR, so the fix has to anchor the verdict to something fixed and external to the PR. D does that: a set added-latency budget on named hot paths means each diff is measured against the same threshold every time, so the same change blocks or passes consistently. A makes the rule precise but PR-relative—ranking within each PR makes the cutoff depend on the other changes present, so identical changes still flip. B tunes the prompt to reproduce last sprint's calls, but those calls are the inconsistent ones, so it bakes the contradiction in. C adds real measurements yet still asks Claude to decide what "meaningfully" means per run, leaving the cutoff to drift even with accurate numbers.

questions.text: Your CI/CD cost report flags the nightly `release-notes-draft` job as the single largest Claude line item: it summarizes every merged PR, and the report also lists several other smaller Claude jobs. The nightly run starts around 06:00 and its output is read aloud at the 08:00 release-readiness standup, which is the sole sign-off gate before deploy: the team walks each PR's notes live, signs off once, and ships — there is no re-review later, so any PR whose notes aren't on screen at 08:00 ships without ever being reviewed. You want to capture the Message Batches API's 50% discount (asynchronous, results retrieved by polling, typically finishes within an hour but carries no completion-time guarantee and may take up to ~24h). How do you proceed?

options.label: A
