options.text: Keep `release-notes-draft` synchronous, and chase the 50% discount on the other batchable jobs in the report instead.
options.explanation: Correct. This job feeds a one-shot sign-off gate at a fixed 08:00 instant, and the discount is purchased by accepting up-to-24h, no-guarantee delivery — a tail a 06:00 batch can land well after 08:00, sending an unreviewed PR to deploy. So it stays synchronous, and the savings come from the report's other jobs that can actually tolerate the asynchrony — cutting cost without betting the review gate.

options.label: B
options.text: Submit it as a batch — it runs unattended overnight with nobody blocked on it, the profile batching is built for.
options.explanation: Tempting because "unattended, nobody waiting" is the textbook batch signal, and most batches do finish within the hour. But this output is consumed at a fixed 08:00 gate, and the API guarantees no completion time — on a slow night the notes sit queued past the standup, which is exactly the failure the discount asks you to accept.

options.label: C
options.text: Batch it for the discount, then at 08:00 post whatever PRs have returned and let stragglers backfill into the notes doc.
options.explanation: Graceful degradation reads well and would work if the notes were a document people re-read later. Here the standup is the lone sign-off gate and runs once; a PR that backfills afterward was never reviewed and has already shipped, so partial-at-08:00 trades an unreviewed deploy for a few dollars of savings.

options.label: D
options.text: Split the run into one batch request per PR so the small jobs clear the queue faster and you still bank the discount.
options.explanation: Smaller units feel like they should clear the queue sooner, but the no-completion-time guarantee is per request, not a function of request size — a single one-PR request can sit just as long as a large one. Fanning out keeps the discount yet leaves every result exposed to the same unbounded tail before the 08:00 gate.

questions.explanation: A is correct: this job feeds a one-shot 08:00 sign-off gate, and the Batches API trades its 50% discount for asynchronous, no-guarantee, up-to-24h delivery — a tail that can leave a PR's notes queued past 08:00, shipping it unreviewed. A keeps this one job synchronous yet still captures the discount on the report's other, deadline-tolerant jobs, so it cuts cost without gambling the review gate. B is the recall trap: \"unattended, nobody blocked\" is the usual batch signal and most batches finish within an hour, but \"most\" is not \"guaranteed,\" and a slow night leaves the notes queued past the cutoff. C's post-partial-then-backfill is real graceful degradation, but the gate runs once and ships once — a PR that backfills later was never reviewed, so this trades an unreviewed deploy for small savings. D assumes per-PR requests return faster, yet the lack of a completion-time guarantee is per request, not a function of request size, so splitting changes cost structure, not the deadline risk.

questions.text: Your Claude-powered PR reviewer groups its findings into named rule packs and posts each as an inline comment. A weekly report tracks the share of comments authors mark "wrong call," and the rate splits sharply by pack: auth-vuln sits at 7%, race-condition at 13%, and resource-leak at 10%, but import-ordering lands at 49% and comment-style at 55%. Sampling the rejected ones shows why: import-ordering keeps flagging orderings the repo's own isort config already accepts, and comment-style keeps flagging docstrings that conform to a house convention the team adopted two sprints ago that these two packs were never updated for. Last Tuesday a maintainer opened a proposal to demote the reviewer to advisory-only, and three teams have already added an editor rule that auto-collapses every comment the bot posts, so its accurate concurrency and security catches are disappearing along with the noise. Which approach best rebuilds confidence in the reviewer while keeping it in the inline review flow?

options.label: A
options.text: Raise the per-finding confidence bar the reviewer must clear before posting on the import-ordering and comment-style packs, and keep tuning each until its wrong-call rate drops to match the trusted packs.
options.explanation: Wrong. The rejected findings are not low-confidence guesses — the packs assert with full confidence against rules that no longer match the repo's isort config and the new docstring convention, so a misfire scores just as high as a real hit. A confidence knob cannot tell a stale-rule false positive from a true catch, so raising the bar mostly suppresses the trusted findings too while the bad rules keep firing during the collapse.

options.label: B
options.text: Stop the import-ordering and comment-style packs from posting, update those two packs to the repo's current isort config and docstring convention offline, then restore them to inline commenting.
options.explanation: Correct. The wrong calls trace to two packs whose rules drifted out of sync with the repo, so pulling exactly those two ends the rejected-comment flood at once and stops the trusted packs from being auto-collapsed as collateral; reconciling the rules with the current config and convention removes the cause rather than the symptom, and restoring them inline keeps every pack in the review flow the stem insists on preserving.

options.label: C
options.text: Move the import-ordering and comment-style findings out of inline comments into a single end-of-review advisory summary, leaving only the trusted packs posting inline.
options.explanation: Wrong. Relocating the two packs is the advisory-only posture the maintainer's proposal threatens and the stem is trying to avert, only applied to half the reviewer; and because the rules are still out of date, roughly half those summary lines remain false positives — now in a digest authors learn to skim — so it hides the misfires instead of reconciling the rules that produce them.

options.label: D
options.text: Have Claude re-read each import-ordering and comment-style finding in a second pass and drop the ones it judges weak before any of them post inline.
options.explanation: Wrong. A model self-review pass is a probabilistic filter over a deterministic defect: the packs are firing on rules that contradict the repo's actual isort config and docstring convention, which the model has no reason to know unless those are fixed first. It keeps the bad findings live during the trust collapse and adds a pass of latency per finding while leaving the stale rules in place.

questions.explanation: B is correct: the high wrong-call rate is concentrated in two packs whose rules fell out of sync with the repo's isort config and a docstring convention adopted two sprints ago, so pulling exactly those two immediately stops the rejected comments driving the auto-collapse, lets the trusted packs keep surfacing real concurrency and security catches, and reconciling the rules offline before restoring them inline removes the cause while honoring the stem's requirement to keep the reviewer in the inline flow. A raises the confidence bar, but a stale rule misfires at full confidence, so a threshold cannot separate a false positive from a true catch and mostly suppresses good findings while the bad rules keep firing. C relocates the two packs to an advisory summary, which concedes the advisory-only demotion the stem wants to avoid and leaves the false positives intact, just skimmed past, without touching the outdated rules. D adds a model self-review pass over rules that contradict the repo's actual config, so the model cannot reliably catch what it was never told changed, and it keeps the misfires live while adding latency.

questions.text: Your CI pipeline asks Claude to generate unit tests for each changed file. The tests pass and catch real regressions, but a quarterly audit flags the suite as hard to maintain: across files the section layout differs, helper and test names follow no shared convention, and assertions are grouped inconsistently—some files bundle several checks per case, others split each into its own. You already rewrote the prompt to spell out the house rules for all three, and the latest run is still a mix: some files come out clean, others drift right back. Which prompting change most reliably makes the generated tests structurally consistent across all three dimensions?

options.label: A
options.text: Move the house rules out of the prompt into a versioned test style guide and reference it from the prompt, giving the model one authoritative spec to consult per file
options.explanation: Centralizing the conventions in a maintained document is something a careful team would do anyway, and a single source of truth feels more durable than rules embedded in a prompt. But relocating the same prose does not change the mechanism: the scenario already shows the model applying written rules unevenly, and a referenced spec is still instruction the model interprets file by file.

options.label: B
options.text: Retrieve a handful of the repository's own passing tests at prompt time and include them so the model patterns its output on tests the team already ships
options.explanation: Grounding the model in real, currently-shipping tests is a strong technique—the examples stay current with the codebase for free and reflect actual house usage. The problem is what the sample is drawn from: the suite the audit examined is the one with mixed layout, naming, and assertion grouping, so a retrieved set is as likely to seed the drift as the target shape.

options.label: C
options.text: Add three or four worked test cases to the prompt, authored by hand in the target style and chosen to cover both a single-assertion case and a multi-check case
options.explanation: A small curated set fixes layout and naming the way a template would, and—because the cases are picked to span a lone assertion and a bundled one—it also demonstrates how many checks belong in one case, the judgment the audit found drifting. Hand-authoring to the intended shape, rather than sampling, keeps the examples from carrying in the existing inconsistency.

options.label: D
options.text: Provide a fixed test skeleton in the prompt with labeled sections and a placeholder for each test case, and have the model fill the skeleton in for every file
options.explanation: A skeleton is a deterministic forcing function for the structural dimensions, so it looks like the most reliable way to pin layout and naming, and reaching for scaffolding over examples is reasonable here. It stalls on the third dimension: a fixed slot layout cannot decide how many logical checks a given function warrants, so it either freezes a count that misfits some files or leaves grouping to the model—the exact assertion inconsistency the audit flagged.

questions.explanation: The audit names three dimensions of drift—layout, naming, and assertion grouping—and the rewrite that wins must address all three, which is why C is best. A relocates the same descriptive rules into a style document without changing how reliably the model applies them per file. B uses demonstration rather than instruction, but retrieves from the very suite the audit found inconsistent, so the sampled cases can carry the drift back in. D's skeleton is a deterministic win for layout and naming—and on those two axes it rivals C—but a fixed slot layout cannot decide how many logical checks a function warrants, so the assertion-grouping inconsistency survives. C beats D because hand-authored cases chosen to span single- and multi-check examples convey the grouping judgment a template cannot encode, while still fixing layout and naming.

questions.topic: Cutting Per-Comment Triage Time in Automated Review
questions.text: Your CI pipeline posts an average of 22 review comments per pull request through the `claude-review` GitHub Action. A retrospective times the developer workflow and finds that the dominant cost is one step repeated per comment: to decide whether to accept or dismiss a finding, the developer leaves the PR thread, opens the changed file, scrolls to the referenced line, and reads the surrounding code to reconstruct which rule the comment is flagging — about two minutes of that reconstruction per comment. The team wants to shrink that per-comment reconstruction step. Which change best targets it?

options.label: A
options.text: Attach to each comment a permalink that jumps straight to the exact line in the changed file, plus a link to the rule's documentation page
options.explanation: Tempting because permalinks are the standard GitHub idiom and they remove the manual scrolling the retrospective described, so it feels like a direct hit on the navigation. But following the link still pulls the developer off the PR thread and into the file view to read the code and the rule doc separately — it makes the same reconstruction faster to start rather than removing it, so two minutes per comment becomes a slightly shorter trip, not no trip.

options.label: B
options.text: Enrich each comment with the rule's full metadata — its rationale, category, severity, and links to past PRs where the same rule previously fired — so the developer has the rule's history on hand when judging the finding
options.explanation: Reasonable on its face: a developer who knows what the rule is for and how it has played out before can judge a finding with more confidence, and richer comments generally help. But the bottleneck is seeing the actual offending code in context, not understanding the rule in the abstract; with the lines themselves still missing, the developer opens the file and reconstructs the situation exactly as before, now carrying extra reading on top.

options.label: C
options.text: Ship an IDE extension that ingests the posted findings and renders each one next to its line in the editor as the developer opens the changed file
options.explanation: Attractive because it puts the finding right beside the code, which is precisely where the developer ends up reconstructing it. But triage of all 22 comments happens on the PR thread, and this routes every one of them through the editor instead — the developer still opens and navigates to each file, now via a second tool, so it shifts the reconstruction into the IDE rather than collapsing the step.

options.label: D
options.text: Have each comment quote the offending lines and name the rule that fired, both rendered in the comment body
options.explanation: With the flagged lines and the rule's name sitting in the comment itself, the developer reads the finding and the code together where the comment already is and rules on it then and there. That is the open-the-file-and-find-the-line reconstruction the retrospective measured, and putting both pieces in the comment body is what removes it rather than relocating or front-loading it.

questions.explanation: All four options try to ease the per-comment reconstruction, so the answer turns on whether the change removes that step or merely moves it. D wins: quoting the offending lines and naming the rule inside the comment lets the developer judge the finding in the same place they are already reading, so the file-opening, line-finding reconstruction the retrospective timed never has to happen. A adds a permalink and a doc link, but a click still sends the developer into the file and a separate rule page — it speeds the trip instead of eliminating it. B supplies the rule's rationale and history but withholds the very thing the developer goes to the file for — the actual code — so the reconstruction continues with extra reading attached. C surfaces findings beside the code, but only inside the IDE, forcing every comment through a second tool while triage still lives on the PR thread; it shifts the step rather than collapsing it. Only D folds both pieces the judgment needs into the comment itself.

questions.topic: Workflow vs agent for a routine refactor
questions.text: Your platform team's subagent migrates every call from a deprecated helper `formatAddr` (imported from `utils/legacy`) to its replacement `renderAddress` (a separate function in `lib/address`), updating imports across 200 engineers' repos. Rerunning it on the same repo produces a different diff each time — sometimes it misses a file that uses `formatAddr`, sometimes it rewrites an unrelated `formatAddr` that is a local variable — and tool calls range from 6 to 19 with token cost swinging 3x. What change would most reliably produce a correct, repeatable diff at predictable cost?

options.label: A
options.text: Express the migration as a codemod that operates on each file's parsed module, updating each `formatAddr` call to `renderAddress` and rewriting the import, run once per repo.
options.explanation: Working from the parsed module lets the transform resolve which `formatAddr` references actually bind to the deprecated import — distinguishing the real helper from a same-named local, an aliased import, or a re-export — so it updates exactly the right call sites and leaves unrelated ones alone. Because the result is a deterministic function of each file's structure, the diff is identical on every run and the work is one bounded step, which makes both the correctness and the cost predictable.

options.label: B
options.text: Write a script that updates the import and the call sites referencing `formatAddr` to the new helper, run uniformly across the repos in one pass.
options.explanation: A scripted string-level rewrite is deterministic and removes the model, but matching the name `formatAddr` cannot tell the deprecated import apart from a shadowed local variable, an aliased import, a re-export, or an occurrence inside a comment or string literal. So it reproduces the exact misses and over-edits from the scenario — now deterministically rather than randomly — which fixes the variance without fixing the correctness.

options.label: C
options.text: Set the subagent to temperature 0 and cap its iterations and tool allowlist so every run follows the same bounded path.
options.explanation: Lowering temperature and capping iterations narrows the cost swing, but the model still decides which files to open and which references to edit, so the missed and over-edited files persist. A hard iteration cap can also stop a larger repo mid-migration, converting a variance problem into a partial-diff correctness problem.

options.label: D
options.text: Drive the language server's rename-symbol refactor through the subagent so every reference to `formatAddr` is updated consistently across the repo.
options.explanation: Rename-symbol is binding-aware and would update references precisely, but it renames one symbol in place — it cannot repoint the import to `lib/address` or substitute a different function, and the target here is a separate helper in a different module, not a renamed `formatAddr`. It performs the wrong operation for a cross-module API migration.

questions.explanation: A is correct: operating on each file's parsed module lets the transform resolve which `formatAddr` references actually bind to the deprecated import, so it edits exactly the right call sites and produces an identical diff on every run in one bounded step — addressing both the correctness symptoms (misses, over-edits) and the cost variance at once. B is a deterministic script but matches on the name `formatAddr`, so it cannot separate the deprecated import from shadowed locals, aliased imports, re-exports, or comment/string occurrences; it makes the wrong diff repeatable instead of making it correct. C reduces sampling and iteration variance but leaves file and reference selection to the model and risks truncating large repos at the cap. D's rename-symbol is binding-aware but only renames one symbol in place; it cannot repoint the import to a different module or swap in a different function, which is what this migration requires. Only A both resolves references correctly and yields a repeatable diff at predictable cost.

questions.topic: Subagent context isolation for repo-wide analysis
questions.text: A staff engineer runs a custom /audit-deprecation slash command over a ~3,200-file monorepo to find every caller of a deprecated legacyAuth() helper and score each call site against a fixed migration-risk scale the command spells out (touches an auth boundary = high, behind a feature flag = medium, internal-only = low). The helper is re-exported under two aliases and wrapped by a couple of thin adapter modules, so some callers reach it indirectly. Telemetry shows the main agent reads files into its own context until the run aborts with a context-window-exceeded error after touching roughly 40% of the directories, and the partial report misses several call sites. How should this audit be architected?

options.label: A
options.text: Run a ripgrep pass for legacyAuth across the repo, then have the agent open only the matched files and score each against the migration-risk scale.
options.explanation: Tempting because a deterministic text search is the natural way to shrink 3,200 files to a handful, and reading only the hits stays well under the context budget. It loses recall for this audit: a literal match on legacyAuth never surfaces the two aliased re-exports or the adapter wrappers, so the indirect callers go unscored and the report reproduces the same missed call sites it already showed.

options.label: B
options.text: Split the tree into slices and dispatch a subagent per slice that reads its slice, applies the command's migration-risk scale to each affected site, and returns the scored sites.
options.explanation: Correct: each subagent reads and scores its slice inside its own context, so the coordinator's context stays bounded no matter how many callers exist, and the worker that read the slice has the usage context the scale depends on. Because every subagent applies the same defined scale, the ratings stay comparable across slices, and reading full slices lets workers trace the aliases and adapters to the indirect callers.

options.label: C
options.text: Split the tree into slices and dispatch a subagent per slice that reads its slice and returns the relevant call-site code, then apply the migration-risk scale to every returned site in the coordinator so the scoring is done in one place.
options.explanation: Tempting because doing all the scoring in the coordinator is a real way to guarantee one rater applies the scale identically to every site. It separates the judgment from the context it needs: the scale keys on how each site uses the helper and which alias or adapter path reaches it, and the coordinator sees only returned snippets while the subagent that read the slice held that surrounding context. To restore it the coordinator would have to pull back substantial code, trading away the context savings.

options.label: D
options.text: Walk the tree in sequential batches in a single agent, compacting each batch's findings into a running summary before loading the next so the working context stays within budget.
options.explanation: Tempting because rolling compaction is a documented way to keep a long-running agent under its context budget, and batching does bound the memory. It loses fidelity: per-site scores are exactly the fine-grained detail compaction tends to fold away, so the summary carries forward fewer concrete call sites and the audit ends up with the same incomplete coverage.

questions.explanation: B is correct: dispatching a subagent per slice that both reads and scores its slice keeps the coordinator's context bounded while keeping the scoring next to the code it depends on, and having every subagent apply the command's defined scale keeps ratings comparable across slices; reading full slices also lets workers trace the aliases and adapters to indirect callers. A narrows the file set with a literal text search, which never surfaces the aliased re-exports or adapter wrappers, so it misses call sites just as the run already did. C uses the same fan-out but moves scoring to the coordinator, separating the judgment from the per-site usage and alias/adapter context that the scale needs — the coordinator sees only returned snippets, and restoring that context means shipping back enough code to erode the savings. D bounds memory with rolling compaction in one agent, but compaction is lossy and drops the individual call-site scores the audit must enumerate.

questions.text: Your developer-productivity rollout includes a `/refactor` subagent that rewrites code across multiple files in one run. Telemetry from a two-week pilot shows it applied edits that overwrote or deleted files in 9% of runs before any engineer saw the change, and three of those landed broken code on a branch. You want the agent to keep planning refactors on its own but stop an unreviewed destructive write from landing. Which change is most effective?

options.label: A
options.text: Instruct the refactor subagent in its system prompt to surface the planned edits and wait for the engineer's go-ahead before it overwrites or deletes any file.
options.explanation: A smart engineer reaches for this because prompting is the usual lever and it does put a person in the loop. It loses to C because compliance with a system-prompt instruction is probabilistic: on some fraction of runs the agent can still apply edits without the pause, which is exactly the 9% behavior already observed. The cost of one missed pause here is an unrecoverable overwrite, so the control cannot depend on the model choosing to follow it.

options.label: B
options.text: Snapshot the working tree before each refactor run so any overwritten or deleted file can be restored from the backup afterward.
options.explanation: This is a reasonable instinct given that files were destroyed, and it does make the damage recoverable. It loses to C because it acts only after the destructive write has already happened: someone must notice the bad change and restore the snapshot, and nothing stops broken code from landing on the branch in the meantime. C prevents the write rather than cleaning up after it.

options.label: C
options.text: Route the destructive apply step through an approval checkpoint that shows the engineer the planned diff and blocks execution until they approve it.
options.explanation: Correct: the approval is enforced in code, not requested of the model, so the irreversible write cannot run until a human has seen the diff and approved it. The agent still plans and proposes refactors on its own; only the destructive apply is held back, which directly stops the unreviewed overwrite from landing.

options.label: D
options.text: Add a post-edit step that runs the test suite after each refactor and automatically reverts the changes if any test fails.
options.explanation: This is tempting because three runs landed broken code, so a test gate looks like it targets the exact failure. It loses to C because it is an automated check with no person reviewing the change, and a clean overwrite or deletion that still compiles and passes the existing tests sails through unreviewed. It catches some breakage but not the unreviewed destructive write the scenario is about.

questions.explanation: C is best: routing the destructive apply through an approval checkpoint that shows the diff and blocks until an engineer approves enforces the review in code, so the irreversible write cannot land unreviewed while the agent keeps planning autonomously. A asks the model to pause via its system prompt, but that compliance is probabilistic and can be skipped on some runs — the very behavior already seen. B snapshots and restores, but it only recovers files after they are destroyed and lets broken code land in the meantime. D auto-runs tests and reverts on failure, but it is an automated gate with no human review, and a clean overwrite or deletion that passes existing tests still goes through unreviewed.

questions.text: To roll out a shared lint config, your platform team runs an orchestrator agent that dispatches one worker subagent per repository to edit the config and open a PR across five repos. A run's logs show the orchestrator's final message read "all 5 repositories updated," yet only 4 PRs exist. In the fifth repo the worker hit a merge conflict and exited without returning anything to the orchestrator, which composed its summary from the results it had received. What change to the orchestration code most directly prevents this false success report?

options.label: A
options.text: Wrap each worker dispatch so a worker that returns a failure status raises an error that aborts the run before the orchestrator composes its summary.
options.explanation: Incorrect. This catches a worker that comes back reporting failure, but the fifth worker came back with nothing at all, so there is no returned failure to raise on; the run still completes and summarizes treating the four it heard from as the whole set.

options.label: B
options.text: Have the orchestrator collect each worker's structured status object as it returns and emit the overall success summary only once every collected status object reads "succeeded."
options.explanation: Incorrect. The four statuses it collected all read "succeeded," so the gate passes. It checks the results that arrived but never asks whether one is missing, so the absent fifth never enters the comparison.

options.label: C
options.text: On any worker that returns a failure, have the orchestrator close the PRs already opened in the other repos so the run leaves no partial rollout behind.
options.explanation: Incorrect. All-or-nothing cleanup still triggers off a returned failure, which never arrives from the conflicted repo, and even when it does fire it addresses partial-state cleanup rather than the inflated success message the question is about.

options.label: D
options.text: Have the orchestrator hold the set of repos it dispatched and require a returned success for each before summarizing, counting a dispatched repo with no result as a non-success.
options.explanation: Correct. The bug is a result that never arrived, so only reconciling returns against the dispatched set of five catches it: the conflicted repo has no entry, fails the per-repo check, and blocks the success summary so the orchestrator can re-plan or escalate.

questions.explanation: The failure here is a lost result, not a reported one — the fifth worker returned nothing — so the fix has to measure returns against what was dispatched. D holds the dispatched set of five and requires a returned success for each, so a repo with no entry blocks the summary. A raises only on a returned failure status, which the conflicted worker never sent. B gates on the statuses it collected; the four that arrived all read "succeeded," so it passes over a denominator of four instead of five. C rolls back partial work but also fires only on a returned failure and targets cleanup rather than the false success report.

questions.topic: Expose internal docs via MCP vs pasting into the prompt
questions.text: Your rollout dashboard shows the shared CLAUDE.md has grown to ~28K tokens after teams pasted the full internal engineering handbook into it, and platform metrics report it is re-sent on every Claude Code session, inflating per-session input cost. A review of edits finds the handbook changes several times a week, yet any given /review or /test run touches only one or two of its ~40 sections. How should you expose the handbook to the agents through the MCP server that already fronts your internal docs?

options.label: A
options.text: Split the handbook into individually addressable MCP resources, one per section, and drop it from CLAUDE.md so each run retrieves only the sections it touches.
options.explanation: Per-section resources let a /review or /test run pull just the one or two sections it uses, the served copy is the live one so edits propagate immediately, and dropping it from CLAUDE.md ends the 28K re-send every session. It is the only exposure that addresses all three observations at once -- relevance, freshness, and standing cost -- without leaving a second copy to maintain.

options.label: B
options.text: Expose the handbook through a single get_handbook MCP tool that returns the full document on demand, and remove the pasted copy from CLAUDE.md.
options.explanation: On-demand loading removes the per-session blob and always returns current content, so it looks like a clean fix. But a single full-document call still pulls all ~40 sections into context every time docs are needed; it defers the 28K cost rather than eliminating it and ignores the finding that a run touches only one or two sections.

options.label: C
options.text: Keep a short section index in CLAUDE.md that points to the MCP server, and have agents fetch the named section through it when a task needs handbook content.
options.explanation: A lightweight index trims standing tokens while preserving discoverability and selective fetch, which is appealing. Its weakness is the index itself: with the handbook re-sectioned several times a week, the in-repo list of section names drifts out of sync, so you are back to maintaining a stale second copy -- the freshness problem the dashboard flagged.

options.label: D
options.text: Serve the handbook as per-section MCP resources, but also embed the three or four most-referenced sections in CLAUDE.md so common runs skip the retrieval round-trip.
options.explanation: Inlining hot sections is a reasonable latency optimization and keeps most of the doc selective. The catch is that those embedded sections carry their own standing token cost on every session and go stale on the same frequent-edit cadence, so the bloat-and-freshness problem returns for them -- and 'most-referenced' is a guess that drifts as work changes.

questions.explanation: All four route the handbook through the existing MCP server, so the decision turns on retrieval granularity and freshness given that runs touch only 1-2 of ~40 sections and the doc changes several times a week. A wins: per-section resources fetch only what a task needs, stay current automatically, and leave no copy behind -- resolving relevance, freshness, and standing cost together. B's single full-document tool defers the 28K load rather than eliminating it and still pulls all 40 sections whenever docs are needed. C's in-CLAUDE.md index reintroduces a second copy that drifts as the handbook is re-sectioned, recreating the staleness problem. D embeds the most-referenced sections to save a round-trip but reinstates standing token cost and staleness for those sections, and the 'most-referenced' set is speculative.

questions.topic: Tool description quality for an internal API tool
questions.text: Your MCP server exposes two similar internal-docs tools: search_runbooks (on-call/incident procedures) and search_design_docs (architecture RFCs). Both have one-line descriptions and a query parameter typed as an optional string. Traces show the model calls search_runbooks for architecture questions in 22% of invocations and sometimes sends an empty query, so engineers get irrelevant or empty results. Which change is the most effective first step to reduce this misuse?

options.label: A
options.text: Add four to six few-shot examples to the system prompt that map sample questions to the correct tool and show query filled with the user's topic.
options.explanation: Few-shot routing examples are a standard way to steer selection between confusable tools, so this looks like a direct fix. But it lives in the shared prompt rather than the tool surface, so it scales poorly as more tools are added, and it still leaves query optional in the schema, so empty-query calls remain valid and continue to slip through.

options.label: B
options.text: Rewrite each tool's description to contrast it with its sibling and mark query as required with a description of the topic text it expects.
options.explanation: Correct. The model-facing schema is what drives both decisions here: a description that says what each tool covers and how it differs from the other gives the selection signal that fixes the 22%, and making query a required parameter with a stated purpose closes the empty-query path. One change at the surface the model actually reads addresses both failure modes.

options.label: C
options.text: Merge the two tools into one search_internal_docs tool with a required corpus enum (runbooks or design_docs) and a required query, removing the choice the model gets wrong.
options.explanation: Collapsing the confusable pair is a real design move and would force a non-empty query through the schema, which is why it is tempting. But it relocates rather than removes the discrimination problem - the model must still pick the right corpus value from a thin description - and reshaping the API is a heavier change than a first diagnostic step calls for when the descriptions have never been written properly.

options.label: D
options.text: Add server-side validation that rejects an empty query and returns an error suggesting the sibling tool when results look topically mismatched.
options.explanation: A deterministic check is a strong guard for the empty-query case and the corrective error can nudge the model toward the right tool, so this reads as rigorous. But it acts after a bad call is already made, spending extra round trips on recovery, and it never improves the up-front selection signal, so the model keeps choosing search_runbooks for architecture questions and only learns by failing.

questions.explanation: Two failures co-occur: the model picks the wrong one of two confusable tools (22%) and sometimes sends an empty query because the parameter is optional. B fixes both at the schema the model reads each turn - contrasting descriptions supply the missing selection signal, and a required, documented query eliminates the empty-string path - which is why it is the strongest first step. A adds few-shot routing in the prompt, which can help selection but lives off the tool surface and leaves query optional, so empty calls persist. C merges the pair, which would enforce a non-empty query but only relocates the selection problem to a corpus enum and reshapes the API more than a first step warrants. D validates and corrects server-side, a sound guardrail that acts only after the bad call and never improves the up-front description the model uses to choose, so the 22% misselection continues. Only B repairs the up-front signal and the argument in one move.

questions.topic: MCP transport: stdio vs Streamable HTTP for a shared server
questions.text: Your internal-docs MCP server ships in each engineer's Claude Code config as a stdio server. A platform telemetry report across the 200 engineers shows every client spawns its own local copy of the server as a subprocess, each maintaining a separate documentation index; a docs update reaches an engineer only after they pull the latest index and restart their client, so indexes drift for days. Which change best addresses this?

options.label: A
options.text: Configure the local servers to read from one shared network-mounted index store, so every instance serves the same authoritative documentation content.
options.explanation: A shared index volume removes the drift symptom, but it centralizes storage, not the server. Each client still launches its own stdio subprocess, there is still no single network endpoint, and access control and update logging remain spread across 200 independent processes rather than one service.

options.label: B
options.text: Host the server once and expose it over the HTTP+SSE transport with `--transport sse`, the remote MCP transport, so every client connects to that single endpoint.
options.explanation: This correctly moves to one networked server, but it picks the wrong transport. HTTP+SSE was superseded by Streamable HTTP in the 2025-03-26 MCP spec revision and `--transport sse` is retained only for backward compatibility with older clients; standing up a new shared server on the deprecated transport is choosing the legacy mechanism over the current one.

options.label: C
options.text: Deploy the server as a standalone service on the Streamable HTTP transport and register it on each client with `claude mcp add --transport http`.
options.explanation: Streamable HTTP is the current MCP transport for a single standalone server that many clients reach over the network. One deployment serves one index, updates land centrally, and the per-client subprocess and its drift disappear in one move.

options.label: D
options.text: Run the existing server behind a shared `mcp-proxy` bridge that fronts it over the network, and point every client at the bridge's endpoint.
options.explanation: An mcp-proxy bridge is the right tool when you must expose a stdio server you cannot modify. Here you control the server, so bridging keeps it running as a single stdio process behind an extra hop and a new failure point, instead of running it natively on the transport built to serve many clients directly.

questions.explanation: C is correct: the stdio transport runs the server as a per-client subprocess, which is why 200 engineers get 200 separate, drifting indexes with no shared endpoint. Streamable HTTP is the current MCP transport for one standalone server that many clients connect to remotely, so a single deployment gives one authoritative index with central updates, auth, and logging (`claude mcp add --transport http`). A centralizes only the index storage; each client still spawns its own stdio subprocess and there is still no single server endpoint or central access control. B moves to one networked server but selects HTTP+SSE, which the 2025-03-26 spec superseded with Streamable HTTP and which survives only for backward compatibility, so it adopts the deprecated transport. D bridges the unchanged stdio server with mcp-proxy, a technique meant for servers you cannot modify; since you control this one, it adds a hop and a single point of failure while keeping the stdio process the native transport is designed to replace.

questions.topic: CLAUDE.md hierarchy for shared team conventions
questions.text: During the Claude Code rollout to ~200 engineers, an onboarding survey shows newcomers' sessions repeatedly skip the team's house rules for this repo: use pnpm not npm, conventional-commit messages, leave generated/ untouched. The rules are correct and stable, and they apply to this codebase specifically. You want a new hire to inherit them from the moment they clone the repo and open a session, without each person configuring anything. Which approach fits best?

options.label: A
options.text: Ship the conventions as an organization-wide managed-policy CLAUDE.md pushed to every developer's machine through your MDM tooling.
options.explanation: Managed-policy CLAUDE.md loads at session start and cannot be overridden, which is why it is attractive for guaranteed adherence. But it is scoped to the machine, not this repository: it would apply in every repo on every device and is provisioned through IT/MDM rather than traveling with the clone, so a freshly cloned repo carries nothing repo-specific on its own.

options.label: B
options.text: Bake the conventions into the onboarding setup script so it writes them to each new hire's ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md during machine provisioning.
options.explanation: User-level CLAUDE.md does load automatically for every project, so wiring it into provisioning feels like zero-touch. The catch is that it lives outside version control on each machine: the rules drift per developer, never update when the repo's conventions change, and a clone on an unprovisioned or reimaged machine inherits nothing.

options.label: C
options.text: Commit the conventions under .claude/rules/, splitting them into topic files each carrying paths frontmatter scoped to the files it governs.
options.explanation: Committed rules files do ship with the clone and are the documented way to modularize project instructions, which makes this look like the cleaner structured choice. But path-scoped rules load only when Claude touches files matching their patterns; broad session-start conventions like a package manager or commit format are not tied to a file pattern, so they would stay out of context at the start of a session, which is exactly when newcomers go astray.

options.label: D
options.text: Commit a CLAUDE.md with the conventions to the repository root and version it alongside the codebase.
options.explanation: Correct. A CLAUDE.md at the repo root travels with version control and is read in full at the start of every session launched in that tree, so each clone carries the current rules and a new hire inherits them with nothing to configure.

questions.explanation: D is correct: a CLAUDE.md committed at the repository root is version-controlled, so it travels with the clone, and Claude Code reads it in full at the start of every session in that tree, so each new hire inherits the current repo-specific rules with no per-person setup. A is the documented mechanism for org-wide policy, but it is machine-scoped and IT-deployed rather than repo-scoped and clone-borne, so it does not carry this codebase's rules with the clone. B auto-loads but lives outside version control on each machine, so it drifts per developer and a clone on an unprovisioned machine inherits nothing. C ships with the clone, yet path-scoped rules load only when Claude opens files matching their patterns, so broad session-start conventions like the package manager would not be in context when a newcomer begins, which is the moment the survey flags.

questions.topic: Custom slash command for a repeated workflow
questions.text: Your rollout dashboard shows engineers typing the same instruction by hand dozens of times a day: "open a draft pull request for the changes on the current branch, write a summary, and link the Jira ticket." The wording drifts between people, so PR descriptions come out inconsistent. You move the instruction into a version-controlled `/open-pr` command file under `.claude/commands/`, and developers will name the ticket when they run it, as in `/open-pr PROJ-1423`. The command body has one spot where the ticket key needs to appear so the generated description references it. Which way of wiring the file puts that key into the prompt?

options.label: A
options.text: Write `$ARGUMENTS` at the point in the body where the ticket key should read, then describe the PR around it.
options.explanation: `$ARGUMENTS` expands to the full argument string typed after the command name, so `/open-pr PROJ-1423` substitutes `PROJ-1423` at that spot before Claude sees the prompt. The key lands inside the body where the description text references it, which is what the workflow needs.

options.label: B
options.text: Write `$1` at that point so the first token after the command name is substituted in.
options.explanation: `$N` is shorthand for `$ARGUMENTS[N]` and the index is 0-based, so the first token is `$0` and `$1` is the second. With a single key passed, `$1` matches nothing and expands to empty, leaving the description without a ticket.

options.label: C
options.text: Leave the body free of any placeholder and let the command pick up the ticket key from the input the developer supplies.
options.explanation: When a command body contains no `$ARGUMENTS`, Claude Code still forwards the input by appending an `ARGUMENTS: PROJ-1423` line to the end of the content. The key reaches Claude, but it arrives as a trailing label rather than inside the sentence that drafts the description, so the targeted spot stays blank.

options.label: D
options.text: Add `argument-hint: [ticket]` to the command frontmatter and reference `ticket` at that point in the body.
options.explanation: `argument-hint` populates the autocomplete hint shown while typing the command and binds no value, so a bare `ticket` token in the body is not a placeholder and is never replaced. The hint guides the typist but does nothing to inject the key into the prompt.

questions.explanation: A wires `$ARGUMENTS` into the body, and it expands to the full argument string as typed, so `/open-pr PROJ-1423` drops `PROJ-1423` at the exact spot the description references. B reaches for `$1`, but `$N` is 0-based shorthand for `$ARGUMENTS[N]`: `$0` is the first token and `$1` is the second, so a single passed key leaves `$1` empty. C relies on the auto-append fallback, which does forward the key but only as a trailing `ARGUMENTS:` line, not inside the sentence that needs it, so the targeted spot stays blank. D uses `argument-hint`, which only feeds autocomplete and binds no value, so a bare `ticket` in the body is never substituted.

questions.topic: Plan mode vs direct execution for a risky migration
questions.text: A postmortem from last sprint shows an engineer asked Claude Code to migrate a service from the old logging library to the new one in a single direct pass; Claude edited 43 files and every edit compiled, used the sanctioned new API, and passed code review. A week later the team found the migration was wrong at the design level: the new library expects one logger created at startup and threaded through, but Claude had recreated a fresh logger per module exactly as the old library did, so each call site looked correct on its own while the service as a whole lost its shared context and log correlation. Another sweeping framework migration is now scheduled across the same repo. How should the team run the next migration so this kind of approach-level mistake gets caught before Claude commits to dozens of edits?

options.label: A
options.text: Provide Claude an explicit mapping of every old call to its new-API replacement in the migration spec so each edit is generated against the documented surface.
options.explanation: A smart engineer reaches for this because it pins the new surface down up front and feels like it removes guesswork. But last sprint's edits were not ignorant of the surface — every call already used the sanctioned new API correctly in isolation. A 1:1 call mapping would have reproduced the same per-module-logger pattern, because the defect was the overall ownership model, not which functions to call. It improves generation but adds no checkpoint where the chosen approach is examined before the edits land.

options.label: B
options.text: Run the migration in Plan mode and have the engineer approve Claude's proposed migration approach before any files are edited.
options.explanation: Correct. The defect lived in the strategy — recreate a logger per module versus create one and thread it through — which is a single decision that is legible in a plan but invisible in any one edit. Surfacing that plan for approval before the 43 edits exist lets the engineer reject the wrong ownership model once, and the same up-front review of intent carries over to whatever design choice the new framework migration hinges on.

options.label: C
options.text: Run the migration on an isolated branch and review the full diff across all touched files before merging it to main.
options.explanation: This is standard catch-it-before-it-lands hygiene and feels safe because nothing reaches main unreviewed. But a full-diff review on a branch is exactly what already happened — the 43-file diff was reviewed and approved, because each locally valid edit hid the aggregate design error. Re-running that same after-the-fact review repeats the modality that already missed it, and only after the entire autonomous pass has been paid for.

options.label: D
options.text: Add a PreToolUse hook that checks each edit against the new logging API surface and blocks any Edit that calls something outside it.
options.explanation: The deterministic-gate instinct is appealing — a hard per-edit check rather than a hope for compliance. But the hook inspects one Edit at a time, and every edit here used the new API correctly, so each would pass the gate cleanly. The mistake is emergent across call sites — the wrong logger-ownership pattern — with no per-edit predicate to encode, so a surface allowlist sees nothing to block.

questions.explanation: B is correct: the failure was an approach-level choice — a fresh logger per module instead of one shared instance threaded through — so every individual edit compiled, used the new API, and passed review while the design as a whole was wrong. That kind of error is legible exactly once, as the proposed approach, which Plan mode surfaces for approval before any of the 43 edits exist, and the habit generalizes to whatever design decision the next framework migration turns on. A supplies a correct old-to-new call mapping, but the edits already used the new API correctly per site; a 1:1 mapping reproduces the same ownership pattern and adds no checkpoint on the approach. C reviews the full diff on a branch, which is the same after-the-fact review that already approved this diff because each locally valid edit masked the aggregate flaw. D's PreToolUse hook checks each edit against the API surface in isolation, but every edit calls the sanctioned API, so the gate passes each one while the emergent cross-site pattern — the real defect — has no per-edit predicate to catch.

questions.text: Telemetry on a shared `/review` slash command used by 200 engineers shows comments are acted on only 28% of the time. In the transcripts, the same defect class is tagged "critical" in one PR and "minor" in another, some comments cite a file and line while others are loose paragraphs, and many never say what to change. The command body is one line: "Review this diff and report any problems you find." Which change to the command would most improve how consistently engineers can act on its output?

options.label: A
options.text: Embed four worked example comments in the command body, each showing a real finding written the way you want it: the file and line, the severity, and the exact code change to make.
options.explanation: Worked examples are a strong technique for pinning a comment's shape, and a competent engineer reaches for them first when output is uneven. But exemplars demonstrate the format without defining where one severity tier ends and the next begins, so across the wide range of defects this command sees, identical issues still drift between tiers run to run; the four samples also cannot cover the long tail, leaving the model to improvise on anything they do not resemble.

options.label: B
options.text: Add a CLAUDE.md table mapping each common issue type to a default severity, and have the command consult it before labeling any finding.
options.explanation: A lookup table feels like the deterministic way to stop label drift, since the same issue type would always map to the same tier. The catch is that a single defect class legitimately spans tiers depending on exposure, code path, and blast radius, so a fixed type-to-severity map mislabels the cases that matter while doing nothing to require a file:line or a concrete fix in each comment.

options.label: C
options.text: Specify the per-comment fields the output must contain and define each severity tier by attaching a sample finding that belongs in it, so labels are judged against fixed reference cases rather than the model's read of the diff.
options.explanation: Defining the tiers by attaching a reference finding to each one gives the model stable, absolute anchors, so the same defect class lands in the same tier from run to run, while mandating the fields per comment forces every finding to carry a file:line and a stated change. This is the one option that closes both the tier drift and the missing-remediation gap together.

options.label: D
options.text: Split the command into two passes: the first locates issues, the second rewrites each one into a precise fix with its location, so each pass has a single job.
options.explanation: Separating detection from remediation is a real way to raise the quality of the fix text, and decomposition often steadies an LLM that is juggling goals. It does sharpen the suggested-change part, but neither pass is given a definition of what each severity tier means, so the cross-run label inconsistency that is driving distrust survives the split while latency and cost roughly double.

questions.explanation: C is correct because the two failures, drifting severity labels and comments with no file:line or fix, both trace to a command that says nothing about what its output must look like; attaching a reference finding to each severity tier gives the model absolute anchors so identical defects are labeled identically, and naming the required per-comment fields forces every comment to be actionable. A uses worked examples, which fix the shape of a comment but never define the tier boundaries, so labels keep drifting across the diverse defect set and the examples leave the long tail uncovered. B's static issue-type-to-severity table strips out the context (exposure, code path) that legitimately moves a defect between tiers and still mandates neither location nor fix. D's two-pass split improves the fix text but gives no severity definitions, so the cross-run inconsistency that is eroding trust persists while latency and cost grow. Only C anchors the labels and guarantees the fields in a single change.

questions.topic: Reducing hallucinated/nonexistent APIs in generated code
questions.text: Engineers report that code from your `/review` and `/test` commands fails at runtime because it calls internal-SDK methods that do not exist — plausible names like `client.getCustomerV2()` and `orders.batchFetch()` that the real library never defined. This affects roughly 9% of generated files, and the invented names follow the SDK's own naming style. The team runs an MCP documentation server that can return the SDK's real method signatures on request. What is the most effective fix?

options.label: A
options.text: Add a post-generation check that type-checks each generated file against the real SDK and, when it finds a call to an unknown method, sends the file back to the model to be regenerated until it type-checks clean.
options.explanation: Tempting because it is a deterministic gate against ground truth — exactly the kind of guarantee prompting cannot give, and it provably catches every invented call. But on a rejection it returns only the verdict, not the real signatures, so the model regenerates from the same incomplete knowledge and guesses again; the loop spins (or stalls on the 9% it cannot complete) until the correct signatures are actually supplied in context. The gate detects the gap but does not close it, so it presupposes the real fix rather than being it.

options.label: B
options.text: Have a build step query the MCP server for the full SDK and write all of its method signatures into the shared CLAUDE.md so the authoritative interface is always loaded for every command.
options.explanation: Tempting because it puts real, authoritative signatures from the MCP server into context and reuses existing infrastructure. But dumping the entire interface into CLAUDE.md loads a large block of signatures into every call regardless of relevance, crowding the context, and the written copy is a point-in-time export that diverges from the server as the SDK changes — so the model is grounded on a stale, unfocused snapshot rather than the live interface for the methods a given file actually needs.

options.label: C
options.text: Index the existing codebase and retrieve real call sites for the relevant SDK objects into context before generation, so the model imitates how the methods are actually invoked in your code.
options.explanation: Tempting because it grounds generation in real, working usage drawn from your own repository rather than the model's memory. But call sites only cover methods the codebase already exercises, so any method outside that set — including the ones being invented — has no example to retrieve; and example invocations show argument shapes, not the authoritative signature list that establishes which methods exist at all. Coverage gaps leave the same room to hallucinate.

options.label: D
options.text: Have the `/review` and `/test` commands query the MCP server for the signatures of the SDK methods they are about to use and place those signatures in context before the code is generated.
options.explanation: Correct: the commands fetch the live, authoritative signatures for exactly the SDK surface each task touches and supply them at generation time. The model selects from the real method set for that specific task instead of producing names from memory, which removes the gap that produces invented calls — and it draws from the MCP server directly, so there is no separately maintained copy to drift.

questions.explanation: The invented methods come from generating against the model's recollection of the SDK rather than its actual interface; the fix has to put the right signatures in front of the model at the moment it writes code. D does exactly that: each command queries the MCP server for the signatures of the methods it is about to use and includes them in context, so generation draws from the live, authoritative, task-relevant interface. A is a real deterministic gate and catches every bad call, but on rejection it feeds back only the verdict, not the signatures, so the model regenerates from the same incomplete knowledge and the loop does not converge on the 9% — it detects the gap without closing it. B also uses the MCP server's real signatures but exports the whole interface into CLAUDE.md, a bulky point-in-time snapshot that crowds every call and drifts from the server over time, rather than the focused live lookup. C grounds in real code but only covers methods the repository already uses and shows call shapes rather than the authoritative method list, leaving the invented (unused) methods unretrieved. Only D supplies the live, focused ground truth for the exact surface each task touches.

questions.topic: Structured output for a tool consuming Claude's result
questions.text: An internal tool calls the Messages API to summarize a pull request, then parses Claude's reply as JSON to populate dashboard fields. Logs show the parse step throwing on about 7% of calls: on those, Claude opens with a sentence of prose or wraps the object in a ```json fence before the data. The fields it returns are otherwise correct. What change most reliably yields output the tool can consume?

options.label: A
options.text: Declare the dashboard fields as a tool's input_schema, set tool_choice to require that tool, and read the fields from the resulting tool_use block.
options.explanation: Routing the result through a forced tool means Claude returns the fields as a tool_use input object validated against the schema, so the program reads them directly and no longer parses free text. The prose-and-fence failure mode disappears because there is no surrounding text to strip.

options.label: B
options.text: Prefill the assistant turn with an opening `{` so the response is constrained to begin inside the JSON object.
options.explanation: Prefilling with `{` is a documented way to suppress the leading sentence and the opening fence, and here it would clear most of the 7%. But it only constrains the start of the reply: the model can still append a closing fence or a trailing remark after the object, and it enforces no schema on the keys, so some parses still fail.

options.label: C
options.text: Set temperature to 0 and include one worked example of the exact object in the prompt so the formatting stops varying.
options.explanation: Greedy decoding with a concrete example is a sound way to stabilize variable output, which is why it is tempting. The wrapping here is a learned formatting habit rather than sampling noise, though, so temperature 0 makes the same prose-or-fence reply appear more consistently rather than removing it.

options.label: D
options.text: Wrap the parse in a try/catch that, on failure, re-sends the reply with the parse error and asks Claude to return just the object.
options.explanation: A repair loop is a common resilience pattern and would recover most of the failing 7%, so it looks like a safe fix. It still treats text-parsing as the contract and leans on a second probabilistic call that can itself wrap the output, adding latency and cost on exactly the calls that already failed.

questions.explanation: A wins because forcing a tool turns the dashboard fields into a schema-validated tool_use input object the program reads directly, so there is no free text to parse and the prose-or-fence failure cannot occur. B (prefill `{`) is a real technique that clears the leading prose and fence, but it constrains only the start of the reply and enforces no schema, so trailing text or off-shape keys can still break the parse. C (temperature 0 plus an example) stabilizes sampling noise, yet the wrapping here is a formatting habit, so determinism just reproduces the same wrapped reply more reliably. D (parse-failure repair loop) recovers most failures but keeps text-parsing as the contract and relies on a second probabilistic call that can wrap its own output, adding latency and cost on the calls that already failed.

questions.topic: Context budgeting for a huge codebase
questions.text: Telemetry from your 200-engineer rollout shows that on the largest monorepo, sessions hit the context limit roughly 30% of the time and average 4x the token cost of other teams, with no measurable quality gain. The team is asking how to give Claude Code enough grounding in this codebase to make accurate edits without sessions ballooning. Which approach best addresses this?

options.label: A
options.text: Have a subagent explore the relevant subsystem and return a distilled summary to the main session, so the main context stays lean while still carrying what the task needs.
options.explanation: Returning a distilled summary keeps the main thread small, but for editing tasks the summary is the wrong artifact: making a correct change needs exact current contents, signatures, and line numbers, which a summary drops. The main session then re-reads the real files anyway, adding a hop without removing the fidelity gap. A read-the-source path beats a summarize-then-act path here.

options.label: B
options.text: Add a navigable index to CLAUDE.md — a module-to-path map, entry points, and build/test commands — and start each task by pointing Claude at the relevant directory.
options.explanation: Correct. A small index gives durable orientation that survives every session cheaply, and pointing Claude at a starting directory lets its Glob/Grep/Read pull in exactly the files the task touches. Sessions stop dumping the tree to compensate for not knowing where things live, which is what drove the limit hits and 4x cost.

options.label: C
options.text: Have engineers run /compact mid-task to summarize the transcript, and clear context between unrelated tasks, so the window is trimmed back down well before it approaches the limit.
options.explanation: Compaction is good hygiene, but it acts after context has already been loaded — it summarizes or discards what is there rather than changing what gets pulled in. On this repo the cost is incurred when whole subtrees enter context in the first place; trimming afterward fights the symptom and can drop detail the task still needs.

options.label: D
options.text: Define subsystem boundaries and have engineers open one session per subsystem, scoping each task to a single area so no session needs to hold more than one part of the repository.
options.explanation: Narrowing scope reduces how much a session must hold, and for self-contained work it helps. But it is a workflow constraint, not grounding: cross-cutting changes still span subsystems, and within any one session Claude still lacks a map of where things live, so it can fall back to broad reads. It manages session size without giving Claude the orientation that makes targeted reads possible.

questions.explanation: B is the best move: a compact navigable index in CLAUDE.md (module-to-path map, entry points, build/test commands) plus pointing Claude at the relevant directory gives durable, cheap orientation and lets its own Glob/Grep/Read fetch exactly the task-relevant files — removing the reason sessions were dumping whole subtrees and directly cutting the limit hits and 4x cost. A keeps the main context lean but a distilled summary strips the exact contents and line numbers edits require, so the real files get re-read anyway. C's /compact is sound hygiene but acts only after context is already loaded, fighting the symptom rather than what gets pulled in. D narrows session scope but is a workflow band-aid that breaks on cross-cutting changes and still leaves Claude without a map within a session.

questions.topic: Prompt caching for repeated large context
questions.text: An internal docs assistant calls the Messages API directly from one long-lived service process that holds a single API key. The service treats each question as a standalone request and replays no prior messages. Each request sends a `tools` array assembled from a tool module imported when the service boots, then a `system` array, then the user's question. The `system` array holds two blocks: a short banner naming the assistant and the identifier of the conversation the request opens, followed by the same ~30K-token docs corpus; the `cache_control: {"type": "ephemeral"}` breakpoint is placed at the end of the corpus block. Engineers ask hundreds of questions over a working session, sometimes minutes apart and sometimes back to back. On every call `usage` reports `cache_creation_input_tokens` near 30K and `cache_read_input_tokens` of 0, and input cost never falls. What is the most likely root cause?

options.label: A
options.text: Cache entries are scoped per workspace rather than per organization, so the assistant resolves to its own partition and rewrites the prefix instead of sharing an existing one; routing the service through a shared workspace would let it reuse the same entry.
options.explanation: Per-workspace cache scoping is real and would produce writes without reads, but a single process on one key already resolves to exactly one workspace, so there is no partition boundary being crossed here and nothing for shared routing to fix.

options.label: B
options.text: Ephemeral cache entries live for only about five minutes, and engineers deliberate between questions long enough for each entry to lapse before the next call, so every request rebuilds the prefix; a longer cache lifetime would keep entries warm between questions.
options.explanation: The roughly five-minute lifetime is genuine, but it cannot account for the back-to-back questions, which arrive well inside that window and would still read from a live entry. Misses on every call, including the rapid ones, point to a prefix that never matches rather than one that keeps expiring.

options.label: C
options.text: The banner sits ahead of the breakpoint and the conversation identifier it carries differs each time the service opens a new conversation, so the bytes before the breakpoint shift between calls and no stored entry matches; placing the banner after the breakpoint would let the corpus cache.
options.explanation: Correct: the cache matches an exact byte prefix over tools, then system, then messages, up to the breakpoint. The banner is the first system block, and because each request opens a fresh conversation its identifier varies the leading bytes, so every call lays down a new entry and matches none, exactly the non-zero creation with zero reads observed. Move the banner behind the breakpoint and the stable corpus reads from cache.

options.label: D
options.text: The `tools` array is the leading segment of the cached prefix, and if its serialization shifts between calls everything behind it is invalidated and rewritten; pinning the tools to one fixed payload reused verbatim would restore reads.
options.explanation: A changing tools segment would indeed invalidate the whole prefix and yield writes without reads, but the array comes from a module imported once at boot and serialized the same way each call, so this leading segment is already stable and is not the part that varies between requests.

questions.explanation: C is correct: the cache matches an exact byte prefix over tools, then system, then messages, up to the breakpoint. The banner is the first system block, ahead of the breakpoint, and because the service opens a fresh conversation per request the identifier in that banner shifts the leading bytes from call to call, so each request writes a new entry and matches none, which is the non-zero `cache_creation` with zero `cache_read` shown. Moving the banner behind the breakpoint stabilizes the prefix and lets the corpus read from cache. A invokes real per-workspace scoping, but one process on one key lands in a single workspace, so no partition is crossed. B describes the real five-minute lifetime, yet the back-to-back questions arrive inside that window and would still hit a live entry, so expiry cannot explain misses on every call. D correctly notes that a shifting tools segment would invalidate the prefix, but the array is built from a module imported once at boot and serialized identically each call, leaving the banner as the only segment that varies.

questions.topic: Workflow vs agent for a fixed extraction pipeline
questions.text: Your invoice-extraction pipeline runs the same fixed stages on every document (parse, call Claude with the emit_invoice tool, validate against the schema, write the record to the accounting ledger) at thousands of documents per day. Monitoring shows that about 9% of invoices arrive with a missing, ambiguous, or low-confidence field, and today those either fail validation and stall the batch or get written with a best-guess value that later has to be corrected in the ledger. What is the most appropriate way to handle this 9% within the pipeline?

options.label: A
options.text: For invoices whose fields come back missing or low-confidence, hand that record to a tool-using agent that can pull the source PDF and the vendor master record and decide on its own how to cross-check and re-derive the value before emitting.
options.explanation: Tempting because an ambiguous invoice really does look like it needs flexible, case-by-case cross-referencing, and an agent that can choose its own lookups feels like the natural fit. It is the wrong choice because the failing situations are a finite, known set, so model-directed control flow buys nondeterministic iteration counts, variable latency and cost, and harder auditing precisely on the records destined for an accounting ledger, where predictable, traceable handling matters most.

options.label: B
options.text: Re-run extraction on every failed record with a stronger system prompt and a handful of few-shot examples of tricky invoices, so the model fills the missing or ambiguous fields on a second pass before the record is written.
options.explanation: Tempting because prompting is the usual first lever and a sharper prompt with examples often does lift extraction quality. It is wrong here because it answers an uncertainty problem with probabilistic compliance: a confidently wrong value still passes straight into the ledger, and you have traded a visible validation stall for a silent correctness error that surfaces only when someone audits the books.

options.label: C
options.text: Make schema validation a hard precondition for the write: any record with a failed field or a confidence score below threshold is held out of the ledger and sent to a review queue, and only resolved records are committed.
options.explanation: Correct. The stages are already fixed, so the gap is not the control flow but what happens at the write step. Gating the commit on deterministic schema and confidence checks keeps every uncertain record out of the ledger until a person resolves it, so nothing unverified is ever booked and each entry stays traceable, while the clean 91% flow through untouched at steady cost.

options.label: D
options.text: Relax validation so an uncertain record is still written immediately, but flagged as "pending" with its low-confidence fields marked, and reconcile the pending entries in a scheduled human review afterward.
options.explanation: Tempting because it keeps throughput high, avoids stalling the batch, and still puts a human in the loop to clean things up, which feels like a reasonable balance. It is wrong because it admits unverified entries into the accounting ledger before they are confirmed; even marked as pending, those rows are live records that downstream reports and reconciliations can read, which is exactly the auditability the ledger cannot give up.

questions.explanation: The pipeline's stages are already fixed and the 9% of problem records form a finite, known set, so the real question is what the write step does with uncertainty, not whether to add model-directed control flow. C is best: gate the commit on deterministic schema and confidence checks, holding uncertain records in a review queue so only verified entries reach the ledger. A reaches for a self-directing agent on cases that are bounded, paying nondeterministic cost and latency and weaker auditability where the ledger needs the opposite. B applies a stronger prompt and few-shot pass, but that is probabilistic and lets a confidently wrong value into the books. D also routes to human review, which is why it is the closest competitor, but it writes the unverified record first and reconciles later; those provisional rows are live ledger entries before confirmation, breaking the gate-before-write guarantee that C preserves.

questions.text: Your invoice extraction pipeline ingests three known source streams — digital invoices, remittance emails, and scanned PDFs — and calls Claude with the emit_invoice tool through the Messages API. The weekly accuracy report breaks field-level accuracy down by stream: 96% on digital invoices, 88% on remittance emails, but only 71% on scanned PDFs, where the single shared prompt frequently misreads totals and currency. What is the most effective change to close the accuracy gap?

options.label: A
options.text: Augment the shared system prompt with scan-specific guidance and worked examples of reading totals and currency off noisy, low-contrast scans.
options.explanation: A is wrong. Targeted instructions and examples are a reasonable lever and would nudge scan handling, but scans still travel the same text-oriented path the digital and email streams use; the prompt cannot make the model read a page image it never receives as one, so the worst stream stays bottlenecked by the shared handling and the gap only narrows by probabilistic compliance.

options.label: B
options.text: Run the scanned PDFs through a dedicated OCR engine first, then feed the recovered text into the digital-invoice path that already scores 96%.
options.explanation: B is wrong. Folding scans into the proven 96% path is appealing, but OCR flattens the scan to a linear text string and discards the spatial layout — column alignment, decimal placement, currency glyphs — which is exactly where totals and currency get misread. The image signal that could disambiguate those fields is thrown away before extraction, so the recovered text carries the same errors the shared prompt already makes.

options.label: C
