options.text: Pass the already-known stream label into the shared prompt as context so the model adapts its reading approach to each stream.
options.explanation: C is wrong. Conditioning on the stream tag is lightweight and uses information the pipeline already has, and it would help the model set expectations per stream. But a single prompt steered by a label still produces one extraction behavior over the same input modality; it gives scans no image-level handling, so the field-reading failures on totals and currency persist.

options.label: D
options.text: Send each stream down its own extraction path, and have the scanned-PDF path read from the page image rather than from extracted text.
options.explanation: D is correct. The failure is concentrated in one input modality, and the fix is to stop forcing it through shared text handling: a dedicated scan path that reads the page as an image lets the model use layout and visual cues to resolve totals and currency, which the other paths never lost. It also reuses the stream the pipeline already tracks, so no new detection step is needed.

questions.explanation: D is correct: scan accuracy lags because all three streams share one text-oriented extraction path, and the fix is a stream-specific path that lets the scan case read the page as an image, recovering the layout and visual cues (decimal placement, currency glyphs) that resolve the misread totals and currency. A keeps scans on the shared text path and leans on prompt compliance, so it narrows the gap only probabilistically. B is the closest competitor — feeding scans into the 96% path is tempting — but OCR linearizes the page and discards the spatial signal where these specific fields fail, so the recovered text inherits the same errors. C uses the known stream label but still drives a single prompt over one modality, giving scans no image-level handling. Only D gives the scan stream genuinely different, modality-appropriate handling.

questions.topic: Escalating low-confidence extractions to a human
questions.text: The weekly accuracy report shows that about 9% of invoices auto-posted by your extraction pipeline were later corrected by accounting. The errors cluster in scanned PDFs with faint text, where Claude fills in an amount or due date that looks reasonable on the record but turns out to be wrong. You want to catch these uncertain extractions before the JSON reaches the accounting system. Which approach catches them most reliably?

options.label: A
options.text: Extend the emit_invoice schema with a per-field confidence signal (or null-with-reason), and have your code route any flagged record to a review queue before posting.
options.explanation: This captures the model's own uncertainty at the moment of extraction, which is the only signal that distinguishes a confident read from a guess on faint text. The downstream code then escalates deterministically on that flag.

options.label: B
options.text: Add programmatic validation before posting: check that line items sum to the total, the date parses and falls in a sane range, and the vendor matches your master list, routing any failure to review.
options.explanation: Deterministic business-rule checks are reliable for malformed data, but the failing values here are internally consistent and well-formed. A plausible wrong amount still sums correctly and a plausible wrong date still parses in range, so these checks pass the very records that are wrong.

options.label: C
options.text: Gate on document quality: run the scanned PDFs through OCR-confidence scoring and send any page below a contrast or confidence threshold to review.
options.explanation: The errors do cluster in faint scans, so gating on scan quality is intuitive. But it is a document-level proxy: it floods review with legible-but-faint pages while never telling you which specific field the model actually guessed, so escalation is both noisier and less targeted.

options.label: D
options.text: Extract each invoice twice and flag any record where the two passes disagree on a field, posting the records where both passes agree.
options.explanation: Disagreement across passes is a standard self-consistency check. The trap is that on faint text the model tends to settle on the same plausible reading both times, so the confident hallucinations agree with themselves and slip through while the check mostly catches genuinely ambiguous cases that were not the problem.

questions.explanation: A is best because the failures are plausible, well-formed values produced on faint text, so the only signal that separates a real read from a guess is the model's own uncertainty at extraction time; exposing that through the schema and escalating on it in code catches the right records. B's business-rule validation only catches malformed data, but a plausible wrong amount still sums and a plausible wrong date still parses, so those records pass. C gates on document quality, a proxy that over-escalates legible faint scans and never identifies which field was guessed. D's double-extraction relies on disagreement, but the model produces the same plausible value on both passes for confident hallucinations, so they agree and slip through.

questions.text: A weekly accuracy report on your invoice extraction pipeline shows 100% of emitted records passing emit_invoice schema validation, yet a manual audit finds that ~9% of records drawn from ambiguous documents (multi-currency invoices, scanned remittance emails) carry a confidently wrong vendor or amount while remaining perfectly well-formed JSON. You want to raise field-level accuracy on exactly these hard cases, processing thousands of documents per day with no human in the loop. Which change is most effective?

options.label: A
options.text: Have the extractor emit a per-field confidence alongside each value, then automatically re-extract only the fields whose confidence falls below a tuned threshold.
options.explanation: Confidence-gated re-extraction is a cheap, targeted form of self-correction with no human involved, which is why it is tempting. But the audited failures are confidently wrong: the model assigns high confidence to the values it misreads, so those fields stay above the threshold and are never selected for re-extraction. The gate filters out uncertain answers, not the confident misreads that make up the measured 9%.

options.label: B
options.text: Add a second pass that scores each emitted record against the original document and regenerates the specific fields whose scored value disagrees with the source.
options.explanation: Correct. The check is grounded in the source document itself, so a value that is well-formed and confidently asserted but contradicted by the invoice is caught and only the disagreeing fields are regenerated. Comparing against independent ground truth is the one approach here that surfaces a confident misread, and it scales without human review.

options.label: C
options.text: Run extraction twice on each document and reconcile the result, keeping fields where the two independent passes agree and re-extracting only the fields where they differ.
options.explanation: Double extraction with reconciliation is a familiar way to catch flaky outputs without a human, so it looks like a fit. The problem is that a confident misread on an ambiguous document is reproducible: both passes read the same scanned remittance the same way and return the same wrong vendor, so the fields agree and reconciliation flags nothing. It catches variance between runs, not a stable confident error.

options.label: D
options.text: Add an automated evaluator pass that audits each record for internal consistency (currency code valid for the vendor's region, total equal to the sum of line items, dates within the invoice period) and regenerates any field that violates a rule.
options.explanation: A dedicated evaluator that checks each record and regenerates violations is the right shape of solution and needs no human, which makes it persuasive. But a confidently misread vendor or amount on a multi-currency invoice can be entirely self-consistent: the record already passes schema validation and can satisfy every cross-field rule while still naming the wrong party. Internal consistency cannot detect a value that disagrees only with the source document.

questions.explanation: B is correct: the failures are confidently wrong but well-formed, so the only check that surfaces them is one grounded in the original document — a second pass scores each record against the source and regenerates the fields that disagree, all without human review. A re-extracts only low-confidence fields, but the errors are high-confidence, so they never trip the gate. C reconciles two extraction runs, but a confident misread on an ambiguous document is reproducible, so both passes agree and nothing is flagged. D audits internal consistency (currency, totals, dates), but a wrong vendor or amount can be perfectly self-consistent and still pass every rule; only checking against the source catches it. A, C, and D all add an automated verification step targeted at the hard cases, but each grounds its check in the model's own output rather than the document, which is exactly why they miss confident errors.

questions.topic: Reliable JSON via tool use vs prompt-and-parse
questions.text: In the invoice-extraction pipeline, json.loads() throws on 9% of documents: Claude is prompted to "respond only with a JSON object," but its raw text sometimes opens with a sentence of explanation or wraps the object in a markdown fence. The downstream accounting system rejects any record that does not contain every field of the target invoice shape with the correct types. Which change most reliably yields records that conform to that shape?

options.label: A
options.text: Add three few-shot exchanges to the prompt whose assistant turns are bare JSON objects matching the target shape, plus a line telling Claude to omit any prose or code fences.
options.explanation: Exemplars and a tighter instruction raise the rate of clean JSON, but the output is still free-form generation: a fraction of documents will continue to draw a preamble, a missing field, or a wrong type, so the parse-and-reject failures persist for the cases the accounting system cares about most.

options.label: B
options.text: Keep prompting for JSON, but strip any markdown fence, slice from the first { to the last }, and re-run json.loads(), repeating the call up to three times when it still fails.
options.explanation: Post-processing the text plus a few retries recovers some of the 9%, but it operates on whatever Claude happens to emit. Brace-slicing mis-handles nested or truncated objects, and a retry that parses does not verify the object has the required fields or correct types, so malformed-but-parseable records still slip through.

options.label: C
options.text: Define an emit_invoice tool whose input_schema is the target shape, set strict: true, force it with tool_choice, and read the validated object from the tool_use block.
options.explanation: Forcing a strict-mode tool means Claude's emitted arguments are constrained to the declared input_schema, so the object you read from the tool_use block already carries every required field with the right types. There is no prose to strip and no separate validation pass, which removes the parse-and-conformance failure class the accounting system rejects on.

options.label: D
options.text: Prefill the assistant turn with an opening { so the response is forced to begin as a JSON object rather than prose or a fence.
options.explanation: Prefilling with { reliably kills the leading-prose and code-fence symptoms named in the logs, which is why it is tempting here. But it only biases the start of the output; the remainder is still unconstrained, so truncation, a dropped required field, or a wrong type can still produce a record the accounting system rejects.

questions.explanation: C is best: a strict-mode tool whose input_schema is the target shape, forced via tool_choice, constrains Claude's arguments to that schema, so the object read from the tool_use block already has every required field with correct types and no prose to parse — directly satisfying the accounting system's conformance check. A improves the odds with exemplars but leaves output free-form, so missing-field and wrong-type cases survive. B strips and retries on whatever text is emitted; brace-slicing breaks on nested or truncated objects and a parse that succeeds does not guarantee the required fields or types. D's prefill removes the prose/fence symptom but leaves the body unconstrained, so truncation or a wrong type still yields a rejected record. C is the only option that guarantees shape conformance, not just parseability.

questions.topic: Forcing tool use to guarantee the schema
questions.text: Your invoice-extraction pipeline calls the Messages API with a single tool, emit_invoice, whose input_schema defines the target JSON record. Batch logs show that for roughly 9% of documents Claude replies with a free-text summary of the invoice instead of emitting a tool_use block, so those records never reach the accounting system. You want every document to come back as a record that conforms to emit_invoice. Which change does that?

options.label: A
options.text: Mark every field in emit_invoice's input_schema as required and tighten its type constraints so the API rejects any output that does not match the record shape.
options.explanation: Tempting because the symptom is non-conforming output, so a stricter schema reads like direct enforcement. But input_schema is only validated against the arguments when the tool is actually called; it has no effect when Claude answers in prose, so the 9% that skip the tool entirely are untouched.

options.label: B
options.text: Prefill the assistant turn with the opening brace of the JSON record so Claude continues by completing the structured output you defined.
options.explanation: Prefilling is a documented way to steer output shape, so seeding the JSON looks like it would force the record. But prefill shapes the assistant's text completion, not a tool_use block; the result is prose JSON that bypasses the tool pathway and is never validated against emit_invoice.

options.label: C
options.text: Validate each response for a populated record and, when one is missing, re-issue the request with tool_choice forcing emit_invoice before passing the result downstream.
options.explanation: Reactive recovery looks careful: it only forces the tool when the default response actually skipped it. But it still lets the first call return non-conforming output and adds a round trip; the conforming record is obtained only after detecting and repairing the miss, not on the original request.

options.label: D
options.text: Set the request's tool_choice to {"type": "tool", "name": "emit_invoice"} so the model returns that tool's call on every document.
options.explanation: Correct. Naming the tool in tool_choice makes Claude emit an emit_invoice tool_use block on the first call for every document, and its arguments are validated against the tool's input_schema, so the free-text path that dropped 9% of records is removed.

questions.explanation: The discriminating axis is whether the change forces a schema-conforming emit_invoice tool_use block on the first call. Only D does: setting tool_choice to {\"type\": \"tool\", \"name\": \"emit_invoice\"} compels the tool call on every document, with arguments validated against input_schema, eliminating the free-text misses. A tightens input_schema, but schema validation only fires when the tool is called, so prose responses still slip through. B prefills the JSON, which shapes the assistant's text rather than producing a validated tool_use block, leaving the structured output unenforced. C forces the tool only on retry after detecting a missing record, so it adds a round trip and still permits a non-conforming first response instead of guaranteeing one up front.

questions.topic: Handling schema validation failures / tool errors
questions.text: Batch telemetry shows that for 9% of documents, Claude's `emit_invoice` call returns arguments that fail your post-call JSON Schema validation — `amount` as a localized string (`1.250,00`, or a bare `1.250` that is 1250 in one source PDF's locale but 1.25 in another's) and `due_date` in assorted non-ISO layouts. The PDFs come from thousands of vendors, so the set of locales, currency conventions, and date formats keeps drifting. The source PDF whose locale settles each value was read by Claude earlier in that same turn, but is not available to your validation code. You want the 9% recovered with the correct values, without a manual queue and without loosening the strict schema. Which change recovers the most of them correctly?

options.label: A
options.text: On a validation failure, append a `tool_result` with `is_error: true` to the same conversation turn naming the field and constraint that failed, and let Claude reissue `emit_invoice`.
options.explanation: Correct. Reporting the error inside the same turn means the source PDF Claude read earlier is still in context, so it can re-consult that document's locale and currency cues to decide whether a bare `1.250` is 1250 or 1.25, then re-emit in the schema's form. The disambiguation depends on the original document, which only the in-turn model still has; the schema stays the authoritative gate and no queue or parser is added.

options.label: B
options.text: On a validation failure, send the rejected arguments plus the JSON Schema to a dedicated reformatting call whose only job is to return values that satisfy the schema, then write that corrected record.
options.explanation: Incorrect, though tempting because a small, single-purpose correction step is cleaner and cheaper than re-running the whole extraction. Its input is the flaw: this call sees only the malformed arguments, never the source PDF, so for a bare `1.250` it must guess a magnitude and can emit a schema-valid but wrong number. A keeps the document in context to re-derive the value rather than merely reshape the string.

options.label: C
options.text: Add a normalization step before validation that parses locale-aware decimals and the common date layouts into the schema's number and ISO-8601 forms, coercing the known malformations into valid records.
options.explanation: Incorrect, but a reasonable instinct: deterministic normalization is fast, free, and needs no model round-trip. It loses here because the parser sees only the string, so it must assume one locale and will read a `1.250` that is really 1.25 as 1250 — a silent, confident mis-coercion — and the drifting vendor set keeps producing conventions it has not coded for. A resolves each value against the document that defines its locale.

options.label: D
options.text: Add few-shot examples to the system prompt showing `amount` as a bare number and `due_date` as ISO-8601, drawn from the localized and ambiguous cases you currently see fail.
options.explanation: Incorrect. Example-driven instructions will lower the 9% — a real, worthwhile improvement — but it is preventive, not corrective: it offers no path for the residual records that still slip through at batch scale, which is exactly the population the scenario asks you to recover. A handles those with a concrete second attempt while the document is still in view.

questions.explanation: A is best: returning the validation error inside the same turn keeps the source PDF in Claude's context, so it can re-read that document's locale and currency cues to recover the true value — deciding whether a bare `1.250` is 1250 or 1.25 — and re-emit `emit_invoice` in the schema's required form. That disambiguation is impossible from the emitted string alone, which is why both string-only fixes lose: B's dedicated reformatting call and C's pre-validation normalizer each see only the malformed arguments, never the document, so they can produce schema-valid numbers with the wrong magnitude, and C is further undercut by the drifting, open-ended set of vendor conventions. D's few-shot prompting reduces the failure rate but is preventive only and leaves the residual 9% with no correction path. All four preserve the strict schema; A wins because it is the only option that re-derives each failing value from the original document instead of reshaping a string whose meaning that document defines.

questions.topic: CLAUDE.md for the pipeline repo conventions
questions.text: A code-review report on the invoice-extraction repo shows that across the last 20 PRs, Claude Code keeps re-deriving the same repo conventions from scratch each session: it puts the currency-normalization helper in the wrong module, forgets that low-confidence fields must be emitted as null in the emit_invoice payload, and re-discovers where the JSON schema lives. PRs touch every part of the repo, not just the extraction module, and each one wastes turns relearning rules that never change. What is the most effective way to stop this recurring waste?

options.label: A
options.text: Put the conventions in the user-level ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md so they load automatically whenever this developer starts a session.
options.explanation: This is a genuine auto-loaded surface, which is why it tempts: the rules would be present every session without anyone invoking anything. But ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md is tied to one developer's machine and is not committed, so other contributors and CI never see it and the repo's own conventions live outside the repo.

options.label: B
options.text: Record the helper location, the null-on-low-confidence rule, and the schema path in the CLAUDE.md at the repository root.
options.explanation: The root CLAUDE.md is read into context at the start of every session in this repo regardless of which files a PR touches, and it is committed with the code, so all three durable conventions are present up front for every contributor instead of being rediscovered.

options.label: C
options.text: Place a CLAUDE.md inside the extraction module directory, next to the helper and emit_invoice code the rules govern, so the guidance sits with the code it describes.
options.explanation: Co-locating guidance with the code it governs is a reasonable instinct, but a CLAUDE.md in a subdirectory is pulled in based on what the session is working under. Since these PRs span the whole repo, that file is frequently out of scope exactly when the conventions are needed.

options.label: D
options.text: Document the null-on-low-confidence requirement directly in the emit_invoice tool description so it is in context whenever Claude prepares that payload.
options.explanation: Putting the rule on the tool that enforces it is natural and it would surface at the right moment for emission. But it only addresses one of the three recurring problems; the helper's module and the schema location still get relearned, so the waste largely continues.

questions.explanation: B is best: the repository-root CLAUDE.md is loaded into context at the start of every session in the repo no matter which files a PR touches, and it is version-controlled with the code, so all three stable conventions (helper module, the null-on-low-confidence emission rule, schema path) are known up front for every contributor. A uses an auto-loaded file but the wrong scope: ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md is personal and uncommitted, so teammates and CI never inherit it. C co-locates the guidance but a subdirectory CLAUDE.md is scoped to work under that path, and these PRs span the whole repo, so it is often out of scope when the rules are needed. D documents only the emission rule on emit_invoice, leaving the helper location and schema path to be relearned, so most of the waste remains.

questions.topic: Slash command to scaffold a new extractor
questions.text: A retro on the invoice-extraction pipeline finds engineers hand-copied the same boilerplate for a new `emit_invoice`-style extractor 14 times last quarter, with drifting results. The lead wants any engineer who clones the repo to be able to type `/new-extractor remittance-email` inside Claude Code and get an identically scaffolded extractor, where the argument names the new source. Which approach makes that invocation work for the whole team?

options.label: A
options.text: Put the full scaffold procedure in the repo's `CLAUDE.md` under a "New extractor" heading, and have engineers ask Claude to scaffold the extractor for a given source.
options.explanation: A committed `CLAUDE.md` is shared with every clone and is the right home for durable project conventions, so this is a reasonable instinct. But it only makes the guidance ambient context; it does not create an invocable `/new-extractor` command. Engineers would still drive it through free-form requests rather than the single deterministic invocation the lead specified, so the drift the retro flagged can return.

options.label: B
options.text: Add a committed skill at `.claude/skills/new-extractor/SKILL.md` whose description tells Claude to generate a new extractor when an engineer asks to scaffold one.
options.explanation: Skills live in the repo and are shared on clone, so packaging the scaffold as a skill looks like a clean fit. The mismatch is the invocation model: a skill is surfaced by Claude when the request matches its description, not run by typing a `/`-prefixed name with a positional argument. It cannot give the team the explicit `/new-extractor remittance-email` entry point the lead wants.

options.label: C
options.text: Add a `.claude/commands/new-extractor.md` file with the scaffold prompt that references `$ARGUMENTS`, and commit it to the repo.
options.explanation: Correct. A Markdown file under the project's `.claude/commands/` becomes a slash command named after the file, and `$ARGUMENTS` receives the text typed after it -- so `/new-extractor remittance-email` runs that prompt with `remittance-email` substituted in. Committing it ships the command to everyone who clones the repo.

options.label: D
options.text: Create the command at `~/.claude/commands/new-extractor.md` and document in the README that each engineer should add the same file after cloning.
options.explanation: This produces a working `/new-extractor` command and is the fastest path for the engineer who writes it. The problem is scope: `~/.claude/commands/` is per-user and outside the repo, so the team only gets it by each person manually replicating the file -- exactly the copy-by-hand drift the retro is trying to eliminate.

questions.explanation: C is correct: a project-scoped slash command is a Markdown file in `.claude/commands/`; the filename becomes the command name and `$ARGUMENTS` carries the typed argument, so committing `new-extractor.md` gives every cloner a working `/new-extractor remittance-email`. A puts the procedure in a committed `CLAUDE.md`, which is shared but only ambient guidance -- it never creates the `/`-invocable command, so engineers fall back to free-form requests. B packages it as a committed skill, but skills are surfaced by description matching, not run by typing a slash name with a positional argument, so they can't provide the explicit `/new-extractor` entry point. D does build a real `/new-extractor` command, but at user scope (`~/.claude/commands/`) that each teammate must hand-replicate, which recreates the manual drift the team wants to remove.

questions.topic: CI test of extraction accuracy on a fixture set
questions.text: The nightly batch report shows the `emit_invoice` tool got `due_date` right on 88% of documents, while `invoice_id` and `amount` each landed around 99%. Accounting rejects a batch outright if any field they consume comes in under their 95% bar, so you want a CI test in the Claude Code repo to gate merges before the next regression ships. You have a versioned fixture set of documents, each paired with a hand-labeled, known-correct JSON record. How should the CI test be structured?

options.label: A
options.text: Run the pipeline on the fixtures, compute each field's accuracy against the labeled records, then average those per-field rates into one score and fail CI when that score is below 95%.
options.explanation: A single averaged score lets the strong fields carry the weak one: with `invoice_id` and `amount` near 99% and `due_date` at 88%, the mean lands around 95.3% and the gate passes green even though `due_date` is well under accounting's bar. This reproduces the very masking the batch report already exposes.

options.label: B
options.text: Run the pipeline on the fixtures and gate on `due_date` alone, comparing it field-by-field against the labels and failing CI when its accuracy is below 95%.
options.explanation: Focusing on the field that actually broke is a reasonable triage instinct, but accounting consumes every field, not just `due_date`. A future change that drags `invoice_id` or `amount` under 95% would clear this gate untouched, so it protects the last regression rather than the next one.

options.label: C
options.text: Run the pipeline and compute per-field accuracy against the labels, then fail CI only when any field's accuracy comes in lower than the previous CI run recorded for that field.
options.explanation: A per-field regression gate avoids hard-coding a threshold and catches sudden drops, which feels robust. But a field can sit steadily at 88% run after run without ever dropping relative to the prior run, so it never trips even though it lives permanently below accounting's absolute 95% bar.

options.label: D
options.text: Run the pipeline on the fixtures, compute each field's accuracy against the labels, and fail CI when the lowest of those per-field rates is below 95%.
options.explanation: Accounting's bar binds on whichever field is weakest, so the gate must too: take each field's deterministic accuracy against its labels and fail on the minimum. Here `due_date` at 88% is the floor, so this catches today's breach and any single field that slips under 95% later.

questions.explanation: All four options deterministically compare emitted fields against the hand-labeled records, so the decision turns on how per-field accuracy is aggregated and scoped. D is correct: accounting rejects a batch if any consumed field is under 95%, so the gate must bind on the worst field — failing when the minimum per-field accuracy drops below 95%. A averages the per-field rates, which lets `invoice_id` and `amount` (~99%) lift the mean to roughly 95.3% and pass the gate while `due_date` sits at 88% — the exact masking the report reveals. B gates on `due_date` only, which protects the field that already broke but lets a later regression in `invoice_id` or `amount` slip through unchecked. C gates on per-field regression versus the prior run, so a field stuck at 88% never trips because it never drops relative to itself, even though it stays under the absolute 95% bar.

questions.text: A field-level accuracy report on the invoice extraction pipeline shows emit_invoice is filled out correctly on clean documents but misses on ambiguous ones. The system prompt already spells out detailed disambiguation guidance for these fields (how to infer currency when an amount like "1,250" carries no symbol, and to leave due_date null when only relative terms like "net 30 from receipt" appear). Across the day's invoices the model honors that guidance on some documents and disregards it on others, emitting a wrong currency or an invented date on a sizable slice of the ambiguous inputs. The schema validates every output, so accounting never sees malformed JSON. What is the most effective change to raise accuracy on these fields?

options.label: A
options.text: Add 3-4 worked examples to the prompt that resolve representative ambiguous documents the desired way.
options.explanation: Correct. The detailed written guidance is already present and the model applies it unevenly across documents, which is exactly the gap concrete examples close: demonstrated resolutions give the model a pattern to imitate where prose rules it can already read are not reliably moving its behavior.

options.label: B
options.text: Rewrite the disambiguation section of the system prompt to be more explicit and prescriptive about currency inference and relative-date handling.
options.explanation: Incorrect, though tempting because sharper instructions usually mean more reliable behavior. The report states detailed guidance for these very cases is already in the prompt and is being followed inconsistently, so adding more of the same lever repeats what is already underperforming rather than changing how reliably the model acts on it.

options.label: C
options.text: Lower the sampling temperature on the extraction call so the model commits to its single most-likely interpretation on each field.
options.explanation: Incorrect, and attractive because uneven outputs read like sampling noise. The ask is higher accuracy, not lower variance: reducing temperature makes the model emit its top choice more consistently, which locks in the same incorrect currency or invented date on ambiguous inputs instead of teaching it the right call.

options.label: D
options.text: Constrain the emit_invoice schema with a currency enum and stricter field types so only valid values can be written.
options.explanation: Incorrect. A smart engineer reaches for it because an enum forces clean currency values, but the schema already guarantees valid, well-formed JSON. Restricting the allowed set cannot tell the model which currency is correct for a symbol-less amount, and it does nothing for the relative-date failures at all.

questions.explanation: A is correct: detailed disambiguation prose is already in the prompt and the model honors it on some documents while disregarding it on others, and worked examples are what most reliably convert guidance the model can already read into behavior it actually imitates. B sharpens that same prose, repeating the lever the scenario shows is being applied inconsistently. C lowers temperature, which reduces variance but not error — it makes the model commit harder to its existing wrong judgment rather than correcting it, and the goal here is accuracy. D adds a currency enum and stricter types, but the schema already enforces valid JSON; constraining the value set cannot choose the right currency for a symbol-less amount and ignores the relative-date failures entirely.

questions.topic: Missing fields: null vs omit via schema/prompt
questions.text: Reviewing a day of output from the extraction pipeline, an accounting engineer notes that when a remittance email carries no due date, the emit_invoice tool call sometimes returns "due_date": null, sometimes omits the due_date key, and occasionally returns "due_date": "unknown". The downstream ingester breaks on the missing key and on the non-date string. The team wants to fix this in the emit_invoice input schema. Which schema change yields one consistent representation of a missing due date?

options.label: A
options.text: Keep due_date in required and constrain it to {"type":"string","format":"date"}, so the field carries a validated ISO date and malformed values are caught at the boundary.
options.explanation: The strictest contract is attractive because it forces a real, parseable date and rejects junk. But it leaves no valid representation for a legitimately absent date: the model cannot omit the key (it is required) and cannot send null (the type is string only), so on a dateless email it is pushed to fabricate a date or re-emit a non-date string—keeping the breakage alive.

options.label: B
options.text: Keep due_date in required and set {"type":["string","null"],"format":"date"}, so a present date is validated and an absent one is recorded as null.
options.explanation: Requiring the key closes the omit path, and allowing null gives a missing date one sanctioned in-field home. With a place to put absence, the model stops inventing a string for "no date," so the three observed modes collapse onto a single value the ingester reads directly—no extra field, no token to special-case.

options.label: C
options.text: Drop due_date from required and type it {"type":["string","null"],"format":"date"}, letting the model omit the key or send null when no date is found.
options.explanation: Optional-plus-nullable is the idiomatic way to model a field that may be absent, and it reads as clean. But it sanctions two representations of absence at once—an omitted key and an explicit null—so the inconsistency persists, and the ingester continues to break on the missing-key case it already fails on.

options.label: D
options.text: Keep due_date in required and define it as {"oneOf":[{"type":"string","format":"date"},{"const":"none"}]}, making the value either a valid date or the literal token none.
options.explanation: A single explicit token for the empty case guarantees a present value and avoids nulls a strict parser might mishandle. But it puts a non-date string back into the date field, which is exactly what the ingester breaks on, so date parsing must carve out a special case for the token instead of reading a clean typed absence.

questions.explanation: B is correct: keeping due_date required closes the key-omission path, and typing it [\"string\",\"null\"] gives a missing date one schema-sanctioned in-field value (null). Because absence now has a home, the model stops inventing a string for it, and the three observed modes collapse to one value the ingester can read—without adding a field or a token. A is the strictest contract but leaves no valid way to express a genuinely absent date (the key cannot be omitted and null is not allowed), forcing a fabricated date or a non-date string. C models absence with both an omitted key and an explicit null, sanctioning two representations and leaving the missing-key break in place. D guarantees a present value but encodes absence as the non-date token \"none\", reintroducing the very non-date string the date ingester fails on. B alone gives absence a typed, in-field home while keeping the field present.

questions.topic: Prefilling / output-format control for strict JSON
questions.text: In the invoice-extraction pipeline, your parser logs show that about 8% of Messages API responses fail to deserialize because Claude prepends conversational preamble such as "Here is the extracted record:" before the JSON object. You want the smallest first-step change that stops the preamble from appearing at the start of the text response. Which change should you make?

options.label: A
options.text: Tighten the system prompt with a direct instruction that the response must be a single JSON object with no surrounding text or commentary.
options.explanation: A sharper instruction usually shifts behavior in the right direction and is the cheapest thing to try, which is why it is tempting. But it still leaves the leading characters up to the model's sampled completion, so a fraction of responses keep emitting the intro phrase; instruction wording cannot pin the first token of the output.

options.label: B
options.text: Set response_format to "json" on the Messages API request so the endpoint returns only the JSON portion of the output.
options.explanation: Engineers coming from other providers reach for a response_format flag because that pattern exists elsewhere, so it feels like the obvious switch. The Anthropic Messages API exposes no such field for text responses, so it would be ignored or rejected and the preamble would persist.

options.label: C
options.text: Prefill the assistant turn with an opening "{" character on the request.
options.explanation: Correct. Supplying the start of the assistant message makes generation continue from that point, so the model's first emitted tokens extend the JSON object instead of opening with prose. The "{" you provide is prepended on your side; the response continues the object, which removes the preamble at its source with a one-line change.

options.label: D
options.text: Add a post-processing step that discards everything before the first "{" and parses the remainder as JSON.
options.explanation: Salvaging the payload after the fact is a common production patch and would recover many of the failing responses, which makes it look like a quick win. It is brittle, though: a "{" inside a quoted string value or a markdown code fence shifts where the slice starts, so it patches the symptom downstream rather than stopping the preamble where it is generated.

questions.explanation: C is the smallest change that stops the preamble at the point it is generated: prefilling the assistant turn with "{" makes the model continue the JSON object from that character rather than open with prose. A is a reasonable first instinct but leaves the opening tokens to probabilistic sampling, so some responses still leak the intro. B reaches for a response_format flag that the Messages API does not provide for text output, so it has no effect. D recovers many payloads by slicing before the first "{", but it is a brittle downstream patch that breaks when a "{" appears inside a string value or code fence, and it does not stop the preamble from being produced.

questions.topic: Long-document position effects (lost in the middle)
questions.text: A nightly accuracy report on the invoice pipeline breaks field-level correctness down by where each value sits in the source document. For short remittance emails all fields score above 97%, but on the long multi-page scanned PDFs a pattern appears: vendor and total_amount (which sit in the header and footer) hold at ~96%, while line_items and due_date (which appear on the dense middle pages) drop to 78-81%. The emit_invoice schema, the model, and the few-shot examples are identical across both document types. Which change most directly closes the accuracy gap on the long PDFs?

options.label: A
options.text: Reorder the assembled document so the dense middle pages are moved to the very start and end of the context, placing line_items and due_date at the edges where extraction is already strong.
options.explanation: A competent engineer would reason that since header and footer fields score ~96% while middle fields lag, simply relocating the middle pages to those high-performing edge positions should lift them. But the edge advantage is zero-sum within a single context: moving line_items and due_date to the boundaries pushes vendor, total_amount, and other content into the newly-formed middle, so the gap moves to whichever fields now sit there rather than closing overall.

options.label: B
options.text: Add a per-field checklist to the system prompt that enumerates line_items and due_date and requires Claude to confirm it has located each one on the middle pages before calling emit_invoice.
options.explanation: A structured per-field checklist is a standard way to make a model more thorough, so requiring explicit confirmation for the weak fields looks like a targeted fix. The problem is that the under-attention to middle pages is a property of how the context is read, not a step the model forgets to perform; the checklist adds an instruction but does not force a fresh, located read of those pages, so the 78-81% fields are confirmed from the same shallow pass.

options.label: C
options.text: Move the long PDFs onto a model with a larger context window and stronger long-context benchmark scores, keeping the schema and examples unchanged.
options.explanation: Long, dense inputs intuitively call for more context capacity, so upgrading the model seems like the direct lever. But the same model already scores 97% on the short emails, which shows the inputs fit the window and the model is capable; the degradation tracks field position within the document, so a higher-capacity model carries the same relative position effect and the middle pages stay weaker.

options.label: D
options.text: Have the model first extract a verbatim source quote for each target field from the document, then populate emit_invoice from those quotes.
options.explanation: Requiring an explicit quote for each field forces the model to locate and read the source text for line_items and due_date on the middle pages before committing a value, instead of emitting them from a single shallow pass. Anchoring each value to located text is what lifts the under-attended middle fields, which is exactly where the gap is.

questions.explanation: The report isolates the failure to fields physically located on the middle pages of long PDFs while edge fields (vendor, total_amount) hold steady, the signature of a position effect: the center of a long context is read less reliably than its start or end. D closes the gap because requiring a verbatim quote per field forces the model to locate and read each value's source text on the middle pages before emission, lifting the specific under-attended fields. A relocates the middle pages to the strong edges but the edge advantage is zero-sum, so the gap simply shifts to whatever content now occupies the center. B adds a per-field checklist, but an instruction does not change how thoroughly the middle is read, so those fields are confirmed from the same shallow pass. C upgrades the model, but the same model already scores 97% on the short emails, showing capacity is not the limit; a larger-context model carries the same relative position effect. Only D changes how the middle of the document is actually read.

questions.text: An invoice-extraction pipeline calls the Messages API with the `emit_invoice` tool, running about 40,000 documents through Claude Opus 4.8 each night; downstream accounting reads the results the next morning and bounces any run with extraction errors back for manual rework. Ops reports the nightly run keeps tripping the account's requests-per-minute limit, and finance flags that monthly token spend has roughly doubled as volume has grown. The records are never needed in real time. Which change most directly cuts the spend and clears the throughput ceiling?

options.label: A
options.text: Resubmit the nightly documents through the `/v1/messages/batches` endpoint instead of synchronous `messages.create()` calls.
options.explanation: Correct. The Message Batches endpoint processes the same requests on the same model asynchronously and bills all token usage at half the standard rate, so the doubled spend is cut without any change to extraction quality that would trigger accounting's rework. Its rate limits are separate from the synchronous Messages API, so the 40,000-document run no longer competes for the per-minute ceiling, and the morning deadline comfortably absorbs the asynchronous completion window.

options.label: B
options.text: Add `cache_control` breakpoints to the system prompt and the `emit_invoice` schema so the shared prefix is served from cache on every call.
options.explanation: Incorrect, though tempting: across 40,000 near-identical calls the stable prefix is large, and caching is the standard first lever for repeated-context cost. But it discounts only that prefix — each invoice's content and the emitted JSON still bill in full — so it makes a dent in spend rather than halving it, and it leaves every request on the synchronous path still contending for the same per-minute limit.

options.label: C
options.text: Request a usage-tier upgrade from Anthropic to raise the account's requests-per-minute and tokens-per-minute limits.
options.explanation: Incorrect. Raising the limits is the direct answer to the rate-limit trips and lets the nightly run push through without throttling, which is why it looks right. But a higher ceiling changes only how fast requests are accepted, not what they cost — every request still bills at the standard per-token rate, so the doubled spend finance flagged is untouched.

options.label: D
options.text: Switch the extraction calls from Claude Opus 4.8 to Claude Haiku 4.5 to lower per-token cost and shorten each response.
options.explanation: Incorrect. A cheaper, faster model looks like it hits both axes at once — lower token prices and quicker turnaround — which is why it is the intuitive pick. But this is the workload where accounting rejects runs with extraction errors and sends them back for manual rework, and dropping to a smaller model risks exactly that accuracy regression; the cheaper run that has to be redone by hand is a false economy here.

questions.explanation: A is correct: moving the nightly job to the Message Batches endpoint keeps the same model (so extraction accuracy is unchanged and accounting's rework gate is not tripped), bills every token at half price (cutting the doubled spend), and runs on rate limits separate from the synchronous Messages API (clearing the per-minute ceiling) — all within an asynchronous window the morning deadline tolerates. B (prompt caching) discounts only the repeated prefix and stays on the synchronous path, so it trims spend partially and does nothing for the rate limit. C (a usage-tier upgrade) raises the throughput ceiling but bills at the same per-token rate, leaving the cost problem intact. D (downshifting to a smaller model) appears to cut cost and latency together but risks the extraction-accuracy regression this rework-sensitive workload can't absorb, so it solves neither axis cleanly.
